\section{Elementary Particles and Field Excitations in ECT}
\label{sec:particles}

\textit{Status guide for this chapter.}
The material below spans three levels of rigour, organised into
three categories of excitations
(Section~\ref{sec:particle_structure}):
\begin{description}[leftmargin=3em,style=unboxed,noitemsep]
  \item[(i) Required condensate excitations (Level~A/B).]
    Graviton: linearised tensorial sector of the effective metric
    (A/B; Section~\ref{sec:graviton}).
    Soft orientation mode $\chi$ (Level~B;
    Section~\ref{sec:particle_soft_orientation}).
    Primary radial mode $\sigma$ (Level~A/B;
    Section~\ref{sec:radial_mode}).
    These arise directly from the condensate action and its
    broken-phase structure.
  \item[(ii) Structurally embedded SM fields (Level~B/C).]
    Gauge bosons from condensate phase symmetries
    (Section~\ref{sec:gauge_particles}; full development in
    Section~\ref{sec:gauge}).
    Higgs boson reinterpreted as secondary radial mode
    (Section~\ref{sec:ew_particles}).
    Fermions: structural reconstruction on the ordered branch
    (Section~\ref{sec:fermion_particles}; full development in
    Section~\ref{sec:fermions}).
    Neutrino sector: seesaw-type embedding
    (Section~\ref{sec:neutrino_particles}).
    These assume Standard-Model structure as external input;
    the SM gauge group, Yukawa couplings, and generation structure
    are not derived from the minimal bosonic condensate action.
  \item[(iii) Topological and conditional sectors (Level~A/B/C).]
    Texture/skyrmion-like states classified by
    $\pi_3(S^3) = \mathbb{Z}$; anti-predictions
    $\pi_0 = \pi_1 = \pi_2 = 0$; conditional secondary defects
    (Section~\ref{sec:predicted_states}).
\end{description}

\noindent
The only currently identified ECT-specific fermion interaction beyond
the Standard Model is the fifth-force coupling
$\beta_5\bar\Psi\gamma^A n_A\Psi$
(Section~\ref{sec:fifth_force_fermion}).
A condensed overview with status table is given in the chapter
summary~(Section~\ref{sec:particle_summary}).

\subsection{General Structure of Particle Excitations}
\label{sec:particle_structure}

In the scalar-only ECT basis the fundamental degree of freedom
is the condensate field $\Phi$.
The orientation field $n_A$ is introduced only as a derived
ordered-branch variable through $Q_A = \partial_A\Phi = u\,n_A$
and is not an independent microscopic field in the present
formulation~(Section~\ref{sec:sector_map}).
Elementary particles and field excitations arise as condensate
fluctuations, as fields associated with internal symmetries or
spinorial structures, or as topological configurations of the ordered
vacuum.

\paragraph{Three categories of excitations.}
The particle content discussed in this chapter falls into three
categories with different derivation status:

\emph{(i) Required condensate excitations.}
These follow from the structure of the ordered branch and the
$O(4)\to O(3)$ symmetry breaking:
\begin{itemize}
  \item primary radial mode $\sigma$, $m_\sigma \sim \bar{M}_{\rm Pl}$
    (amplitude fluctuation; Section~\ref{sec:radial_mode});
  \item surviving soft scalar $\chi$ from the integrability-constrained
    orientation sector
    (Section~\ref{sec:particle_soft_orientation});
  \item graviton as linearised effective-metric perturbation
    (Section~\ref{sec:graviton}).
\end{itemize}

\emph{(ii) Structurally embedded Standard Model fields.}
These are accommodated within the condensate framework but not derived
from P1--P5:
\begin{itemize}
  \item gauge bosons from condensate phase symmetries
    (Section~\ref{sec:gauge_particles});
  \item electroweak sector and the Higgs interpretation
    (Section~\ref{sec:ew_particles});
  \item fermions as spinorial excitations compatible with the ordered
    background (Section~\ref{sec:fermion_particles});
  \item neutrino sector (Section~\ref{sec:neutrino_particles}).
\end{itemize}

\emph{(iii) Topological sectors and other conditional states.}
These arise either from the homotopy of the vacuum manifold or from a
possible second transition:
\begin{itemize}
  \item texture/skyrmion sectors from $\pi_3(S^3) = \mathbb{Z}$
    and anti-predictions from $\pi_0 = \pi_1 = \pi_2 = 0$;
  \item (conditional) secondary defects from $\pi_2(S^2)$ if
    $O(3)\to O(2)$ exists;
  \item the Higgs-like secondary radial mode $h$ as a
    reinterpretation of the observed Higgs boson.
\end{itemize}
Topological sectors are developed in
Section~\ref{sec:predicted_states}, while the conditional Higgs-like
reinterpretation is discussed primarily in
Section~\ref{sec:ew_particles}.

\paragraph{Connection to the three-scale hierarchy.}
The particle mass scales are tied to the three condensate
scales~(Section~\ref{sec:three_scales}):
$m_\sigma \sim u_0 \sim \bar{M}_{\rm Pl}$ (first condensate),
$m_h \sim v_2 \approx 246\,$GeV (second condensate, if it exists),
and the surviving soft mode $\chi$ (massless at tree level in the
minimal broken-phase EFT).
The IR galactic scale $v_{\rm gal}$ is not associated with a
particle excitation but with the macroscopic $\phi$-sector
closure~(Section~\ref{sec:dark_sector}).

The purpose of the following subsections is therefore not only to list
possible excitations, but also to distinguish clearly between required
modes of the primary condensate, structurally embedded Standard Model
sectors, and conditional or topologically classified states.



\subsection{Soft Orientation Sector of the Condensate}
\label{sec:particle_soft_orientation}

\textit{Status: minimal orientation EFT is Level~B.
Three independent physical Goldstone particles are \textbf{not} claimed
in the present scalar-only basis; see Section~\ref{sec:goldstone_integrability_main}
and Appendix~\ref{app:goldstone_integrability}.}

The ordered branch breaks $O(4)\to O(3)$, so the vacuum manifold is
\begin{equation}
  O(4)/O(3)\simeq S^3.
\end{equation}
In a generic internal-symmetry breaking this would yield three
independent Goldstone bosons.
However, in the present scalar-only ECT basis the ordered quantity is
$Q_A=\partial_A\Phi$, which is an exact one-form.
The integrability constraint $\partial_A Q_B-\partial_B Q_A=0$ implies
that the orientation triplet reduces to one longitudinal soft scalar
mode~$\chi$ at linear order~\cite{Ivanov1975,McArthur2010}:
\begin{equation}
  \pi_i = \frac{1}{u_0}\partial_i\chi, \qquad \sigma = \partial_w\chi.
\end{equation}

\paragraph{Minimal orientation EFT.}
A three-component orientation parametrisation of $S^3$ remains a useful
effective language for the broken branch, but it should be understood
as a minimal EFT organisation, not as three independent propagating
particles.
The broken-generator counting $\dim O(4)-\dim O(3)=3$ is a
geometric statement; the actual independent propagating soft content
in the scalar-only basis at linear order is $N_G^{\rm phys}=1$.

\paragraph{Mass of the surviving soft mode.}
In the minimal broken-phase EFT there is no orientation potential, so
the surviving scalar mode $\chi$ is massless at tree level.
A pseudo-Goldstone mass could arise from explicit $O(4)$-breaking,
but no such mechanism is present in P1--P5~(open; OP-GM).

\paragraph{No dark-matter role.}
The macroscopic sector of ECT already explains galactic rotation curves,
the RAR, and BTFR through the $\phi$-sector without invoking any
Goldstone-like dark matter.
The soft orientation sector must \emph{not} be repurposed as a
dark-matter patch for phenomena already explained by the $\phi$-closure.


\paragraph{Role in the predicted-states classification.}
The surviving soft scalar $\chi$ is listed as item~(ii) in the
classification of new states beyond the observed SM spectrum
(Section~\ref{sec:predicted_states}).
Its mass, couplings, and possible cosmological abundance remain
open~(Level~B).

\paragraph{Status summary.}
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{p{6.0cm}p{2.0cm}p{5.0cm}}
\hline
Statement & Status & Comment \\
\hline
$O(4)\to O(3)$ breaking admits $S^3$ vacuum manifold
  & A & Geometric statement \\
Naive Goldstone counting: $\dim O(4) - \dim O(3) = 3$
  & A & Broken-generator counting \\
Integrability reduces propagating soft content to
  $N_G^{\rm phys} = 1$
  & B & Integrability of $Q_A=\partial_A\Phi$ in the scalar-only basis;
    Appendix~\ref{app:goldstone_integrability} \\
Surviving mode $\chi$ massless at tree level
  & B & No orientation potential in P1--P5 \\
Pseudo-Goldstone mass mechanism
  & Open (OP-GM) & No explicit $O(4)$-breaking in P1--P5 \\
Dark-matter role of $\chi$
  & Not claimed & Galactic phenomenology uses $\phi$-sector,
    not $\chi$ \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsection{Radial Condensate Mode}
\label{sec:radial_mode}

\textit{Status: layered.
The primary radial mode of the first condensate is required at Level~A
as the amplitude fluctuation of the ordered branch.
Its Planckian scale follows at Level~B from the matching
$u_0\sim\bar{M}_{\rm Pl}$.
A secondary Higgs-like radial mode is structurally admissible only if an
electroweak-like second condensate transition exists; this remains
Level~B/C and does not by itself establish the full Standard Model Higgs
sector.}

\paragraph{First radial mode: amplitude fluctuation of the ordered branch.}
In the scalar-only ECT basis the fundamental degree of freedom is the
condensate field $\Phi$; the ordered background is characterised by
$Q_A=\partial_A\Phi = u_0\,n_A$, where $n_A$ is the derived orientation
variable~(Section~\ref{sec:sector_map}).
Amplitude fluctuations of $Q_A$ --- i.e.\ fluctuations of
$u_0=|\partial\Phi|$ --- define the radial mode~$\sigma$:
\begin{equation}
  u = u_0 + \sigma, \qquad
  m_\sigma^2 = V''(u_0) \sim 2\lambda\,u_0^2.
  \label{eq:sigma_mass_radial}
\end{equation}
For a generic quartic potential $V = \frac{\lambda}{4}(|\partial\Phi|^2 - u_0^2)^2$
and with the gravitational matching $u_0\sim\bar{M}_{\rm Pl}$
(Section~\ref{sec:three_scales}):
\begin{equation}
  m_\sigma \sim \sqrt{2\lambda}\,\bar{M}_{\rm Pl} \sim \bar{M}_{\rm Pl}
  \qquad\text{(Level~B: consistency-scale identification).}
  \label{eq:sigma_planck}
\end{equation}
This mode is Planck-heavy and integrates out at $E\ll m_\sigma$.
It therefore provides the natural heavy scale controlling the validity
range of the broken-phase EFT.
It is \emph{not} the Standard Model Higgs boson.

\textbf{Spin and propagation.}
The mode $\sigma$ is a Lorentz scalar (spin-0 in the emergent Lorentzian
description): it is the amplitude fluctuation of the scalar condensate
$\Phi$ and carries no orientation or gauge indices.
In the ordered background it propagates with an effective mass $m_\sigma$
and sees the same emergent Lorentzian metric as other EFT modes.

\paragraph{Why the primary radial mode is not the soft orientation scalar.}
The primary radial mode $\sigma$ must also be distinguished from the
surviving soft scalar $\chi$ of the orientation sector
(Section~\ref{sec:goldstone_integrability_main}).
This distinction is ECT-specific and follows from the integrability of
the gradient condensate: $Q_A=\partial_A\Phi$ is an exact one-form,
so amplitude and orientation fluctuations are not independent generic fields.
At linear order, $\sigma$ describes fluctuations of the magnitude
$|Q_A|=|\partial_A\Phi|$,
whereas $\chi$ parameterises the surviving longitudinal soft variation
of the orientation sector.
Thus $\sigma$ and $\chi$ are different scalar excitations with different
physical roles: $\sigma$ is heavy and radial, while $\chi$ is soft and
branch-sensitive.

\paragraph{Higher-dimension operators after integrating out $\sigma$.}
Integrating out the heavy primary radial mode does not merely remove it
from the low-energy spectrum.
It also generates higher-dimension operators suppressed by powers of
$m_\sigma^{-2}$, schematically of the form
\begin{equation}
  \Delta\mathcal{L}_{\rm EFT}
  \sim \sum_{n\ge 1}\frac{\mathcal{O}_{2n+4}}{m_\sigma^{2n}}.
  \label{eq:HDO_sigma}
\end{equation}
This is the standard EFT consequence of decoupling.
In ECT it explains why the broken-phase low-energy theory remains
reliable at $E\ll m_\sigma$, while still retaining calculable
suppressed corrections from the heavy radial sector~(Level~B).


\paragraph{Connection to the pre-quantum action scale.}
The primary radial mass $m_\sigma$ is closely related to the
physical scale that enters the distinguished EFT action scale of the
coherent-topological branch~(Section~\ref{sec:hbar_status}):
\begin{equation}
  S_0^{\rm EFT} = \frac{K_\theta\,m_\varphi}{2}.
  \label{eq:S0_sigma_bridge}
\end{equation}
In the minimal single-condensate realisation, the radial core scale
$m_\varphi$ entering $S_0^{\rm EFT}$ is naturally identified with the
primary radial mass $m_\sigma$.
This is a nontrivial structural link: the same condensate scale that
controls the EFT breakdown threshold is also tied to the pre-quantum
action scale later matched to $\hbar$.
In other words, the quantum sector and the UV structure of the
geometric branch share a common mass scale.
This connection is a Level~B consistency statement, not a
parameter-free prediction, because both $S_0^{\rm EFT}$ and $m_\sigma$
depend on the condensate parameters $(u_0, \lambda)$ in the present
notation, which are fixed by matching.

\paragraph{The primary radial mode as physical UV threshold.}
In standard quantum field theory, the UV cutoff $\Lambda$ is
typically introduced as a formal regulator without intrinsic physical
meaning.
In ECT the physical EFT breakdown scale is set by the \emph{mass}
of the primary radial mode $m_\sigma$.
The broken-phase EFT is valid for $E \ll m_\sigma$; at
$E \sim m_\sigma$ the radial mode becomes dynamical and the
effective description must be replaced by the full condensate theory.
This is the standard EFT picture, but with a concrete physical
interpretation: the scale at which the EFT breaks down is the
scale at which the condensate amplitude itself begins to fluctuate.
The Euclidean propagator of the radial mode
(Appendix~\ref{app:corr_length}) decays asymptotically as
\begin{equation}
  G_\sigma(r) \;\propto\; \frac{e^{-m_\sigma r}}{r^{3/2}}
  \qquad (r\to\infty),
  \label{eq:G_sigma_decay}
\end{equation}
confirming that amplitude correlations decay on the scale
$\xi_{\rm cond}\sim m_\sigma^{-1}$.
Beyond this distance the ordered branch is effectively stiff against
amplitude fluctuations, and the low-energy EFT is well-defined
to that approximation~(Level~B).

\paragraph{Second radial mode: candidate from the electroweak-like transition.}
If the second condensate transition $O(3)\to O(2)$ is realised at
scale $v_2$ (Appendix~\ref{app:second_transition}, assumptions A1--A2),
the effective broken-phase description at that scale admits a radial
fluctuation around the second condensate vacuum.
In an effective field theory language (\emph{not} a second independent
fundamental field, but an effective condensate sector at scale $v_2$):
\begin{equation}
  \phi_{\rm ew} = v_2 + h, \qquad
  m_h^2 = V_{\rm ew}''(v_2) = 2\lambda_2\,v_2^2,
  \label{eq:higgs_mass_candidate}
\end{equation}
where $\lambda_2$ is the quartic coupling of the second-condensate
effective sector at scale~$v_2$.

This mode is \emph{structurally analogous} to the Standard Model
Higgs boson in the following sense:
\begin{itemize}
  \item it is the radial excitation of the second-condensate VEV
    (formally analogous to the Higgs field);
  \item the spontaneous breaking of the internal symmetry at $v_2$
    would generate masses for the vector bosons of the embedded
    electroweak-like gauge sector, if a local gauge realisation exists
    (formally analogous to the Higgs mechanism);
  \item the relation $m_h = \sqrt{2\lambda_2}\,v_2$ has the same
    structure as the SM Higgs mass formula.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Honest status of the Higgs-like mode.}
Three levels must be distinguished:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Level B/C (structural):} if the second transition
    exists and if the corresponding $O(3)$-sector admits a local
    gauge realisation (not implied by P1--P5; part of OP-EW),
    a radial mode $h$ at scale $v_2\approx246$\,GeV is structurally
    present.
  \item \textbf{Matching constraint:} the SM Higgs mass
    $m_H\approx125.1$\,GeV~\cite{ATLAS2012Higgs,CMS2012Higgs}
    would require $\lambda_2(v_2)\approx0.13$.
    This value is not derived from ECT postulates P1--P5; it is a
    matching input if one demands consistency with the measured Higgs mass.
  \item \textbf{Open (OP-EW):} the dynamical mechanism producing
    $v_2$, the origin of $\lambda_2$, and the localisation of the
    $O(3)$ symmetry at scale $v_2$ are not derived from the minimal
    scalar condensate and remain open problems.
\end{enumerate}

\noindent\textbf{Relation to the three-scale hierarchy.}
The two radial modes define the two UV condensate scales:
$m_{\sigma}\sim\bar{M}_{\rm Pl}$ (first condensate) and
$m_h\sim v_2$ (second condensate, if it exists).
The IR galactic scale $v_{\rm gal}$ is not a UV condensate VEV
and has no associated radial mode~(Section~\ref{sec:three_scales}).


\paragraph{Higgs self-coupling and testability.}
If the secondary condensate potential is quartic near $v_2$,
\begin{equation}
  V_{\rm ew}(\phi_{\rm ew})
  =
  \frac{\lambda_2}{4}\left(\phi_{\rm ew}^2-v_2^2\right)^2,
\end{equation}
then the Higgs mass satisfies
\begin{equation}
  m_h^2 = 2\lambda_2\,v_2^2,
  \qquad
  \lambda_2 = \frac{m_h^2}{2v_2^2}.
\end{equation}
The corresponding cubic self-interaction is
\begin{equation}
  V_{\rm ew} \supset \lambda_2\,v_2\,h^3,
\end{equation}
or equivalently
\begin{equation}
  V_{\rm ew}'''(v_2) = 6\lambda_2\,v_2 = \frac{3m_h^2}{v_2}.
\end{equation}
Different collider conventions for the trilinear Higgs coupling differ
by combinatorial factors; the expression above refers to the third
derivative of the effective potential at the minimum.
Deviations from this quartic relation would signal non-quartic
corrections to the effective potential at scale $v_2$---for instance,
induced by the primary condensate sector or by the dynamics of the
second transition itself.
Such deviations are in principle testable through Higgs pair production
and other future precision-Higgs measurements.
At present ECT does not predict their magnitude; this remains a
Level~C phenomenological possibility.

\paragraph{Possible mixing with the primary radial mode.}
If the Higgs-like excitation $h$ and the primary radial mode $\sigma$
belong to different ordered phases of the same underlying medium, a
mixing term between them is generically allowed unless forbidden by an
additional symmetry.
After diagonalisation, such mixing would generically induce small
universal deviations in the Higgs couplings to Standard Model fields.
In the decoupling regime $m_\sigma \gg m_h$ these effects are expected
to be suppressed by powers of $m_h^2/m_\sigma^2$ or by the corresponding
portal coefficient.
At present ECT does not predict the size of this mixing; it is a
Level~C phenomenological possibility.

\paragraph{Reframing the hierarchy problem.}
The usual SM hierarchy problem asks: why is the Higgs mass so small compared
to the Planck scale~\cite{tHooft1979}?
ECT recasts this as a condensate-spectrum question: why does a second
condensate branch appear at $v_2\sim 10^{-16}\,u_0$?
This is still an open problem~(OP-EW), but the reframing is itself meaningful:
instead of a fine-tuning of one scalar mass, the challenge becomes explaining
the dynamical origin of a second ordered phase at an intermediate scale.
A dimensional-transmutation estimate via RGE of the condensate quartic coupling
would in principle generate such a scale, but this requires external SM input
(top-quark Yukawa $y_t\approx0.95$) and remains Level~C~(Appendix~\ref{app:second_transition}).


\paragraph{Radiative stability: what ECT does and does not solve.}
The reframing of the hierarchy problem as a condensate-spectrum
question does not by itself resolve the radiative stability issue.
If the secondary radial mode $h$ couples to other fields (gauge
bosons, fermions) with generic EFT couplings, its mass receives
quadratic radiative corrections of order
\begin{equation}
  \delta m_h^2 \;\sim\; \frac{g^2}{16\pi^2}\,m_\sigma^2
  \;\sim\; \frac{g^2}{16\pi^2}\,\bar{M}_{\rm Pl}^2.
  \label{eq:quadratic_correction}
\end{equation}
This is a schematic EFT estimate; the precise coefficient depends on
the couplings and field content of the secondary sector.
The resulting fine-tuning is of the same order as in the Standard Model.
ECT does not claim to solve this problem.
What it offers is a different \emph{physical context}:
the fine-tuning question becomes whether the dynamics of the
second condensate transition naturally selects $v_2 \ll u_0$.
A possible mechanism is dimensional transmutation via the
RG running of the effective quartic coupling from $u_0$ to $v_2$
(Section~\ref{sec:three_scales},
Appendix~\ref{app:second_transition}), but this remains
Level~C and has not been demonstrated within ECT.
Absent an additional symmetry, compositeness mechanism, or dynamical
selection principle, the secondary scalar remains quadratically
sensitive to the primary UV scale.
Any claim that ECT resolves the naturalness problem would be
premature.

\paragraph{Comparison with other Higgs frameworks.}

\emph{Standard Model Higgs:}
in the SM the Higgs is an elementary scalar doublet introduced
phenomenologically~\cite{EnglertBrout1964,Higgs1964}.
In ECT radial modes emerge naturally as condensate amplitude excitations;
the primary mode is necessarily present, while the Higgs-like secondary
mode is conditional on the second transition.

\emph{Condensed-matter amplitude (Higgs) modes:}
in superfluids and in appropriate broken-phase condensed-matter systems,
one encounters a phase mode together with a massive amplitude
(Higgs-like) mode~\cite{LittlewoodVarma1981,Volovik2003}.
The ECT primary radial mode $\sigma$ is the direct analogue of such an
amplitude mode --- but here the condensate underlies spacetime structure itself,
not a material medium.

\emph{EFT hierarchy and decoupling:}
in standard EFT, heavy particles decouple at low energies and generate
suppressed higher-dimension operators.
The Planck-heavy primary mode $\sigma$ of ECT plays the analogous role:
it integrates out at $E\ll m_\sigma$, leaving the broken-phase EFT
that governs lower-energy physics.

A further useful comparison is with frameworks in which the Higgs
is itself a non-elementary excitation of an underlying sector.

\emph{Composite Higgs models~\cite{Kaplan1984,Agashe2005}:}
in these models the Higgs is not elementary but a pseudo-Nambu--Goldstone
boson of a new strongly coupled sector.
The ECT secondary radial mode shares the feature of being a
\emph{condensate excitation} rather than a fundamental scalar, but
differs in mechanism: it is the amplitude fluctuation of a second
ordered phase, not a pseudo-NGB of a confining gauge theory.
Unlike pseudo-Nambu--Goldstone Higgs scenarios, the ECT secondary radial
mode is not protected by an approximate shift symmetry in the present
formulation.
The two approaches face the same hierarchy challenge; ECT recasts it
as a condensate-dynamics question rather than a compositeness question.

\paragraph{Status summary.}
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{p{6.3cm}p{2.0cm}p{5.0cm}}
\hline

  & A & Amplitude fluctuation of $|\partial\Phi|$ \\
Primary radial mode is distinct from the SM Higgs
  & A & Different condensate level \\

  & A & Integrability of $Q_A$ (Section~\ref{sec:goldstone_integrability_main}) \\

  & B & Uses scale matching $u_0\sim\bar{M}_{\rm Pl}$ \\

  sector through $S_0^{\rm EFT}$
  & B & Via the radial-core scale in the minimal
    single-condensate realisation \\

  & B & Correlation-length and decoupling scale \\

  & B/C & Conditional on the electroweak-like second condensate step \\

  & B/C & Formal radial analogue; not a derivation \\
$m_h = \sqrt{2\lambda_2}\,v_2$ with $v_2$ from $G_F$ matching
  & B/C & Matching input, not ECT prediction \\

\subsection{Emergence of the Graviton}
\label{sec:graviton}

\textit{Status: Level~A for the existence of an induced effective-metric
sector sourced by condensate orientation fluctuations.
Level~B for the Fierz--Pauli-type quadratic structure and for the
massless spin-2 interpretation.
Full non-perturbative ghost-freedom and TT projection remain
open~(OP3).
Connection to macroscopic gravity: Section~\ref{sec:macroscopic},
Appendix~\ref{app:phi_first_EFE}.}

Within the classification of Section~\ref{sec:particle_structure},
the graviton belongs to the class of required condensate excitations of
the primary ordered branch.

\paragraph{Strategy: graviton via effective metric fluctuations.}

A direct fluctuation of the orientation field $n_A$ carries vector
rather than tensor character and therefore does \emph{not} produce a
spin-2 excitation.
To obtain a massless spin-2 graviton one must instead work with
fluctuations of the \emph{effective metric} induced by the condensate.
Unlike Einstein--aether constructions, the preferred direction in ECT is
not introduced as an independent fundamental vector field but emerges
from the ordered condensate background.

\textbf{Step 1: effective metric.}
The condensate background defines an effective metric through the
propagation structure of perturbations.
For the general two-parameter form with parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$,
the effective inverse metric is
\begin{equation}
\label{eq:geff}
G^{AB}_{\rm eff} = \delta^{AB} - (\alpha-\beta)\,n^A n^B.
\end{equation}
In the simplified normalisation $\beta=1$ this reduces to
$G^{AB}_{\rm eff} = \delta^{AB} - (\alpha-1)\,n^A n^B$.
In the broken-symmetry vacuum $\langle n_A\rangle=\delta_{Aw}$ one
obtains $G^{AB}_{\rm eff}=\mathrm{diag}(1,1,1,2-\alpha)$, which has
Lorentzian signature for $\alpha>\beta$.
The corresponding covariant metric is
\begin{equation}
\label{eq:geff_cov_legacy}
g^{\rm eff}_{AB} = \delta_{AB}
+ \frac{\alpha-\beta}{(\beta+1)-\alpha}\,n_A n_B
\approx \eta_{AB} + h_{AB},
\end{equation}
where, after analytic continuation $w\to-ic_*t$ and with
$\alpha=2\beta$, $\beta=1$, the background metric is
$\eta_{AB}=\mathrm{diag}(-1,1,1,1)$.
The induced symmetric effective-metric perturbation is
\begin{equation}
\label{eq:graviton_field}
h_{AB}
= -(\alpha-\beta)\!\left(\delta_{Aw}\,\delta n_B + \delta_{Bw}\,\delta n_A\right)
+ O(\delta n^2).
\end{equation}
The candidate spin-2 graviton sector is identified with the
transverse-traceless part of this induced effective-metric
perturbation, rather than with a direct degree of freedom of $n_A$
itself.

\textbf{Step 2: Fierz--Pauli-type structure.}
Expanding $S_{\rm ECT}$~\eqref{eq:phi_first_action} around the ordered
branch and projecting the orientation fluctuation into the
effective-metric channel is expected to yield, at quadratic order, an
action of Fierz--Pauli type:
\begin{equation}
\label{eq:FP_action_legacy}
S^{(2)}_{\rm grav}
\sim
M_{\rm grav}^2
\int d^4X\left[
\nabla_C h_{AB}\nabla^C h^{AB}
- \tfrac{1}{2}(\nabla_A h)^2
+ \cdots
\right],
\end{equation}
where the overall normalisation $M_{\rm grav}$ is fixed by matching to
the macroscopic gravitational sector
(Section~\ref{sec:macroscopic}).
At the present stage this is a structural linearised reconstruction,
not yet a fully explicit first-principles derivation from the bare
condensate action.

\textbf{Step 3: massless spin-2 interpretation.}
After analytic continuation $w\to-ic_*t$, the linearised
effective-metric sector admits a massless spin-2 interpretation at the
quadratic level.
Masslessness is protected, at the linearised effective level,
by the emergent gauge redundancy of the Fierz--Pauli description
(the linearised diffeomorphism invariance of the effective metric
theory).
A non-perturbative proof of masslessness from the bare condensate
action has not been obtained; the interpretation is valid at the
quadratic effective level only.

\textbf{Honest caveats.}
The derivation above relies on two steps that require further
verification: (i) the explicit computation of the quadratic
action~\eqref{eq:FP_action_legacy} directly
from~\eqref{eq:phi_first_action} by expanding in $h_{AB}$,
checking that cross-terms between $\mathcal{L}_n$ and the Ricci
scalar combine correctly; and (ii) the projection from the vector
fluctuation $\delta n_A$ to the symmetric tensor $h_{AB}$
via~\eqref{eq:graviton_field}, which must be verified to capture
exactly two transverse-traceless polarisations and no ghost modes.
These steps are feasible but have not been carried out in full
detail; they are listed as priority open problems in
Section~\ref{sec:open_problems}.

\paragraph{Relation to the radial mode and the condensate spectrum.}
The graviton ($h_{AB}$) and the primary radial mode ($\sigma$)
are both excitations of the single condensate field $\Phi$, but they
belong to different sectors:
$\sigma$ is the amplitude fluctuation of $|Q_A| = |\partial_A\Phi|$
(Section~\ref{sec:radial_mode}), while $h_{AB}$ arises from the
orientation fluctuation $\delta n_A$ projected into the
effective-metric channel.
These are distinct at linear order in the broken-phase EFT and do not
mix in the quadratic action.

\paragraph{The Weinberg--Witten theorem.}
The Weinberg--Witten theorem~\cite{WeinbergWitten1980} states that
a theory with a Lorentz-covariant conserved energy-momentum tensor
cannot contain massless particles with spin $j>1$ that couple to
$T^{\mu\nu}$.
This is often read as an obstruction to emergent or composite graviton
scenarios formulated within a fundamentally Lorentz-covariant framework.
In ECT, the theorem's standard assumptions are not satisfied at the
fundamental level:
\begin{itemize}
  \item the fundamental level of ECT is a Euclidean scalar theory on
    $\mathcal{M}^4$, not a Lorentz-covariant quantum field theory;
  \item the Noether tensor $T^{AB}_{(\Phi)}$ of P3 is an
    $O(4)$-covariant Euclidean object, not a Lorentz-covariant
    $T^{\mu\nu}$;
  \item Lorentz covariance is \emph{emergent} for the perturbative
    sector of the broken phase, not a symmetry of the fundamental
    description;
  \item the graviton is not a bound state of Lorentz-covariant
    particles but a collective mode of the condensate order parameter
    on a pre-Lorentzian background.
\end{itemize}
A useful heuristic analogy is provided by superfluid and
analogue-gravity systems~\cite{Volovik2003,Unruh1981}: effective
spin-2 modes of the acoustic metric do not violate the
Weinberg--Witten theorem because the fundamental theory
(quantum liquid) is not itself Lorentz-covariant.
The point is not that ECT ``evades'' the theorem, but that its
fundamental formulation lies outside the theorem's standard domain of
applicability.

\paragraph{Universality of the emergent light cone.}
In the minimal broken-phase EFT, the graviton sector is tied to the same
effective Lorentzian structure that governs the propagation of the other
low-energy condensate modes.
Accordingly, the gravitational wave cone is not an independent extra
structure but part of the same emergent causal geometry.

\paragraph{Gravitational-wave speed.}
In the minimal matching where the effective propagation speed of the
ordered branch is identified with the observed speed of light,
the graviton sector is compatible with $c_g = c$.
The binary-neutron-star event
GW170817/GRB~170817A~\cite{LIGO_Virgo_GW170817_2017} provides the
observational consistency bound $|\delta c/c| < 10^{-15}$.
This is a consistency check of the matching, not an independent
prediction of the present ECT formulation.

\paragraph{Polarisation content.}
If the linearised effective-metric sector closes precisely to the
Fierz--Pauli form, the propagating graviton content is expected to
reduce to the usual two transverse-traceless polarisations.
Verifying this reduction explicitly remains part of the open
programme~(OP-gauge).
This is in contrast to generic beyond-GR theories, which can admit up
to six independent polarisation modes.
Current and future gravitational-wave observations (pulsar timing
arrays, LISA, third-generation ground-based detectors) can in principle
distinguish two-polarisation from multi-polarisation scenarios.
Possible observational tests of the graviton sector are summarised in
the global falsification table~(Table~\ref{tab:ECT_predictions}).

\paragraph{Comparison with other emergent-gravity approaches.}

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3}
\begin{tabular}{p{3.2cm}p{3.8cm}p{3.2cm}p{3.0cm}}
\hline
Approach & Graviton origin & Lorentzian signature & Metric status \\
\hline
General relativity
  & Fundamental field $g_{\mu\nu}$
  & Postulated
  & Fundamental \\
Sakharov induced gravity~\cite{Sakharov1968}
  & Loop corrections from matter
  & Postulated
  & Background input \\
Analogue gravity~\cite{Unruh1981,Volovik2003}
  & Acoustic metric of fluid
  & Emergent
  & Effective \\
CDT~\cite{Ambjorn2005}
  & Lattice path integral
  & Implemented through causal triangulation rules
  & Emergent from triangulations \\
Einstein-aether~\cite{Jacobson2004}
  & $u^A$ + metric, both fundamental
  & Postulated
  & Fundamental \\
\textbf{ECT}
  & Effective-metric perturbation from condensate SSB
  & \textbf{Emergent} from $O(4)\to O(3)$
  & \textbf{Effective}, from the single scalar $\Phi$
    in the present minimal formulation \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\noindent
Among the approaches compared here, ECT is distinguished by treating
both the Lorentzian signature and the effective metric structure as
emergent from a single scalar condensate on a Euclidean background.
This is a structural claim about the framework, not about its
current level of derivational completeness.

\paragraph{Potential UV advantage (speculative).}
A potential advantage of the ECT graviton construction is that the
spin-2 excitation arises from fluctuations of a condensate order
parameter on a fixed Euclidean background, rather than from direct
quantisation of the metric.
Whether this structural difference leads to improved ultraviolet
behaviour requires explicit power counting and has not been
demonstrated.
This is noted as a speculative structural observation~(Level~C),
not as a completed result.

\paragraph{Connection to the gravitational equations.}
The full development of the gravitational sector---linearised and
generalised Einstein equations, the $\phi$-first scalar-tensor IR
closure, and Theorems A--F---belongs to
Section~\ref{sec:macroscopic} and
Appendix~\ref{app:phi_first_EFE}.
The Deser self-coupling bootstrap~\cite{Deser1970} provides a
possible route from the linearised Fierz--Pauli sector to nonlinear
Einstein equations; the ECT geometric branch is structurally
compatible with this route, though the bootstrap has not been
carried out within ECT~(Level~B/C;
Section~\ref{sec:macroscopic}).
The present subsection establishes only that the condensate order
parameter contains a linearised effective-metric sector with
a Fierz--Pauli-type structure at quadratic order.

\paragraph{Status summary.}

  & A & Propagation structure of perturbations \\

\subsection{Gauge Bosons from Condensate Phase Symmetries}
\label{sec:gauge_particles}

If the condensate field possesses internal phase symmetries, gauge bosons can be accommodated via the phase holonomy mechanism (Level~B; the SM gauge group is embedded, not derived).

For a complex condensate

\begin{equation}
\Phi = \rho e^{i\theta}
\end{equation}

the theory is invariant under

\begin{equation}
\theta \rightarrow \theta + \Lambda(X).
\end{equation}

Introducing the covariant derivative

\begin{equation}
D_A = \partial_A - i e A_A
\end{equation}

generates the gauge kinetic term

\begin{equation}
S \supset \frac{1}{4} \int F_{AB}F^{AB} d^4X .
\end{equation}

The corresponding gauge boson is massless,

\begin{equation}
m_\gamma = 0,
\end{equation}

due to the unbroken $U(1)$ symmetry.

\textbf{Status (Level~B/C, embedding).}
This massless $U(1)$ field admits a structural identification
with the photon-like sector once internal phase symmetry is admitted.
This is an \emph{embedding}, not a first-principles derivation of
the full electromagnetic sector of the Standard Model:
the $U(1)$ symmetry is introduced as an extension of the minimal
bosonic condensate, and the full quantum electrodynamics structure
is not derived from the scalar condensate alone.

\subsection{Electroweak Sector and the Higgs Interpretation in ECT}
\label{sec:ew_particles}

\textit{Status: Level~B/C structural embedding.
The electroweak sector is not derived from minimal ECT; it is shown
to be structurally admissible if a second condensate transition
$O(3)\to O(2)$ exists.
This subsection consolidates the Higgs interpretation from the
radial-mode analysis~(Section~\ref{sec:radial_mode}), the
electroweak-like gauge embedding~(Section~\ref{sec:ew_embedding}),
and the structural candidate mechanism~(Appendix~\ref{app:second_transition}).}

The Higgs discussion in ECT has two complementary sides:
the gauge-embedding side, where the electroweak-like EFT is introduced
(Section~\ref{sec:ew_embedding}), and the radial-mode side, where the
secondary amplitude excitation is analysed as a condensate mode
(Section~\ref{sec:radial_mode}).
The present subsection brings these two viewpoints together.

\paragraph{The Higgs boson as a second condensate radial mode.}
In the Standard Model, the Higgs boson is an elementary scalar field
introduced independently of the spacetime
metric~\cite{EnglertBrout1964,Higgs1964}.
In ECT, the role of the Higgs is played by the radial excitation~$h$
of the second condensate vacuum at scale
$v_2\approx246\,$GeV~(Section~\ref{sec:radial_mode}):
\begin{equation}
  \phi_{\rm ew} = v_2 + h, \qquad
  m_h^2 = 2\lambda_2\,v_2^2.
\end{equation}
This is not an independent fundamental field, but the amplitude
fluctuation of a second ordered phase of the same underlying
$\Phi$-medium whose first ordered phase is associated with the
Lorentzian spacetime and gravitational sector.
The distinction is conceptual: the SM Higgs and the spacetime metric
are unrelated structures; in ECT they would both arise from a single
underlying medium.

\paragraph{Coupling pattern.}
If the electroweak embedding is realised at the level of the SM
gauge structure~(Section~\ref{sec:ew_embedding}), the Higgs couplings
to $W^\pm$, $Z$, and fermions follow the Standard Model pattern
at leading order.
This is a direct consequence of the embedding: the low-energy EFT
is \emph{designed} to reproduce the SM structure, and the Higgs
coupling pattern is therefore an input, not a prediction.
Accordingly, agreement with the observed tree-level Higgs coupling
pattern is a consistency requirement of the embedding, not an emergent
prediction of minimal ECT.

\paragraph{ECT-specific sources of deviation.}
Within the ECT framework, three potential sources of deviation from
SM Higgs properties are structurally identified, though none is
quantitatively predicted at the present stage:

\emph{(i) Mixing with the primary radial mode.}
The Higgs-like mode $h$ and the Planck-heavy primary radial mode
$\sigma$ belong to different ordered phases of the same medium.
A mixing term is generically allowed unless forbidden by an
additional symmetry~(Section~\ref{sec:radial_mode}).
After diagonalisation, such mixing would generically induce small
universal deviations in the Higgs couplings to Standard Model fields.
In the decoupling regime $m_\sigma \gg m_h$ these effects are expected
to be strongly suppressed, typically by powers of $m_h/m_\sigma$ and by
the corresponding portal coefficient.
At present ECT does not predict the size of this mixing.

\emph{(ii) Non-quartic corrections to the Higgs self-coupling.}
If the effective potential at scale $v_2$ deviates from the
quartic form, the cubic self-interaction
$V_{\rm ew}'''(v_2)=3m_h^2/v_2$ receives corrections.
Such deviations are in principle testable through Higgs pair
production at HL-LHC or FCC-hh~(Section~\ref{sec:radial_mode}).

\emph{(iii) Condensate-sector corrections to Yukawa couplings.}
If the Yukawa couplings $y_f$ receive contributions from the
primary condensate sector---for instance through higher-dimension
operators suppressed by the primary radial scale---small deviations
from the SM relation $m_f = y_f v_2$ could appear.
At present ECT does not predict the size of such effects.
In the minimal decoupling picture they are expected to be highly
suppressed, but a quantitative estimate requires a concrete model of
the second transition and of the matter-coupling sector.

\paragraph{Comparison: SM Higgs versus ECT Higgs.}

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.3}
\begin{tabular}{p{4.0cm}p{4.5cm}p{4.5cm}}
\hline
Property & Standard Model & ECT \\
\hline
Ontological status
  & Elementary scalar field
  & Radial excitation of second condensate phase \\
Relation to spacetime
  & Independent of metric
  & Same underlying medium as the gravitational/spacetime sector \\
$v_2 = 246\,$GeV
  & Fundamental VEV
  & Matching constraint (if second transition exists) \\
$m_h = 125\,$GeV
  & Determined by $\lambda$ and $v$
  & Determined by $\lambda_2$ and $v_2$ (same formula) \\
Coupling pattern
  & Predicted by SM gauge structure
  & Reproduced by embedding (input, not prediction) \\
Cubic Higgs interaction near the minimum
  & $V'''(v) = 3m_h^2/v$
  & Same if $V_{\rm ew}$ is quartic; deviations possible \\
Naturalness
  & Quadratic divergence $\sim\Lambda^2$
  & Same problem: $\delta m_h^2\sim m_\sigma^2$ \\
Radial partner
  & None
  & Primary radial mode $\sigma\sim M_{\rm Pl}$ \\
Mixing with primary condensate radial mode
  & No analogue in the minimal SM
  & Possible $\sigma$--$h$ mixing \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\paragraph{What ECT adds and what it does not.}
ECT does not derive the Higgs boson from first principles.
What it adds is a \emph{structural context}: the Higgs is
reinterpreted as one of two radial modes of a single condensate,
the other being the Planck-heavy primary mode that defines the
UV threshold of the effective theory.
This reinterpretation does not currently lead to a quantitative
prediction that differs robustly from the Standard Model, but it
changes the conceptual status of the hierarchy
problem~(Section~\ref{sec:radial_mode}): instead of asking why an
elementary scalar is light, one asks why the condensate undergoes
a second ordering step at $v_2\ll u_0$.

The electroweak sector remains a structural embedding at
Level~B/C, not a completed derivation.
Its full first-principles realisation within ECT requires closing
OP-EW~(Section~\ref{sec:three_scales},
Appendix~\ref{app:second_transition}).

\paragraph{Status summary.}

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{p{6.0cm}p{2.0cm}p{5.0cm}}
\hline
Statement & Status & Comment \\
\hline
Higgs as radial excitation of second condensate
  phase
  & B/C & If second transition exists \\
SM coupling pattern reproduced by embedding
  & B/C & Input, not prediction \\
$\sigma$--$h$ mixing as source of coupling
  deviations
  & C & Generically allowed; magnitude not predicted \\
Non-quartic corrections to Higgs self-coupling
  & C & Testable in principle at HL-LHC/FCC \\
Condensate-sector corrections to Yukawa couplings
  & C & Expected to be suppressed; magnitude not
    predicted \\
Higgs and spacetime associated with the same
  underlying medium
  & B/C & Conceptual reinterpretation, if the second
    transition exists \\
Radiative naturalness of the Higgs-like mode
  & Open & Same quadratic sensitivity issue as in the SM \\
Full EW sector derived from P1--P5
  & Open (OP-EW) & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsection{Fermions (Level~B: structural route)}
\label{sec:fermion_particles}

\textit{Status: Level~B structural reconstruction.
Spinorial excitations are compatible with the ordered condensate
background but are not derived from the bare bosonic action.
Full development: Section~\ref{sec:fermions}.}

Within the classification of Section~\ref{sec:particle_structure},
fermions belong to category~(ii): structurally embedded Standard
Model fields.

The symmetry breaking $O(4)\to O(3)$ leaves a residual continuous
rotation symmetry $SO(3)$, whose double cover $SU(2)$ admits
spinor representations.
After analytic continuation to Lorentzian spacetime the relevant
group becomes $SL(2,\mathbb{C})$, and Dirac spinors can be
constructed on the ordered condensate background as two Weyl
components $\Psi = (\psi_L, \psi_R)^T$.
This is a structural compatibility statement, not a derivation of
spinors as collective condensate excitations.

The fermionic sector is developed in
Section~\ref{sec:fermions}, which covers:
\begin{itemize}
  \item spinor representations of the ordered
    branch~(Section~\ref{sec:spinor_structure});
  \item structural reconstruction of the Dirac
    equation~(Section~\ref{sec:dirac_eq});
  \item chirality and the selection of
    $SU(2)_L$~(Section~\ref{sec:chirality});
  \item Yukawa couplings and fermion
    masses~(Section~\ref{sec:yukawa});
  \item ECT-specific fermion--condensate
    coupling~(Section~\ref{sec:fifth_force_fermion});
  \item spin-statistics structural
    route~(Section~\ref{sec:fermion_statistics}).
\end{itemize}
A currently identified ECT-specific fermion--condensate interaction
beyond the Standard Model embedding is
$\Delta\mathcal{L} = \beta_5\,\bar\Psi\gamma^A n_A\Psi$.
Its structural form is introduced in
Section~\ref{sec:fifth_force_fermion}, while its detailed
phenomenology, observational constraints, and falsification tests are
developed in Section~\ref{sec:fifth_force}.

In the present chapter fermions are treated only at the level of
structural compatibility and effective embedding.
The detailed fermionic programme is deferred to
Section~\ref{sec:fermions}, where spinor structure, the Dirac form,
chirality, Yukawa couplings, the fermion--condensate coupling, and the
spin-statistics route are discussed separately.

\subsection{Neutrino Sector}
\label{sec:neutrino_particles}

\textit{Status: Level~C speculative SM embedding.
The neutrino sector is not derived from the minimal ECT
condensate action; it is a phenomenological embedding
drawing on Standard-Model mechanisms.
Connection to leptogenesis: Section~\ref{sec:baryogenesis}.}

\paragraph{Right-handed neutrino: Dirac versus Majorana possibilities.}
In the minimal ECT embedding discussed so far, right-handed neutrinos
are not required~(Section~\ref{sec:fermion_particles}).
At the present stage ECT does not distinguish between Dirac and
Majorana neutrinos.
A purely Dirac embedding with very small Yukawa couplings remains
logically possible.
The seesaw scenario is discussed here because it provides the most
natural bridge to the leptogenesis mechanism used in
Section~\ref{sec:baryogenesis}.

If right-handed neutrinos are admitted as additional embedded fields
(Level~C), a type-I seesaw mechanism can generate light neutrino
masses, consistent with oscillation
experiments~\cite{Fukuda1998}:
\begin{equation}
  m_\nu \sim \frac{(y_\nu v_2)^2}{M_R},
  \label{eq:seesaw}
\end{equation}
up to convention-dependent factors of order unity depending on the
normalisation of the electroweak condensate field.
Here $y_\nu$ is the neutrino Yukawa coupling,
$v_2 \approx 246\,$GeV is the electroweak condensate
scale~(Section~\ref{sec:ew_embedding}), and $M_R$ is the
right-handed neutrino Majorana mass.
All three parameters are external inputs, not derived from ECT.

\paragraph{ECT-specific scale observation.}
The two condensate scales of ECT ($u_0 \sim \bar{M}_{\rm Pl}
\approx 2.4\times10^{18}\,$GeV and $v_2 \approx 246\,$GeV) define a
geometric mean:
\begin{equation}
  M_R^{\rm geom} \equiv \sqrt{u_0\,v_2}
  \approx \sqrt{2.4\times10^{18}\times246}\;\text{GeV}
  \approx 2.4\times10^{10}\;\text{GeV}.
  \label{eq:MR_geom}
\end{equation}
If $M_R$ is of this order, the seesaw formula~\eqref{eq:seesaw}
does \emph{not} reproduce the observed sub-eV neutrino masses for
order-one Yukawa couplings.
Indeed, for $y_\nu \sim 1$ one finds
$m_\nu \sim v_2^2/M_R^{\rm geom}$ in the keV range.
To obtain $m_\nu$ in the observed sub-eV regime with
$M_R \sim M_R^{\rm geom}$, one would need small neutrino Yukawa
couplings.
This makes $M_R^{\rm geom}$ a suggestive intermediate scale, not a
successful neutrino-mass prediction by itself~(Level~B/C).

For smaller Yukawa couplings the effective neutrino mass decreases as
$m_\nu \propto y_\nu^2$.
Observed sub-eV neutrino masses can be reproduced for broad ranges
of parameters, for example with
$M_R \sim 10^{9}$--$10^{11}\,$GeV and sufficiently small neutrino
Yukawa couplings.
ECT does not predict the required values of $y_\nu$ or $M_R$.

\paragraph{Connection to leptogenesis.}
In the baryogenesis discussion~(Section~\ref{sec:baryogenesis}),
a right-handed neutrino scale $M_R \sim 10^9\,$GeV is used as an
illustrative benchmark for the resonant leptogenesis estimate
$\eta_B \sim 9\times10^{-10}$.
The consistency of the seesaw mechanism with the ECT baryogenesis
channel is a Level~B structural compatibility result, not a
closed derivation.

\paragraph{Low-energy Majorana mass term and the Weinberg operator.}
After integrating out a heavy right-handed neutrino, the low-energy
effective theory contains the dimension-5 Weinberg operator of the
schematic form
\begin{equation}
  \mathcal{L}_{\rm Weinberg}
  \sim
  \frac{c_{ij}}{\Lambda}\,(L_i \Phi_{\rm ew})(L_j \Phi_{\rm ew})
  + \text{h.c.},
\end{equation}
which, after electroweak symmetry breaking, induces a Majorana mass
term for the light neutrinos:
\begin{equation}
  \mathcal{L}_M
  =
  \frac{1}{2}\,m_{\nu,ij}\,\nu_{Li}^T C \nu_{Lj} + \text{h.c.}
  \label{eq:majorana}
\end{equation}
In ECT this is a standard low-energy neutrino-mass embedding, not a
derivation from condensate dynamics.

\paragraph{Observational handle.}
If the neutrino sector is Majorana-like, neutrinoless double beta
decay provides the cleanest experimental probe.
ECT does not currently predict the effective Majorana mass parameter,
so this connection is qualitative rather than quantitative.

\paragraph{What is and is not derived here.}
This subsection imposes the seesaw mechanism as a Standard-Model-like
embedding.
Neither the existence of right-handed neutrinos, nor their Majorana
mass $M_R$, nor the neutrino Yukawa couplings $y_\nu$ are derived
from ECT condensate dynamics.
The geometric-mean observation $M_R^{\rm geom} = \sqrt{u_0 v_2}$
is a suggestive numerical coincidence, not a prediction.
A condensate-specific derivation of the right-handed neutrino scale
remains an open problem~(OP15).

\paragraph{Status summary.}
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{p{6.0cm}p{2.0cm}p{5.0cm}}
\hline
Statement & Status & Comment \\
\hline
Type-I seesaw formula
  $m_\nu \sim (y_\nu v_2)^2/M_R$
  & C & SM embedding; $M_R$, $y_\nu$ not
    derived \\
Geometric-mean scale
  $M_R^{\rm geom} = \sqrt{u_0\,v_2}
  \approx 2.4\times10^{10}\,$GeV
  & B/C & Suggestive numerical coincidence;
    not a derivation \\
Neutrino mass in observed range for suitable
  $M_R$ and $y_\nu$
  & C & Consistency check, not prediction \\
Consistency with leptogenesis channel
  & B & Section~\ref{sec:baryogenesis} \\
Right-handed neutrino from ECT dynamics
  & Open (OP15) & Not derived \\
Low-energy Majorana mass term / Weinberg-operator
  embedding
  & C & Standard neutrino-mass embedding;
    not derived from condensate dynamics \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsection{Predicted New States and Topological Sectors}
\label{sec:predicted_states}
% backward-compatibility alias:
\label{sec:instantons}

\textit{Status: layered.
The topological classification of the vacuum manifold is Level~A
(homotopy of $S^3$).
The existence of specific condensate excitations and their
phenomenological properties are Level~B/C.
The topological mass ansatz cited in the derivation chain~(D7) is
a phenomenological estimate, not a first-principles derivation.
The topological classification is therefore strongest in its
qualitative sector statements (which sectors are allowed or forbidden),
whereas quantitative mass estimates belong to phenomenological closure
and are directly falsifiable independently of the underlying homotopy
classification.
This distinction is important for falsifiability: topology fixes which
sectors are allowed or forbidden, whereas any quantitative mass ansatz
for those sectors belongs to phenomenological closure and can be tested
independently.}

ECT admits three qualitatively distinct classes of states beyond the
observed Standard Model spectrum: smooth condensate excitations,
topologically classified sectors of the ordered vacuum, and
(conditionally) defect configurations from a possible second
transition.
This subsection classifies each class and specifies its derivation
status.

The spectrum discussed here combines three strands developed earlier in
the chapter: the smooth radial-mode sector
(Section~\ref{sec:radial_mode}), the electroweak-like conditional sector
(Section~\ref{sec:ew_particles}), and the topological classification of
vacuum manifolds (Appendix~\ref{app:relic_homotopy} and
Section~\ref{sec:qs_defects}).

% ============ CLASS 1: SMOOTH EXCITATIONS ============

\paragraph{Class~1: smooth condensate excitations.}

\emph{(a) Primary radial mode $\sigma$.}
The amplitude fluctuation of the ordered condensate, with
$m_\sigma \sim \sqrt{2\lambda}\,u_0 \sim \bar{M}_{\rm Pl}$
(Section~\ref{sec:radial_mode}).
Its presence is a Level~A consequence of treating the ordered branch as
a condensate with amplitude as well as orientation fluctuations.
It is Planck-heavy, integrates out at $E \ll m_\sigma$, and defines
the physical UV threshold of the broken-phase EFT.

\emph{(b) Surviving soft scalar $\chi$.}
In the scalar-only ECT basis, the integrability constraint
$\partial_A Q_B - \partial_B Q_A = 0$ reduces the naive three-component
Goldstone sector to a single longitudinal scalar mode $\chi$
(Section~\ref{sec:goldstone_integrability_main},
Appendix~\ref{app:goldstone_integrability}).
Its mass, couplings, and stability are not determined at the present
stage~(Level~B).
Whether $\chi$ plays a cosmological role (e.g.\ as a dark-sector
candidate) remains open~(Section~\ref{sec:dark_sector}).

% ============ CLASS 2: TOPOLOGICAL SECTORS ============

\paragraph{Class~2: topological sectors from the primary SSB.}

The vacuum manifold of the primary ordering transition is
$\mathcal{M}_1 = O(4)/O(3) \simeq S^3$.
Its homotopy groups determine which topological defects are
structurally permitted and which are
forbidden~\cite{Nakahara2003}~(Level~A):

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{lll}
\hline
Homotopy group & Value & Consequence \\
\hline
$\pi_0(S^3)$ & $0$ & No stable domain walls \\
$\pi_1(S^3)$ & $0$ & No stable cosmic strings \\
$\pi_2(S^3)$ & $0$ & No stable magnetic monopoles \\
$\pi_3(S^3)$ & $\mathbb{Z}$ & Texture/skyrmion-like sectors \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\emph{Anti-predictions (Level~A).}
The vanishing of $\pi_0$, $\pi_1$, and $\pi_2$ means that ECT
\emph{predicts the absence} of topologically stable domain walls,
cosmic strings, and magnetic monopoles from the primary condensate
transition.
This is a structural consequence of the topology of $S^3$ and does
not require additional suppression mechanisms or cosmological
dilution.
The cosmological implications of this result---in particular the
absence of the standard relic catastrophe---are developed in
Section~\ref{sec:relic_ect}.

\emph{Texture/skyrmion sector (Level~A for existence of sectors,
Level~B/C for dynamics).}
The non-trivial homotopy class $\pi_3(S^3) = \mathbb{Z}$ guarantees
the existence of topologically nontrivial field configurations
classified by an integer topological charge $Q \in \mathbb{Z}$.
These are texture-type or skyrmion-like configurations: spatially
extended field configurations in which the ordered condensate wraps the
vacuum manifold $S^3$ a nontrivial number of times.

The existence of nontrivial homotopy classes does not by itself imply
the existence of stable finite-size particle-like solitons.
Whether the corresponding configurations are dynamically stabilised
against collapse or unwinding depends on the nonlinear terms of the
effective action, possible higher-derivative contributions, gauge
effects, or other UV-completion data.
Accordingly, topology guarantees the sector labels, but not yet the
existence of stable isolated particles~(Level~B/C;
Section~\ref{sec:qs_defects}).

\paragraph{Status of the topological mass ansatz.}
The derivation chain~(D7) and the falsification
table~(Table~\ref{tab:ECT_predictions}, entry~F6) cite a
phenomenological estimate
\begin{equation}
  m_n \sim n \times 1.6\;\text{GeV}/c^2
  \label{eq:topological_mass_ansatz}
\end{equation}
for the mass of topological sectors with charge $Q = n$.
The derivation chain~(D7) and falsification table~(F6) currently refer
to this phenomenological ansatz as an ``instanton'' spectrum; in the
present section the more neutral term ``topological sector'' is
preferred in order to avoid confusion with Yang--Mills instantons.

This estimate should be understood as follows:
\begin{itemize}
  \item the integer label $n$ follows from the additive topological
    structure $\pi_3(S^3) = \mathbb{Z}$~(Level~A for the existence of
    sectors with charge $Q = n$);
  \item any mass relation $m_n = f(n)$ is dynamical rather than purely
    topological; a linear ansatz $m_n \propto n$ is therefore at most a
    phenomenological assumption, not a Level~A
    consequence~(Level~C);
  \item the overall mass scale $m_1 \sim 1.6\,$GeV is a
    phenomenological estimate obtained from dimensional analysis
    involving the condensate stiffness and core scale; it is
    \emph{not} a first-principles ECT derivation~(Level~C);
  \item whether such states are dynamically stable, whether they
    are produced in the early universe, and whether they
    contribute to observable hadronic or dark-sector physics
    are open questions.
\end{itemize}
No explicit profile solution or rigorous energy bound is presently
available in the ECT framework for these sectors.
A first-principles derivation of the topological mass spectrum
from the ECT action would require solving the full nonlinear
field equations for the skyrmion/texture profile on the ordered
background.
This belongs to the open programme of the theory.

% ============ CLASS 3: SECONDARY DEFECTS ============

\paragraph{Class~3: defect configurations from a possible
second transition (conditional).}

If the second condensate transition $O(3) \to O(2)$ is realised
(Appendix~\ref{app:second_transition}), the secondary vacuum
manifold is
\begin{equation}
  \mathcal{M}_2 = O(3)/O(2) \simeq S^2.
  \label{eq:M2_S2}
\end{equation}
Its homotopy groups are:
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{lll}
\hline
Homotopy group & Value & Consequence \\
\hline
$\pi_0(S^2)$ & $0$ & No stable domain walls \\
$\pi_1(S^2)$ & $0$ & No stable cosmic strings \\
$\pi_2(S^2)$ & $\mathbb{Z}$ & Possible monopole-like defects \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
Thus if the second transition exists, ECT would admit a sector of
$\pi_2(S^2) = \mathbb{Z}$ topological configurations.
Their interpretation as global hedgehogs or monopole-like states
depends on whether the secondary symmetry is global or gauged and on the
details of the corresponding EFT completion.
For a global secondary symmetry this would correspond to hedgehog-type
(global-monopole) configurations; in a gauged realisation one may obtain
monopole-like configurations, but their detailed status depends on the
full gauge embedding and cannot be inferred from homotopy alone.
This is conditional on OP-EW and is
Level~B/C~(Appendix~\ref{app:second_transition}).

% ============ NEW STATES vs REINTERPRETATION ============

\paragraph{New states beyond the observed Standard Model spectrum.}
Summarising the three classes above, the genuinely new condensate
states admitted by ECT, beyond the observed SM spectrum, are:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item a Planck-heavy primary radial mode $\sigma$
    (Level~A/B);
  \item a surviving soft scalar $\chi$ from the constrained
    orientation sector (Level~B; role open);
  \item a topologically nontrivial texture/skyrmion sector
    labelled by $\pi_3(S^3) = \mathbb{Z}$
    (existence Level~A; dynamics Level~B/C);
  \item (conditional) secondary monopole-like defects from
    $\pi_2(S^2) = \mathbb{Z}$ if the second transition
    exists~(Level~B/C).
\end{enumerate}
Items (i)--(iii) are structural consequences of the primary
ordered branch and do not depend on the second transition.
Item (iv) is conditional on OP-EW.

The Higgs-like secondary radial mode $h$ discussed in
Section~\ref{sec:ew_particles} is not a new state beyond the observed
SM spectrum; rather, it is an ECT reinterpretation of the observed
Higgs boson, conditional on the second transition being realised.

\paragraph{Connection to the dark sector.}
The main astrophysical successes of ECT (rotation curves, RAR, BTFR)
do not require any particle dark matter
(Section~\ref{sec:dark_sector}).
Accordingly, any dark-sector role of the states discussed here would be
supplementary rather than required for the explanation of galactic
rotation phenomenology.
Whether the topological and collective excitations listed above
contribute to the cosmic dark sector---for instance as part of the
dark-matter relic abundance or as sources of gravitational-wave
backgrounds---remains an open question developed in
Section~\ref{sec:qs_defects}.

\paragraph{Observational signatures and falsifiability.}
Two entries in the falsification
table~(Table~\ref{tab:ECT_predictions})
are directly connected to the predicted states:
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{F6:} if the topological mass ansatz
    $m_n \sim n \times 1.6\,$GeV is taken at face value,
    collider searches for resonances at $1.6$, $3.2$, $4.8\,$GeV
    would either confirm or exclude this phenomenological model.
    Non-observation would disfavour the specific mass estimate,
    not the existence of topological sectors themselves.
    In other words, F6 tests a specific phenomenological realisation of
    the topological sector, not the topological sector as such.
  \item \textbf{Absence of primary-SSB relics:} if topologically
    stable domain walls, cosmic strings, or monopoles were
    unambiguously traced to the primary $O(4)\to O(3)$ transition,
    this would contradict the homotopy classification of the primary
    vacuum manifold and hence the corresponding ECT vacuum structure.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Status summary.}

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{p{6.0cm}p{2.0cm}p{5.0cm}}
\hline
Statement & Status & Comment \\
\hline
\multicolumn{3}{l}{\textit{Smooth condensate excitations}} \\
Primary radial mode $\sigma$, $m \sim M_{\rm Pl}$
  & A/B & Amplitude fluctuation of the ordered branch \\
Surviving soft scalar $\chi$
  & B & Integrability-constrained; role open \\
\hline
\multicolumn{3}{l}{\textit{Topological sectors from primary SSB}} \\
$\pi_0 = \pi_1 = \pi_2 = 0$: no stable walls,
  strings, or monopoles
  & A & Direct anti-prediction from the topology
    of $S^3$ \\
$\pi_3(S^3) = \mathbb{Z}$: texture/skyrmion sectors
  exist

Dynamical stability of localised texture/skyrmion
  states
  & Open & Requires nonlinear condensate dynamics \\

  texture sectors
