\subsection{Summary of the particle and excitation spectrum}
\label{sec:particle_summary}

\textit{This subsection provides a condensed overview of the particle
and excitation content discussed in
Sections~\ref{sec:particle_structure}--\ref{sec:predicted_states}.
Detailed status tables are given in the corresponding subsections.}

The excitation spectrum compatible with ECT falls into the three
categories introduced in Section~\ref{sec:particle_structure}.

\paragraph{(i) Required condensate excitations.}
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Graviton:} a linearised tensorial sector of the
    effective metric induced by condensate orientation fluctuations;
    massless spin-2 interpretation at the linearised effective level,
    with full non-perturbative closure still open
    (Section~\ref{sec:graviton}).
  \item \textbf{Soft orientation mode $\chi$:} the surviving
    longitudinal soft mode of the constrained orientation sector
    in the scalar-only broken phase
    (Section~\ref{sec:particle_soft_orientation}).
  \item \textbf{Primary radial mode $\sigma$:} the heavy amplitude
    fluctuation of $|Q_A|$, with $m_\sigma \sim u_0 \sim \bar M_{\rm Pl}$
    (Section~\ref{sec:radial_mode}).
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{(ii) Structurally embedded Standard Model fields.}
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Gauge bosons:} $U(1)$, electroweak-like, and
    non-Abelian gauge sectors accommodated through internal condensate
    symmetries
    (Section~\ref{sec:gauge_particles}; full development in
    Sections~\ref{sec:u1_emergence} and~\ref{sec:gauge_summary}).
  \item \textbf{Higgs sector:} the observed Higgs boson is reinterpreted
    as the radial excitation of a secondary electroweak condensate
    phase, if the second transition exists
    (Section~\ref{sec:ew_particles}).
  \item \textbf{Fermions:} spinorial fields compatible with the ordered
    branch, with Dirac-form reconstruction on the emergent Lorentzian
    background
    (Section~\ref{sec:fermion_particles}; full development in
    Section~\ref{sec:fermions}).
  \item \textbf{Neutrinos:} a seesaw-type embedded mechanism with
    $m_\nu \sim (y_\nu v_2)^2/M_R$, and a suggestive but non-derived
    geometric-mean scale $M_R^{\rm geom}=\sqrt{u_0 v_2}$
    (Section~\ref{sec:neutrino_particles}).
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{(iii) Topological and conditional sectors.}
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Primary topological sectors:} the vacuum manifold
    $O(4)/O(3)\simeq S^3$ admits texture/skyrmion-like sectors
    classified by $\pi_3(S^3)=\mathbb Z$, while
    $\pi_0=\pi_1=\pi_2=0$ implies the absence of stable walls,
    strings, and monopoles from the primary transition
    (Section~\ref{sec:predicted_states}).
  \item \textbf{Conditional secondary defects:} if the second
    transition $O(3)\to O(2)$ exists, monopole-like defects associated
    with $\pi_2(S^2)=\mathbb Z$ may arise
    (Section~\ref{sec:predicted_states}).
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{ECT-specific observational handles.}
The main currently identified ECT-specific fermion interaction beyond
the Standard Model embedding is the fermion--condensate coupling
$\beta_5\bar\Psi\gamma^A n_A\Psi$
(Section~\ref{sec:fifth_force_fermion}; detailed phenomenology in
Section~\ref{sec:fifth_force}).
In the gravitational sector, the main discriminants are the
polarisation content of the linearised graviton sector and the
effective graviton-mass constraint
(Table~\ref{tab:ECT_predictions}).
The main astrophysical successes of ECT---flat rotation curves, RAR,
and BTFR---do not require particle dark matter, but arise from the
macroscopic $\phi$-sector closure
(Section~\ref{sec:macroscopic}).

\paragraph{Status overview.}
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{p{4.8cm}p{2.0cm}p{6.2cm}}
\hline
Excitation / sector & Status & Section \\
\hline
Graviton (linearised tensor sector)
  & A/B & \ref{sec:graviton} \\
Soft orientation mode $\chi$
  & B & \ref{sec:particle_soft_orientation} \\
Primary radial mode $\sigma$
  & B & \ref{sec:radial_mode} \\
Gauge sectors
  & B/C & \ref{sec:gauge_particles}, \ref{sec:gauge_summary} \\
Higgs interpretation
  & B/C & \ref{sec:ew_particles} \\
Fermionic sector (embedded)
  & B/C & \ref{sec:fermion_particles}, \ref{sec:fermions} \\
Neutrino sector
  & C & \ref{sec:neutrino_particles} \\
Primary topological sectors
  & A/B/C & \ref{sec:predicted_states} \\
Conditional secondary defects
  & B/C & \ref{sec:predicted_states} \\
Fermion--condensate coupling
  & B/C & \ref{sec:fifth_force_fermion} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\noindent
Taken together, the chapter distinguishes clearly between
\emph{required condensate excitations}, \emph{embedded Standard-Model
sectors}, and \emph{topological or conditional sectors}.
This distinction is essential for maintaining the Level~A/B/C
discipline of the ECT particle programme.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
\section{Fermionic Sector}
\label{sec:fermions}

\textbf{Status guide.}
This section has three distinct layers:
\begin{description}[leftmargin=3em,style=unboxed,noitemsep]
  \item[Structural reconstruction (Level~B).]
    The Dirac form on the ordered Lorentzian branch, given additional
    representation-theoretic and first-order-minimality inputs.
    Fermions are \emph{not} derived as collective excitations of the bare
    bosonic condensate; they are introduced as additional fields
    compatible with the condensate symmetry.
  \item[Phenomenological embedding (Level~C).]
    Yukawa couplings, fermion masses, neutrino sector.
    These assume Standard-Model structure as external input.
  \item[Open problems.]
    Three fermion generations, Yukawa hierarchy, fermionic
    statistics from condensate structure, right-handed neutrino scale.
\end{description}

\paragraph{Connection to the fundamental ECT action.}
The fermionic sector is introduced consistently with the full ECT
action~\eqref{eq:ECT_action}.  The scalar condensate $\Phi$ couples
to fermions via Yukawa interactions $y\bar\Psi\Phi\Psi$; gauge fields
can be embedded consistently via the phase symmetries of $\Phi$
(Level~B/C; Sections~\ref{sec:gauge}--\ref{sec:fifth_force};
SM gauge group is an input, not derived from the bare condensate action).
The \emph{structural reconstruction} of the Dirac form below is written
as an Euler--Lagrange equation of $S_{\rm ECT+f}$;
the fermion field $\Psi$ and its couplings are additional
inputs not contained in the bare scalar condensate action (Level~B/C):
\begin{enumerate}
\item The ordered-branch vacuum is characterised by
$\langle\partial_A\Phi\rangle=u_0\,\delta_{Aw}$,
where $u_0\sim\bar{M}_{\rm Pl}$ is the UV condensate amplitude
(Section~\ref{sec:three_scales}; note that $n_A=\partial_A\Phi/|\partial\Phi|$
is a derived variable, not an independent field).
\item Fermions $\Psi$ are introduced as additional fields transforming
under the spinor representation of the residual O(3) symmetry
(or its Lorentzian continuation $SL(2,\mathbb{C})$).
\item Their kinetic and mass terms are added to the ECT action:
\begin{equation}
\label{eq:S_fermion}
S_{\rm ECT+f} = S_{\rm ECT} + \int d^4X\sqrt{g}\left[
i\bar\Psi\gamma^\mu\nabla_\mu\Psi - y\bar\Psi\Phi\Psi
- \beta_5\bar\Psi\gamma^A n_A\Psi
\right],
\end{equation}
where $y$ is the Yukawa coupling, $\beta_5$ is the fifth-force
coupling (Section~\ref{sec:fifth_force}), and the gamma matrices
$\gamma^\mu$ are defined in the Lorentzian geometry induced by the
condensate.
\end{enumerate}
Once these additional fermionic inputs are in place,
the Dirac equation (Section~\ref{sec:dirac_eq}) is the
Euler--Lagrange equation of $S_{\rm ECT+f}$ with respect to
$\bar\Psi$, evaluated in the condensate background
(structural reconstruction, Level~B).

Fermionic degrees of freedom can be introduced consistently with
the spinor representations of $Spin(4)\simeq SU(2)_L\times SU(2)_R$,
the double cover of the underlying Euclidean rotation group.
This section performs a \emph{structural reconstruction} of the Dirac form.
Once (i)~emergent Lorentzian geometry and (ii)~spinorial representations of
$Spin(4)$ are admitted as Level~B inputs, the Dirac form can be
constructed algebraically.
The section does \emph{not} derive fermions from the bare bosonic condensate action.

\textbf{Honest framing.}
The symmetry group structure $Spin(4)\simeq SU(2)_L\times SU(2)_R$
admits spinor representations, and after analytic continuation to
$SL(2,\mathbb{C})$ the standard Weyl and Dirac spinors are obtained
by Clifford linearisation.  However, two important points should be
noted.  First, the spinor structure of the rotation group by itself
does not imply fermionic \emph{statistics} (anti-commutation
relations): the spin-statistics connection requires additional input.
Second, the present derivation obtains the Dirac equation as an
effective equation for fluctuations in the Lorentzian background ---
it does not microscopically derive fermions as collective excitations
of the condensate.  The latter would require a separate construction.
This section reconstructs the Dirac equation structurally (Level~B) and discusses chirality
in the ECT framework.

The fermionic programme developed here remains at the level of
structural reconstruction on the ordered branch:
it does not derive fermions from the bare bosonic condensate, but
shows how spinorial fields, Dirac kinematics, chirality, and fermion
couplings can be consistently organised once the emergent Lorentzian
background is admitted.
Quantum-statistical questions are addressed only at the level of a
structural route and belong ultimately to the broader quantum
programme.

\paragraph{Section roadmap.}
The logic of this section is layered.
Section~\ref{sec:spinor_structure} establishes the group-theoretic
compatibility of spinorial representations with the ordered branch.
Section~\ref{sec:dirac_eq} reconstructs the Dirac form on the emergent
Lorentzian background.
Section~\ref{sec:chirality} discusses the structural route to chiral
asymmetry.
Sections~\ref{sec:yukawa} and~\ref{sec:fifth_force_fermion} treat,
respectively, the Standard-Model-like Yukawa embedding and the
ECT-specific fermion--condensate coupling.
Section~\ref{sec:fermion_statistics} outlines the structural route
toward spin-statistics, and Section~\ref{sec:fermion_open}
summarises the open programme.

\subsection{Spinor structure in the condensate background}
\label{sec:spinor_structure}

\textit{Status: Level~A for the group-theoretic statements
($Spin(4)\simeq SU(2)_L\times SU(2)_R$,
$\pi_1(SO(3))=\mathbb{Z}_2$).
$\pi_1(SO(3))=\mathbb{Z}_2$ is a Level~A topological fact about the
residual rotation group; its relevance as a structural motivation for
half-integer representations is Level~B.
Fermions are not derived as collective condensate excitations;
they are introduced as additional fields compatible with the
condensate symmetry.}

Section~\ref{sec:fermion_particles} provided the brief particle-spectrum
roadmap; the present section develops that route in detail.

\paragraph{Spinors as compatible excitations (Level~B).}
In conventional quantum field theory, spinor fields are introduced as
fundamental objects transforming under the Lorentz group $SO(3,1)$.
Within the ECT framework, spinor degrees of freedom are
\emph{compatible} with the condensate geometry: after $O(4)\to O(3)$
breaking the residual group admits spinor representations, and
Clifford linearisation then yields the Dirac form.
This is a structural compatibility statement~(Level~B), not a
derivation of spinors as collective condensate excitations.

\paragraph{Euclidean rotation group and its spinorial content.}
Before symmetry breaking the condensate is invariant under rotations
in four-dimensional Euclidean space, $O(4)$.
The connected Euclidean spin group satisfies
$Spin(4) \simeq SU(2)_L \times SU(2)_R$,
while at the Lie-algebra level
$\mathfrak{so}(4) \simeq
\mathfrak{su}(2)_L \oplus \mathfrak{su}(2)_R$~(Level~A).
Rotations in four dimensions thus naturally split into two
independent $SU(2)$ sectors.
The fundamental representation of each $SU(2)$ factor is
two-dimensional; two-component spinors are among the irreducible
representations naturally available in this decomposition.

\textbf{Important caveat.}
The group-theoretic structure \emph{admits} spinorial representations;
it does not guarantee that spinorial excitations are dynamically
present as stable quasiparticles of the bare bosonic condensate.
The step from ``spinor representations exist'' to ``spinorial
particles appear in the spectrum'' requires introducing additional
fermionic degrees of freedom as external inputs.

With that proviso, if spinorial excitations are admitted, they can
be written as
\begin{equation}
  \Psi = \begin{pmatrix} \psi_L \\ \psi_R \end{pmatrix},
\end{equation}
where $\psi_L$ and $\psi_R$ transform under the two $SU(2)$ factors
of $Spin(4)$.

\paragraph{Topological availability of half-integer representations.}
The residual continuous rotation symmetry after SSB is $SO(3)$,
whose fundamental group is
$\pi_1(SO(3)) = \mathbb{Z}_2$~(Level~A).
This topological fact means that the connected rotation group has a
nontrivial double cover.
Accordingly, spinorial representations become structurally available:
a $2\pi$ rotation, trivial in $SO(3)$ itself, can act as $-1$ in the
spinorial representation lifted to the double cover.
It does not by itself imply that dynamical fermionic excitations must
occur.
What remains an additional input is the physical assumption that
such representations are \emph{realised} by dynamical excitations
of the ordered branch.

\paragraph{From Euclidean to Lorentzian spinors.}
After the condensate selects a preferred direction through $O(4)\to O(3)$,
the effective spacetime acquires a Lorentzian structure.
The corresponding symmetry group becomes $SO(3,1)$, whose universal
cover is $SL(2,\mathbb{C})$.
The pair $(\psi_L, \psi_R)$ is then compatible with the Dirac spinor
structure in the emergent Lorentzian spacetime~(Level~B).

\paragraph{Why spinors: first-order minimality.}
Among all representations of the Lorentz group, spinorial
representations are the unique ones for which a first-order wave
equation can be written via the Clifford algebra
$\{\gamma^\mu,\gamma^\nu\} = 2\eta^{\mu\nu}$:
the resulting Dirac equation is the minimal Lorentz-covariant
first-order equation whose square reproduces the relativistic
dispersion relation.
In ECT, where the Lorentzian metric is emergent and the Clifford
algebra is defined on the effective metric of the ordered branch,
this first-order minimality places spinorial representations in a
structurally privileged position among possible field types on the
ordered condensate background, once such excitations are admitted.
This is not a derivation; it is a compatibility argument.
This compatibility argument acquires dynamical content only once the
first-order Clifford-linearised equation is introduced, as discussed in
Section~\ref{sec:dirac_eq}.

\paragraph{Comparison with other emergent-fermion approaches.}
In superfluid ${}^3$He-A~\cite{Volovik2003}, Weyl fermions emerge
as low-energy excitations near topological Fermi points in
momentum space: the fermionic character is a property of the
quasiparticle spectrum, not an external input.
In ECT the situation is different: fermions are introduced as
additional fields compatible with the ordered branch.
ECT currently provides a structural compatibility route and a
Lorentzian-spinorial reconstruction, but not yet a microscopic
derivation of fermions as quasiparticles of the bare bosonic medium.

\paragraph{Status summary.}
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{p{6.0cm}p{2.0cm}p{5.0cm}}
\hline
Statement & Status & Comment \\
\hline
$Spin(4) \simeq SU(2)_L \times SU(2)_R$
  & A & Double cover of $SO(4)$ \\
Spinorial representations available through the
  double cover of the connected rotation group
  & A & Group-theoretic availability; dynamical
    realisation not implied \\
$\pi_1(SO(3)) = \mathbb{Z}_2$: topological
  availability of half-integer representations
  & A & Topological fact of residual rotation
    group \\
Half-integer representations as structural
  motivation for fermionic excitations
  & B & Physical interpretation of topological
    availability \\
Dirac spinors compatible with ordered Lorentzian
  branch
  & B & Structural compatibility \\
First-order minimality gives spinorial
  representations a structurally privileged role
  & B & Clifford linearisation on the emergent
    metric \\
Fermions as collective condensate excitations
  & Open & Not derived from bare bosonic action \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsection{Structural Reconstruction of the Dirac Form}
\label{sec:dirac_eq}

\textit{Status: Level~B structural reconstruction.
If one admits (i)~emergent Lorentzian kinematics,
(ii)~spinorial representations compatible with the broken
$O(4)\to O(3)$ background, and
(iii)~first-order Clifford linearisation as the minimal relativistic
option, then a Dirac-type first-order equation can be constructed
algebraically.
Each of these inputs goes beyond the minimal scalar condensate
postulate; the reconstruction is therefore Level~B.
The ECT-specific content is that the Clifford algebra is interpreted
with respect to the emergent metric of the ordered branch rather than
introduced on a fundamental spacetime structure from the outset.}

\paragraph{Relativistic dispersion from the emergent metric.}
After $O(4)\to O(3)$ symmetry breaking, low-energy excitations
propagate in an emergent Lorentzian spacetime.
For a free relativistic excitation on the emergent Lorentzian branch,
the natural quadratic invariant is
\begin{equation}
  p_\mu p^\mu = m^2 c_*^2,
  \qquad
  p_\mu = \left(\frac{E}{c_*},\mathbf{p}\right).
\end{equation}
For fermionic fields, the question is then whether this quadratic
relation admits a first-order spinorial linearisation.

\paragraph{Clifford algebra and linearisation.}
Introduce matrices $\gamma^\mu$ obeying the Clifford algebra
\begin{equation}
  \{\gamma^\mu,\gamma^\nu\} = 2\eta^{\mu\nu}.
\end{equation}
This algebra encodes the geometry of the local Lorentzian metric.
In ECT this metric is not a fundamental background but the
\emph{induced effective metric} of the ordered
branch~(Section~\ref{sec:graviton}): the $\eta^{\mu\nu}$ appearing
in the Clifford relation is interpreted as the flat-limit Lorentzian
metric induced by the uniform condensate vacuum.
The Clifford structure is therefore tied to the emergent metric of the
ordered branch rather than imposed on a fundamental spacetime from the
outset.
In that sense it is structurally induced by the condensate geometry,
not introduced independently of it.

Defining $\slashed{p} \equiv \gamma^\mu p_\mu$, one obtains
\begin{equation}
  (\slashed{p})^2
  = \gamma^\mu\gamma^\nu p_\mu p_\nu
  = \tfrac{1}{2}\{\gamma^\mu,\gamma^\nu\}p_\mu p_\nu
  = \eta^{\mu\nu}p_\mu p_\nu
  = p_\mu p^\mu.
\end{equation}
Thus the Clifford algebra provides a natural linearisation of the
quadratic invariant $p_\mu p^\mu$.

\paragraph{Dirac equation.}
The minimal first-order equation consistent with this structure is
\begin{equation}
  (\slashed{p} - mc_*)\Psi = 0.
\end{equation}
In coordinate space, with $p_\mu \to i\hbar\partial_\mu$, this
becomes
\begin{equation}
  \boxed{
    \left(i\hbar\gamma^\mu\partial_\mu - mc_*\right)\Psi = 0.
  }
  \label{eq:dirac_eq}
\end{equation}
This reproduces a Dirac-type equation for relativistic fermionic
excitations on the ordered condensate branch (structural
reconstruction, Level~B).
At the canonical normalisation $\alpha = 2\beta$, $\beta = 1$ one
has $c_* = c$, recovering the standard Dirac form.

\paragraph{Connection to the ECT action.}
The Dirac-type equation~\eqref{eq:dirac_eq} corresponds to the
minimal fermionic part of the extended action
$S_{\rm ECT+f}$~\eqref{eq:S_fermion} when evaluated on the ordered
condensate background and restricted to the standard first-order
kinetic-plus-mass sector.
More generally, varying the full extended action with respect to
$\bar\Psi$ yields the Dirac operator together with the additional
ECT-specific deformation discussed separately in
Section~\ref{sec:fifth_force_fermion}.
This makes the Dirac form consistent with the variational structure of
the \emph{extended} theory.
The point is not that the fermionic sector has been derived from the
bare bosonic condensate action, but that once the fermionic extension
is admitted, the fermionic equation of motion is not added ad hoc at
the equation level: it follows from varying the extended action.

\paragraph{Weyl decomposition.}
In the chiral representation the Dirac spinor decomposes into two
Weyl spinors
\begin{equation}
  \Psi = \begin{pmatrix} \psi_L \\ \psi_R \end{pmatrix},
\end{equation}
transforming under the two inequivalent two-component representations
of $SL(2,\mathbb{C})$.
This connects directly to the ECT observation that the Euclidean
spin group admits the decomposition
$Spin(4) \simeq SU(2)_L \times SU(2)_R$
(Section~\ref{sec:spinor_structure}).

\paragraph{Curved-spacetime generalisation.}
On a non-uniform condensate background, where the effective metric
$g^{\rm eff}_{\mu\nu}$ varies, the corresponding curved-background
Dirac form is naturally expected to take the form
\begin{equation}
  \left(i\hbar\gamma^a e_a{}^\mu \nabla_\mu - mc_*\right)\Psi = 0,
  \label{eq:dirac_curved}
\end{equation}
where $e_a{}^\mu$ is the emergent vierbein and $\nabla_\mu$ includes
the spin connection compatible with $g^{\rm eff}_{\mu\nu}$.
In the ECT interpretation, both the effective vierbein and the
associated spin connection are expected to be induced by the
condensate orientation field and its gradients, rather than introduced
as independent geometric structures.
At the present stage this should be read as a Level~B structural
interpretation, not as a completed explicit derivation.

\paragraph{ECT-specific fermion--condensate coupling.}
Beyond the Dirac form itself, the ordered branch admits an additional
fermion--condensate coupling
$\Delta\mathcal{L} = \beta_5\,\bar\Psi\gamma^A n_A\Psi$.
Its status within the fermionic reconstruction is discussed in
Section~\ref{sec:fifth_force_fermion}, while its phenomenology is
developed separately in Section~\ref{sec:fifth_force}.

\paragraph{What is and is not reconstructed here.}
The present subsection reconstructs the \emph{Dirac form} as the
minimal first-order spinorial equation compatible with the emergent
Lorentzian branch.
It does not yet derive the existence of fermions from the bare bosonic
condensate, nor does it derive fermionic quantisation, anticommutation
relations, or the full spin-statistics theorem.
Nor does the present subsection derive the observed fermion spectrum,
generation structure, or Yukawa hierarchy.
Those questions belong to the broader fermionic and quantum
programmes developed in the following subsections and in
Section~\ref{sec:qs_statistics}.

\paragraph{Status summary.}
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{p{6.0cm}p{2.0cm}p{5.0cm}}
\hline
Statement & Status & Comment \\
\hline
Lorentzian quadratic invariant for free excitations
  on the ordered branch
  & A (EFT) & Kinematic input from the broken-phase
    propagation structure \\
Clifford algebra on the emergent metric
  & B & $\eta^{\mu\nu}$ is interpreted as the
    induced flat-limit metric \\
Dirac-type first-order equation via Clifford
  linearisation
  & B & Structural reconstruction once the
    spinorial and Lorentzian inputs are admitted \\
Connection to $S_{\rm ECT+f}$ variational
  structure
  & B & Euler--Lagrange equation of the extended
    action \\
Weyl decomposition $\leftrightarrow$
  $Spin(4)\simeq SU(2)_L\times SU(2)_R$
  & A/B & Group-theoretic (A); physical
    relevance (B) \\
Curved-background Dirac generalisation with an
  emergent vierbein/spin-connection interpretation
  & B & Structural interpretation; explicit
    derivation pending \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsection{Chirality and fermion interactions}
\label{sec:chirality}

\textit{Status: Level~B structural possibility.
The decomposition $Spin(4)\simeq SU(2)_L\times SU(2)_R$ together
with $O(4)\to O(3)$ SSB provides a structural setting in which an
asymmetry between the two chiral sectors can arise.
At the present stage this is a structural possibility, not a
derivation of the physical weak-interaction chirality.
Connection to gauge sector: Section~\ref{sec:gauge_summary}.}

\paragraph{The chirality question.}
One of the striking features of the Standard Model is the chiral nature
of the weak interaction: the $SU(2)_L$ gauge symmetry acts only on
left-handed fermions, while right-handed fermions are singlets under
the weak interaction.
Within ECT the question is whether this asymmetry has a structural
origin in the condensate geometry.

\paragraph{Why chirality belongs here.}
The issue of chirality enters ECT at an intermediate level between
pure group theory and full gauge phenomenology.
Section~\ref{sec:spinor_structure} establishes that spinorial
representations are compatible with the ordered branch, while
Section~\ref{sec:ew_embedding} discusses the electroweak-like gauge
embedding.
The present subsection isolates the specific question of whether the
condensate geometry can distinguish the two chiral sectors before the
full weak-interaction phenomenology is specified.

This subsection builds on the spinorial compatibility established in
Section~\ref{sec:spinor_structure} and on the Dirac-form
reconstruction of Section~\ref{sec:dirac_eq}.
The question addressed here is not whether spinors can be written on
the ordered branch, but whether the condensate geometry can
structurally bias the left- and right-chiral sectors differently.

\paragraph{From $Spin(4)$ to chiral asymmetry.}
As established in Section~\ref{sec:spinor_structure}, the Euclidean
spin group decomposes as
$Spin(4) \simeq SU(2)_L \times SU(2)_R$, and the Dirac spinor
$\Psi = (\psi_L, \psi_R)^T$ carries representations of both factors.
Before SSB, the two $SU(2)$ sectors are on an equal footing.

The formation of the condensate selects a preferred orientation
$n_A \equiv \partial_A\Phi/|\partial\Phi|$, leading to
$O(4) \to O(3)$.
Geometrically this corresponds to fixing one axis in
the four-dimensional space.
As a result, the symmetry between the two $SU(2)$ factors in the
decomposition of $Spin(4)$ is no longer exact.

\paragraph{Selection mechanism (Level~B structural argument).}
After the emergence of the Lorentzian regime via analytic continuation
$w \to -ic_*t$, the Euclidean rotation group $Spin(4)$ maps to the
Lorentzian spin group $SL(2,\mathbb{C})$.
In $SL(2,\mathbb{C})$, the two $SU(2)$ factors become the left-handed
and right-handed chirality sectors: $\psi_L$ and $\psi_R$ transform
under the $(1/2,0)$ and $(0,1/2)$ representations, respectively.

The condensate direction $n_A$ breaks the equivalence between the
two sectors because the analytic continuation treats the distinguished
$w$-direction differently from the three spatial directions.
If the gauge coupling is mediated through condensate-dependent
structures (as in the electroweak-like embedding of
Section~\ref{sec:ew_embedding}), then the geometric asymmetry of the
ordered branch may be reflected in a chiral asymmetry of the
fermion--gauge coupling.
At the present stage this should be read only as a structural route:
ECT does not yet derive which chirality sector is selected, nor the
detailed form of the weak-interaction vertex.

\paragraph{Connection to the fifth-force coupling.}
The ECT-specific deformation
$\Delta\mathcal{L} = \beta_5\,\bar\Psi\gamma^A n_A\Psi$
(Section~\ref{sec:fifth_force_fermion}) also breaks left--right
symmetry through the condensate direction $n_A$.
The fifth-force coupling and the chiral gauge selection therefore
share a common structural origin in the preferred direction of the
ordered branch.
Whether the two effects are dynamically related or merely share the
same geometric source remains open.

\paragraph{Fate of $SU(2)_R$.}
If the selection mechanism described above is realised, the
$SU(2)_R$ sector is not eliminated but effectively decoupled at low
energies.
Two possibilities remain structurally open within ECT:
\begin{itemize}
  \item $SU(2)_R$ is completely suppressed at all accessible
    energies, reproducing the Standard Model structure exactly;
  \item $SU(2)_R$ becomes operative at very high energies,
    leading to a left--right symmetric extension.
\end{itemize}
ECT does not currently distinguish between these possibilities.
This question belongs to the open fermionic programme summarised in
Section~\ref{sec:fermion_open}.

\paragraph{What is and is not derived here.}
The present subsection shows that the ECT condensate geometry
provides a \emph{structural setting} in which chiral asymmetry can
naturally arise: the decomposition
$Spin(4) \simeq SU(2)_L \times SU(2)_R$ plus SSB creates
a geometric distinction between the two chirality sectors.
It does not derive the physical weak interaction, nor does it
explain why $SU(2)_L$ (and not $SU(2)_R$) is selected, nor does it
derive the observed fermion representations under the electroweak
gauge group.
These questions belong to the open programme~(OP-chirality;
Section~\ref{sec:fermion_open}).

\paragraph{What would be needed for Level~A.}
To promote chirality from structural possibility to derivation, ECT
would need to show: (i)~how the condensate dynamics selects one
chiral sector rather than the other, (ii)~how the electroweak gauge
vertex acquires its observed chiral form, and (iii)~how the observed
fermion representation content follows from the same mechanism.
None of these steps has yet been completed.

\paragraph{Status summary.}
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{p{6.0cm}p{2.0cm}p{5.0cm}}
\hline
Statement & Status & Comment \\
\hline
$Spin(4) \simeq SU(2)_L \times SU(2)_R$
  decomposition
  & A & Double cover of $SO(4)$ \\
Analytic continuation maps the Euclidean spinorial
  structure to Lorentzian chiral sectors
  & B & Structural continuation argument \\
SSB $O(4)\to O(3)$ distinguishes a preferred
  direction and removes the pre-breaking symmetry
  between the two Euclidean $SU(2)$ factors
  & A/B & Geometric statement (A); relevance for
    physical chirality is interpretive (B) \\
The ordered condensate provides a setting in which
  chiral asymmetry can arise
  & B & Structural possibility, not derivation \\
Selection of $SU(2)_L$ as the physical weak factor
  & B/C & Requires additional dynamical input \\
Fermion--condensate coupling and chiral asymmetry
  share the same geometric source $n_A$
  & B & Common structural origin \\
Fate of $SU(2)_R$
  & Open & Suppressed or active only at higher
    energies \\
Full derivation of SM chirality from P1--P5
  & Open (OP-chirality) & \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsection{Yukawa couplings and fermion masses}
\label{sec:yukawa}

\textit{Status: Level~C phenomenological embedding.
Yukawa couplings and fermion masses are not derived from the ECT
condensate action; they are imposed as Standard-Model input.
The mass formula $m_f = y_f v_2$ has the same structure as in the SM,
up to the field-normalisation convention adopted for the embedded
electroweak sector.
Possible condensate-sector corrections are discussed in
Section~\ref{sec:ew_particles}.}

Once fermionic fields $\Psi$ are admitted as additional spinorial
degrees of freedom compatible with the ordered
branch~(Section~\ref{sec:spinor_structure}), their masses arise
from Yukawa couplings to the electroweak condensate sector:
\begin{equation}
  \mathcal{L}_{\rm Yukawa}^{\rm eff}
  \sim
  y_f\,\bar\psi_L\,\Phi_{\rm ew}\,\psi_R + \text{h.c.}
  \quad\Rightarrow\quad
  m_f = y_f\,v_2,
  \label{eq:yukawa_mass}
\end{equation}
where $\Phi_{\rm ew}$ denotes the electroweak-like condensate field of
the embedded low-energy sector, not the fundamental bare condensate
field $\Phi$ of the primary ECT action.
The Yukawa term is written schematically, at the level of the embedded
electroweak EFT.
Here $y_f$ is the Yukawa coupling constant for fermion species $f$,
and $v_2 \approx 246\,$GeV is the electroweak condensate
scale~(Section~\ref{sec:ew_embedding}).
This is the same mass formula as in the Standard Model.
This relation is written in the normalisation convention adopted for the
embedded electroweak sector in the present article; numerical factors
such as $1/\sqrt{2}$ depend on the field normalisation convention.
The coupling $y_f$ is not derived from condensate microphysics;
it is a free matching parameter~(Level~C).

\paragraph{Chiral structure of the Yukawa coupling.}
In the Standard Model, the Yukawa interaction couples left- and
right-chiral fermion sectors through the electroweak scalar,
schematically as
$y_f\,\bar\psi_L\,\Phi_{\rm ew}\,\psi_R + \text{h.c.}$
Within ECT, the distinction between $\psi_L$ and $\psi_R$ has a
structural background in the
$Spin(4)\simeq SU(2)_L \times SU(2)_R$ decomposition and in the
chirality discussion of Section~\ref{sec:chirality}.
This means that a left-right Yukawa bilinear is \emph{compatible with}
the ECT chirality framework.
However, neither the detailed electroweak representation assignments nor
the numerical Yukawa couplings are derived from condensate dynamics:
the Yukawa sector remains an external low-energy embedding.

\paragraph{Yukawa hierarchy.}
The observed fermion masses span more than five orders of magnitude
(from the electron mass $m_e \approx 0.511\,$MeV to the top quark
mass $m_t \approx 173\,$GeV), corresponding to Yukawa couplings
from $y_e \approx 2 \times 10^{-6}$ to $y_t \approx 0.99$.
ECT does not explain this hierarchy.
Any mechanism that would derive the Yukawa pattern from condensate
dynamics would require understanding the internal structure of the
fermionic sector at a level not currently available in the
theory~(OP-Yukawa).

\paragraph{Three fermion generations.}
The Standard Model contains exactly three generations of quarks and
leptons.
ECT does not derive or explain this number.
The generation structure must be imposed as external input at the
present stage~(Level~C; OP-generations).

\paragraph{Possible condensate-sector corrections.}
In the three-scale picture of ECT
(Section~\ref{sec:three_scales}), the primary condensate amplitude
$v_0 \sim \bar{M}_{\rm Pl}$ is separated from the electroweak scale
$v_2 \approx 246\,$GeV by many orders of magnitude.
If the two condensate sectors are not perfectly decoupled,
higher-dimension operators suppressed by the primary condensate scale
(or by the corresponding primary radial threshold) could induce small
corrections to the low-energy Yukawa relations.
After electroweak condensation these corrections would typically be
organised as powers of $v_2/v_0$ in the effective expansion.
This possibility is discussed qualitatively in
Section~\ref{sec:ew_particles}; at the present stage ECT does not
predict the size of such effects~(Level~C).

\paragraph{Connection to the neutrino sector.}
In the present ECT formulation, neutrino masses are treated through a
separate embedded mechanism beyond the minimal charged-fermion Yukawa
structure; the seesaw-type discussion is given in
Section~\ref{sec:neutrino_particles}.

\paragraph{What is and is not derived here.}
This subsection imposes the Standard-Model Yukawa structure as
external input.
ECT provides the geometric and chirality framework
(Sections~\ref{sec:spinor_structure}--\ref{sec:chirality}) within
which the Yukawa coupling can be consistently written, but does not
derive any aspect of the fermion mass spectrum from first principles.
The Yukawa hierarchy, the generation structure, and the numerical
values of $y_f$ remain open problems.

\paragraph{Status summary.}
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{p{6.0cm}p{2.0cm}p{5.0cm}}
\hline
Statement & Status & Comment \\
\hline
Mass formula $m_f = y_f v_2$
  & C & SM embedding; $y_f$ not derived \\
Chiral structure of Yukawa coupling compatible
  with the ECT chirality framework
  & B/C & Structural consistency only;
    representation content and couplings not
    derived \\
Yukawa hierarchy
  & Open (OP-Yukawa) & No ECT mechanism identified \\
Three fermion generations
  & Open (OP-generations) & No ECT mechanism identified \\
Condensate-sector corrections to $y_f$
  & C & Possible in principle; size not
    predicted \\
Neutrino mass mechanism
  & C & Seesaw-type embedding;
    Section~\ref{sec:neutrino_particles} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsection{ECT-specific fermion--condensate coupling}
\label{sec:fifth_force_fermion}

\textit{Status: Level~B/C.
The fifth-force coupling is the main ECT-specific fermion interaction
currently identified beyond the Standard Model embedding.
Its strength is a dimensional estimate; full phenomenology is
developed in Section~\ref{sec:fifth_force}.}

This subsection introduces the structural form and status of the
ECT-specific fermion--condensate coupling.
Its detailed phenomenology, observational constraints, and
falsification tests are developed separately in
Section~\ref{sec:fifth_force}.

The symmetry breaking $O(4)\to O(3)$ introduces a preferred
condensate direction $n_A \equiv \partial_A\Phi/|\partial\Phi|$ in
the effective low-energy theory.
A leading rotationally invariant deformation of the Dirac operator
compatible with the ordered branch is:
\begin{equation}
  \Delta\mathcal{L} = \beta_5\,\bar\Psi\gamma^A n_A\Psi,
  \qquad
  \beta_5 \sim \frac{m_f}{\bar{M}_{\rm Pl}}
  \quad\text{(dimensional estimate)}.
  \label{eq:fifth_force_fermion}
\end{equation}
This scaling should be read as a dimensional estimate within the
low-energy EFT, not as a first-principles derivation from the
microscopic condensate action.

In the exact Lorentz-symmetric low-energy limit this operator would be
absent.
Its presence therefore parameterises a residual sensitivity of the
fermion sector to the preferred condensate direction.

This coupling introduces preferred-direction effects in the ordered
branch.
Its main structural features are:
\begin{itemize}
  \item it introduces preferred-direction corrections to the emergent
    Lorentz-invariant fermion sector through the condensate direction
    $n_A$;
  \item it is absent in the minimal Standard Model fermion sector and
    therefore represents a genuinely ECT-specific deformation of the
    low-energy Dirac operator;
  \item its strength is expected to scale with the fermion mass through
    the dimensional estimate $\beta_5 \sim m_f/\bar{M}_{\rm Pl}$.
\end{itemize}
As discussed in Section~\ref{sec:chirality}, this coupling shares
a common geometric source with the chiral-selection problem:
both are controlled by the preferred condensate direction $n_A$ of
the ordered branch.

The detailed phenomenology of this operator---including spin precession,
equivalence-principle tests, and astrophysical constraints---is
developed in Section~\ref{sec:fifth_force}.
The falsification entry F4 in Table~\ref{tab:ECT_predictions}
summarises the main experimental tests.

\paragraph{What is and is not derived here.}
The operator form $\beta_5\,\bar\Psi\gamma^A n_A\Psi$ is introduced as
an allowed low-energy deformation of the Dirac operator on the ordered
branch.
Its presence is structurally motivated by the preferred condensate
direction $n_A$, but neither the operator coefficient $\beta_5$ nor
its exact symmetry origin has been derived from the microscopic
condensate dynamics.
At present, the estimate $\beta_5 \sim m_f/\bar{M}_{\rm Pl}$ should be
read as phenomenological dimensional scaling.

\paragraph{Status summary.}
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{p{6.0cm}p{2.0cm}p{5.0cm}}
\hline
Statement & Status & Comment \\
\hline
Operator form $\beta_5\bar\Psi\gamma^A n_A\Psi$
  as an allowed low-energy deformation
  & B & Structural EFT operator on the ordered
    branch \\
Coupling strength $\beta_5 \sim m_f/\bar{M}_{\rm Pl}$
  & B/C & Dimensional estimate only \\
Common geometric source with chiral asymmetry
  & B & Both controlled by $n_A$ \\
Full $\beta_5$ derivation from condensate dynamics
  & Open & Section~\ref{sec:fermion_open} \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsection{Spin-statistics: structural route and open problem}
\label{sec:fermion_statistics}

\textit{Status: Level~B structural route.
ECT reproduces the correct topological dichotomy of exchange sectors
for identical excitations in three spatial dimensions and provides a
structural route toward the usual spin-statistics linkage.
The full spin-statistics theorem is not derived.
Full quantum-sector development: Section~\ref{sec:qs_statistics}.}

\paragraph{Why spin-statistics enters the fermionic section.}
Sections~\ref{sec:spinor_structure}--\ref{sec:dirac_eq} established
that spinorial representations are compatible with the ordered
branch and that a Dirac-type equation can be constructed.
However, the existence of a spinorial field representation does not
by itself fix the quantum exchange law of the corresponding
excitations.
The question of why half-integer-spin fields are associated with
antisymmetric exchange belongs to the broader quantum programme,
but it is stated here because it directly concerns the physical
interpretation of the fermionic fields introduced in this section.

\paragraph{Two distinct $\mathbb{Z}_2$ structures.}
It is important to distinguish two different $\mathbb{Z}_2$ facts
that arise in different contexts:
\begin{itemize}
  \item $\pi_1(SO(3)) = \mathbb{Z}_2$
    (Section~\ref{sec:spinor_structure}): this is the topological
    fact that makes spinorial representations available through the
    double cover of the connected rotation group;
  \item $\pi_1(\mathcal{M}_2) = \mathbb{Z}_2$
    (this subsection): this is the topology of exchange classes for
    two identical excitations in three spatial dimensions.
\end{itemize}
The first concerns \emph{single-particle representation theory};
the second concerns \emph{many-body exchange topology}.
Spinorial transformation law and fermionic exchange law are distinct
structures, even though they are linked in relativistic quantum theory.
The spin-statistics problem is precisely the problem of linking
these two structures.

\paragraph{Exchange topology.}
Here $\mathcal{M}_2$ denotes the reduced configuration space of two
identical excitations in three spatial dimensions, obtained after
removing coincidence points and quotienting by particle exchange.
Its fundamental group is
\begin{equation}
  \pi_1(\mathcal{M}_2) = \mathbb{Z}_2,
\end{equation}
which yields exactly two exchange classes: symmetric and
antisymmetric.
Because the ordered branch is effectively $3+1$-dimensional, this
topological result supports only the usual bosonic/fermionic
dichotomy; anyonic braid statistics is not expected for fundamental
particle exchange in the primary branch.

Integer-spin tensorial sectors are compatible with symmetric
exchange; half-integer spinorial sectors with antisymmetric exchange.
ECT therefore reproduces the correct topological dichotomy and
provides a structural route toward the standard spin-statistics
linkage.
This is not yet the full Pauli--L\"uders--Zumino theorem, but it is
the correct structural compatibility statement for the ordered
branch~(Level~B).
The fuller quantum-sector development is given in
Section~\ref{sec:qs_statistics}.

\paragraph{What ECT does not yet derive.}
ECT does not yet derive:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item the full relativistic spin-statistics theorem;
  \item the full operator anti-commutation algebra
    $\{\hat a_i,\hat a_j^\dagger\}=\delta_{ij}$;
  \item the complete many-body fermionic Fock-space construction
    from bare condensate dynamics.
\end{enumerate}
Within the fermionic sector, these questions belong to the open
programme summarised in Section~\ref{sec:fermion_open}
(Open; OP-Q11).

\paragraph{Status summary.}
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{p{6.0cm}p{2.0cm}p{5.0cm}}
\hline
Statement & Status & Comment \\
\hline
$\pi_1(SO(3)) = \mathbb{Z}_2$: spinorial
  representations available through the double
  cover
  & A & Single-particle representation topology \\
$\pi_1(\mathcal{M}_2) = \mathbb{Z}_2$: two
  exchange classes
  & A & Exchange topology in 3 spatial
    dimensions \\
Bosonic/fermionic dichotomy for fundamental
  exchange in the primary branch
  & A/B & Topological dichotomy (A); physical
    interpretation (B) \\
Half-integer representations compatible with
  antisymmetric exchange
  & B & Structural compatibility, not full
    theorem \\
Anyonic braid statistics absent for fundamental
  particle exchange in the primary branch
  & B & Ordered branch is effectively
    $3+1$-dimensional \\
Full spin-statistics theorem
  & Open (OP-Q11) & Developed further in
    Section~\ref{sec:qs_statistics} \\
Operator anti-commutation algebra
  & Open (OP-Q11) & Not yet derived \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\subsection{Open programme and status summary for the fermionic sector}
\label{sec:fermion_open}

\textit{Status: this subsection collects the open problems and the
joint status summary for the fermionic
sector~(Sections~\ref{sec:spinor_structure}--\ref{sec:fermion_statistics}).
The fermionic sector is among the most heavily embedded parts of ECT:
its content is introduced through structural reconstruction on the ordered
branch rather than derived from the bare bosonic condensate.}

\paragraph{Open programme.}
To promote the fermionic sector from structural reconstruction to
first-principles derivation, the following programme would need to be
completed:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item derive the dynamical origin of spinorial fields from the
    bare bosonic condensate, including their fully induced
    vierbein/spin-connection coupling on non-uniform condensate
    backgrounds (currently: external input;
    Section~\ref{sec:spinor_structure});
  \item derive fermionic statistics beyond the structural
    $\pi_1(\mathcal{M}_2) = \mathbb{Z}_2$ exchange-topology route
    (currently: Level~B compatibility;
    Section~\ref{sec:fermion_statistics},
    Section~\ref{sec:qs_statistics});
  \item derive the Yukawa coupling pattern and the fermion mass
    hierarchy (currently: SM input, Level~C;
    Section~\ref{sec:yukawa});
  \item derive the fifth-force coupling $\beta_5$ beyond the
    dimensional estimate (currently: Level~B/C;
    Section~\ref{sec:fifth_force_fermion},
    Section~\ref{sec:fifth_force});
  \item derive the chiral gauge selection $SU(2)_L$ dynamically
    (currently: Level~B structural possibility;
    Section~\ref{sec:chirality});
  \item derive the origin of exactly three fermion generations
    (currently: no mechanism identified;
    Section~\ref{sec:yukawa}).
\end{enumerate}
None of these steps has been completed.
The fermionic sector is a structural reconstruction programme, not
a completed derivation from the bare scalar condensate.

\paragraph{What would Level~A look like?}
A hypothetical Level~A fermionic sector would mean that spinorial
excitations emerge as dynamical quasiparticles of the bare bosonic
condensate, with their statistics, mass spectrum, and gauge quantum
numbers determined by condensate microphysics.
An instructive analogy is provided by emergent fermionic quasiparticles
in systems such as superfluid ${}^3$He-A~\cite{Volovik2003}, although
ECT does not currently provide a comparable microscopic mechanism.
The structural route developed in this section should be understood as
the present limit of what the framework can establish for fermions.
A genuine Level~A completion would require qualitatively new input
beyond what is currently available in ECT.

\paragraph{Connections to the broader ECT programme.}
Several fermionic open problems are shared with other sectors of the
theory:
\begin{itemize}
  \item the spin-statistics question~(ii) connects to the quantum
    sector~(Section~\ref{sec:qs_statistics}; OP-Q11);
  \item the fifth-force phenomenology~(iv) connects to the
    phenomenological and macroscopic sectors
    (Section~\ref{sec:fifth_force});
  \item the chiral selection~(v) connects to the electroweak
    embedding~(Section~\ref{sec:ew_embedding});
  \item the generation puzzle~(vi) remains without a connection to
    any other part of ECT at the present stage.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Joint status summary.}
The table below gives a condensed overview.
Detailed status tables are provided in each subsection.

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{p{4.5cm}p{2.0cm}p{6.5cm}}
\hline
Subsection & Highest level & Summary \\
\hline
\ref{sec:spinor_structure}~Spinor structure
  & A (group theory) / B (physical motivation)
  & $Spin(4)\simeq SU(2)_L\times SU(2)_R$; spinorial reps
    available; fermions not derived from bare condensate \\
\ref{sec:dirac_eq}~Dirac form
  & B
  & Structural reconstruction via Clifford linearisation on
    the emergent metric; connection to $S_{\rm ECT+f}$ \\
\ref{sec:chirality}~Chirality
  & B (structural possibility)
  & SSB provides a structural setting in which chiral asymmetry
    can arise; selection of $SU(2)_L$ is not derived \\
\ref{sec:yukawa}~Yukawa / masses
  & C
  & SM embedding; hierarchy and three generations
    open~(OP-Yukawa, OP-generations) \\
\ref{sec:fifth_force_fermion}~Fifth-force coupling
  & B/C
  & $\beta_5\bar\Psi\gamma^A n_A\Psi$; dimensional estimate;
    phenomenology in Section~\ref{sec:fifth_force} \\
\ref{sec:fermion_statistics}~Spin-statistics
  & A (topology) / B (compatibility)
  & $\pi_1(\mathcal{M}_2)=\mathbb{Z}_2$; for point-particle
    exchange in $3{+}1$ dimensions this allows only
    bosonic or fermionic exchange classes;
    full theorem open~(OP-Q11) \\
\hline
\multicolumn{3}{l}{\textit{Overall}} \\
Fermions as collective condensate excitations
  & Open
  & Not derived from bare bosonic action \\
Full first-principles fermionic sector from P1--P5
  & Open
  & Structural reconstruction only \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\paragraph{Overall assessment.}
At the present stage, the fermionic sector of ECT is best viewed as a
coherent structural programme rather than a completed derivation.
Its internal logic is more structured than a purely ad hoc SM
embedding, because spinor structure, Dirac form, chirality, and the
ECT-specific fermion--condensate coupling are all tied to the ordered
branch geometry.
At the same time, none of the decisive microscopic steps---fermion
emergence, statistics, Yukawa pattern, or generation structure---has
yet been derived from the bare condensate action.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%



\section{Gauge Interactions and a Possible Fifth Force in ECT}
\label{sec:fifth_force}

In the ECT framework the known gauge interactions of the Standard Model
can be interpreted as different symmetry sectors of the condensate
field.  In addition to the familiar gauge interactions, the structure
of the condensate naturally allows an additional coupling between
fermions and the condensate orientation $n_A \equiv \partial_A\Phi/|\partial\Phi|$
that emerges after the symmetry breaking $O(4)\rightarrow O(3)$.

This interaction represents a candidate for a new, extremely weak
``fifth force'' whose strength is suppressed by the Planck scale.

\subsection{Electroweak Interaction}

The electroweak-like sector is discussed in Section~7 as a
\emph{structural embedding} of an $SU(2)\times U(1)$ condensate EFT.
At the current stage this should be read conditionally:
if a second condensate transition is realised in ECT, then low-energy
matching fixes its scale to
\begin{equation}
  v_2 = 246\,\mathrm{GeV}.
\end{equation}
This does \emph{not} mean that the second transition, its gauge
localisation, or the full physical $W^\pm,Z,\gamma$ sector have already
been derived from the minimal ECT postulates.
Rather, the electroweak embedding provides a phenomenologically correct
IR target whose condensate origin remains an open problem
(OP-EW; Section~\ref{sec:three_scales}, Appendix~\ref{app:second_transition}).

\subsection{Strong Interaction}

The strong interaction corresponds to a color-triplet sector of the
condensate with symmetry group $SU(3)$.

The associated gauge fields

\begin{equation}
G_\mu^a , \qquad a=1,\dots,8
\end{equation}

represent the gluons.

Unlike the electroweak case the $SU(3)$ symmetry is not broken,

\begin{equation}
\langle \Phi_{\text{color}} \rangle = 0 ,
\end{equation}

so the condensate remains in the symmetric phase.

This naturally explains why gluons remain massless while quarks are
confined.  A full derivation of confinement within the ECT framework
requires a nonperturbative analysis analogous to that used in lattice
QCD.

\subsection{Fermion Masses}

\textbf{Status (Level~C).}  The Yukawa coupling $y$ and the SM gauge
structure are assumed from the Standard-Model embedding; they are not
derived from the bare condensate action.  The fermion mass formula below
is a consistency statement, not a prediction.

Fermions acquire masses through Yukawa couplings to the electroweak
condensate,

\begin{equation}
\mathcal{L}_{Y}
=
y\,\bar{\Psi}\Phi\Psi .
\end{equation}

After symmetry breaking

\begin{equation}
m_f = y v_2 .
\end{equation}

The smallness of the electron mass

\begin{equation}
y_e \sim 2\times10^{-6}
\end{equation}

remains an open hierarchy problem, as in the Standard Model.

\subsection{A Possible Fifth Interaction}

The presence of a preferred condensate direction $n_A$ allows a
direct coupling between fermions and the condensate background.

The simplest interaction term is

\begin{equation}
\mathcal{L}_5
=
\beta \,
\bar{\Psi}\gamma^A n_A \Psi .
\end{equation}

In the broken-symmetry phase

\begin{equation}
n_A=(0,0,0,1),
\end{equation}

so the interaction reduces to

\begin{equation}
\mathcal{L}_5
=
\beta\,
\bar{\Psi}\gamma^w \Psi .
\end{equation}

Physically this represents a direct coupling between fermions and the
temporal orientation of the condensate.

\subsection{Strength and Range}

Dimensional arguments suggest that the coupling constant is suppressed
by the Planck scale,

\begin{equation}
\beta \sim \frac{m}{M_{Pl}} .
\end{equation}

For electrons this gives

\begin{equation}
\beta_e \sim 2\times10^{-22}.
\end{equation}

The interaction range is controlled by the condensate correlation
length.

For a homogeneous condensate

\begin{equation}
\xi_{\text{cond}} \sim \ell_{Pl},
\end{equation}

making the interaction extremely short-range.

However, on galactic scales the condensate becomes spatially
structured, with a characteristic scale

the effective range of the scalar sector extends to galactic
scales ($\sim$kpc), controlled by the $\phi$-field dynamics
(Section~\ref{sec:mp_galactic}).
In this regime the interaction may produce observable macroscopic
effects.

\subsection{Symmetry Properties}


\paragraph{Important distinction: microscopic fifth force
vs.\ macroscopic $\phi$-closure.}

The macroscopic ordered-branch amplitude variable $\phi$ produces two
observationally distinct effects that must not be conflated:
\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{Microscopic fifth force}
  ($\mathcal{L}_5 = \beta\,\bar\Psi\gamma^A n_A\Psi$,
  $\beta = m_f/\bar{M}_{\rm Pl}$):
  a perturbative, Planck-suppressed additional interaction.
  It introduces preferred-direction corrections to the emergent
  Lorentz-invariant fermion sector.
  It is detectable via spin precession
  ($\omega_5 \sim 10^{-10}$--$10^{-15}\,$rad/s, model-dependent) and
  equivalence-principle tests ($\eta \sim 10^{-15}$).
  It is \emph{far too weak} to affect galactic rotation
  curves.
\item \textbf{Macroscopic $\phi$-closure}
  ($G_{\rm eff}(X) = G_N\,e^{-\beta\phi}$,
  $\mu(g/g_\dagger)\,g = g_{\rm bar}$):
  a \emph{nonperturbative} modification of the gravitational
  coupling itself.
  In the critical branch ($g_{\rm bar} \ll g_\dagger$) the
  effect is $O(1)$ and produces flat rotation curves.
  This is modified gravity, not a fifth force.
\end{itemize}
Both effects originate from the same $\phi$-field, but they
operate in fundamentally different regimes: the fifth force is
a linear perturbation with coupling $\sim m/M_{\rm Pl}$,
while the galactic $\phi$-closure enters a nonlinear critical
branch where the modification of $G_{\rm eff}$ is large.

The fifth interaction possesses several distinctive features:

\begin{itemize}

\item preferred-direction corrections to the emergent Lorentz-invariant
fermion sector through the condensate direction $n_A$,

\item apparent Lorentz-symmetry breaking through the presence of
$n_A \equiv \partial_A\Phi/|\partial\Phi|$, the orientational variable
of the condensate gradient,

\item universal coupling to fermions proportional to their mass.

\end{itemize}

These properties distinguish it from all known Standard Model
interactions.

\paragraph{Connection to the macroscopic amplitude variable.}
In the amplitude-sector formulation
(Section~\ref{sec:mp_galactic}), the scalar-mediated
correction to gravity is controlled by gradients of
$\phi=\frac{1}{\beta}\ln(u/u_\infty)$, i.e.\ by the logarithmic macroscopic amplitude
variable of the ordered branch, rather than by unscreened microscopic
condensate modes.  This immediately explains why the fifth
force is strongly suppressed in dense environments yet can
become relevant in diffuse systems: matter drives the vacuum
deeper into the Lorentzian phase, raises the local effective
mass of the order field, and thereby screens the scalar sector.
ECT therefore predicts that any residual fifth-force signal
should correlate with the ambient ordered-branch amplitude background
and local environment rather than with baryonic mass alone.

\subsection{Experimental Signatures}

Several possible experimental tests may probe the existence of this
interaction.

\paragraph{Spin precession}

The coupling induces an additional spin-dependent energy shift

\begin{equation}
\omega_5
\sim
\frac{\beta m_e c^2}{\hbar}.
\end{equation}

For the bare Planck-suppressed coupling $\beta \sim m_e/M_{\rm Pl}$
this gives $\omega_5 \sim 0.1\,\mathrm{rad\,s^{-1}}$, which is 
already excluded by atomic clock experiments.  However, the 
physically relevant coupling on galactic scales involves the 
\emph{local condensate gradient} rather than the Planck-scale 
amplitude, introducing an additional suppression that is expected 
to reduce $\omega_5$ to experimentally interesting levels
($\sim 10^{-10}$--$10^{-15}\,\mathrm{rad\,s^{-1}}$).
A precise prediction requires a calculation of the local 
ordered-branch amplitude profile, which is left for future work.

\paragraph{Violation of the equivalence principle}

Because different fermions have different values of $\beta$,
test bodies composed of different particles may experience slightly
different gravitational accelerations.

The corresponding Eötvös parameter is approximately

\begin{equation}
\eta
\sim
\frac{|\beta_e-\beta_p|}{g}
\sim 10^{-20}\text{--}10^{-21}.
\end{equation}

This bare estimate is far below current experimental limits
($\eta < 10^{-13}$ from the MICROSCOPE mission \cite{MICROSCOPE2022}).
However, collective condensate effects on galactic scales may 
significantly enhance the effective EP violation; quantifying 
this enhancement from the $\phi$-sector dynamics is an open 
problem (Section~\ref{sec:open_problems}).

\paragraph{Polarization-dependent speed of light}

Photon propagation in the condensate background may depend weakly on
polarization relative to $n_A$,

\begin{equation}
\frac{\delta c}{c}
\sim
\beta^2
\sim
\left(\frac{m_e}{M_{Pl}}\right)^2
\sim
10^{-45}.
\end{equation}

Although currently unobservable, this provides an extremely stringent
constraint on the parameter space of the theory.



\paragraph{Consistency with existing bounds.}
All quantitative estimates in this section are order-of-magnitude and
serve to identify the relevant parametric suppressions (in particular the
Planck suppression $\beta\sim m_f/M_{\rm Pl}$). A systematic comparison to
the full set of laboratory, atomic-clock, spin-precession, and
equivalence-principle constraints requires an explicit mapping of the
effective operator $\bar{\Psi}\gamma^A n_A\Psi$ onto standard
Lorentz-violation frameworks and will be presented elsewhere.



\subsection{Origin of the Fifth Interaction Coupling}

\textit{Status: Level~B dimensional analysis. The Planck suppression
$\beta\sim m_f/\bar{M}_{\rm Pl}$ follows from the UV scale matching;
the fermion mass $m_f$ enters as an external input, not derived from ECT.}

After the first SSB $O(4)\to O(3)$, the ordered branch introduces
the derived orientation variable $n_A=\partial_A\Phi/|\partial\Phi|$
(not an independent field; Section~\ref{sec:sector_map}).
Fermions propagating in this ordered-branch background can couple
to $n_A$ through the leading symmetry-allowed operator:
\begin{equation}
  \mathcal{L}_5 = \frac{1}{u_0}\,\bar{\Psi}\gamma^A n_A \Psi,
\end{equation}
where $u_0\sim\bar{M}_{\rm Pl}$ is the UV condensate amplitude
(Section~\ref{sec:three_scales}, equation~\eqref{eq:u0_Mpl}).

Fermion masses enter through the electroweak-like sector.
If the second condensate transition exists~(OP-EW), fermions
acquire masses via Yukawa coupling to the second condensate:
\begin{equation}
  m_f = y_f\,v_2,
\end{equation}
where $v_2\approx246$\,GeV is the electroweak matching
scale~\eqref{eq:v2_GF} and $y_f$ is an external SM input.
The effective fifth-force coupling then becomes
\begin{equation}
  \beta \sim \frac{m_f}{u_0} \sim \frac{m_f}{\bar{M}_{\rm Pl}},
  \label{eq:fifth_coupling}
\end{equation}
giving
\begin{equation}
  \boxed{\beta \sim \frac{m_f}{\bar{M}_{\rm Pl}}}
  \qquad\text{(Level~B: dimensional analysis).}
\end{equation}
For electrons: $\beta_e\sim m_e/\bar{M}_{\rm Pl}\sim2\times10^{-22}$.

The fifth interaction is therefore naturally Planck-suppressed for
all known particles, consistent with all current experimental bounds.
Two caveats must be noted: (i) the fermion masses $m_f$ are
SM-matched inputs, not derived from ECT; (ii) the electroweak
scale $v_2$ is a matching constraint, not a first-principles
result~(Section~\ref{sec:three_scales}).

\subsection{Fifth Interaction and the Lorentz-Order Weak-Field Sector}

A central empirical fact of galactic dynamics is the existence of a
characteristic weak-field scale of order
\begin{equation}
g_\dagger \sim 10^{-10}\ \mathrm{m\,s^{-2}},
\end{equation}
which controls the transition between Newtonian behaviour and the
deep-critical branch visible in the radial acceleration relation.

In ECT this scale is introduced through the macroscopic amplitude variable
\begin{equation}
\phi = \frac{1}{\beta}\ln\!\left(\frac{u}{u_\infty}\right),
\end{equation}
whose ambient background $\phi_{\rm env}$ determines the effective
critical scale $g_\dagger(\phi_{\rm env})$.

\paragraph{Two distinct weak-field effects.}
ECT contains two conceptually distinct ingredients:
\begin{enumerate}
\item a direct fermion coupling to the preferred condensate direction,
\begin{equation}
\mathcal{L}_5=\beta\,\bar\Psi\gamma^A n_A\Psi,
\qquad
\beta \sim \frac{m_f}{M_{Pl}},
\end{equation}
which is Planck-suppressed and relevant to spin-precession or
equivalence-principle tests;
\item a collective galactic-scale modification of the weak-field branch,
governed by the Lorentz-amplitude-sector closure relation
\begin{equation}
\mu_n\!\left(\frac{g_{\rm obs}}{g_\dagger(\phi_{\rm env})}\right)
g_{\rm obs}
=
g_{\rm bar}.
\label{eq:phi_closure_fifth_sector}
\end{equation}
\end{enumerate}

The first effect is a genuine fifth interaction in the particle-physics
sense. The second is not a new two-body force but a collective
vacuum-order response of the gravitational sector.

\paragraph{Deep-critical branch.}
For $g_{\rm obs}\gg g_\dagger(\phi_{\rm env})$, one recovers the
Newtonian regime. For $g_{\rm obs}\ll g_\dagger(\phi_{\rm env})$,
eq.~\eqref{eq:phi_closure_fifth_sector} reduces to
\begin{equation}
g_{\rm obs}\approx \sqrt{g_{\rm bar}\,g_\dagger(\phi_{\rm env})},
\end{equation}
which reproduces the algebraic deep-MOND form as the low-acceleration
limit of the amplitude-sector branch.

\paragraph{Interpretive status.}
The present use of
eq.~\eqref{eq:phi_closure_fifth_sector}
should be understood as an exploratory algebraic closure of the
amplitude-sector weak-field closure.
A first-principles derivation of the effective transition function
$\mu_n$, together with a full boundary-value solution for $\phi(x)$ in
realistic galactic geometry, remains an open problem.


\subsection{Summary: Interactions in the ECT Framework}

Within the ECT framework the known fundamental interactions of the
Standard Model are \emph{compatible with} different symmetry sectors
of the condensate field (Level~B/C: SM gauge group embedded, not derived).

The electroweak-like interaction is currently treated as a
\emph{structural embedding} of an $SU(2)\times U(1)$ condensate EFT.
If a second condensate transition is realised in ECT, the breaking pattern
\begin{equation}
SU(2)\times U(1)\rightarrow U(1)_{\mathrm{EM}}
\end{equation}
would be associated with a scale fixed by electroweak low-energy matching:
\begin{equation}
v_2 = \left(\sqrt{2}\,G_F\right)^{-1/2} \approx 246\,\mathrm{GeV}.
\end{equation}
The physical $W^\pm$, $Z$ and photon fields and the Higgs-like radial mode
are not yet derived from the minimal ECT postulates; they represent the
correct IR target of the electroweak embedding~(OP-EW;
Section~\ref{sec:three_scales}, Appendix~\ref{app:second_transition}).

The strong interaction corresponds to an unbroken $SU(3)$ sector of the
condensate (Level~C: embedded, not derived); this accommodates the
eight gluon gauge fields.  A microscopic ECT derivation of confinement
from the bare condensate action remains open.

In addition to the Standard Model gauge sectors the symmetry breaking

\begin{equation}
O(4)\rightarrow O(3)
\end{equation}

introduces the derived orientation variable
$n_A \equiv \partial_A\Phi/|\partial\Phi|$
as a preferred direction in the condensate background
(not an independent microscopic field; Section~\ref{sec:sector_map}).
This allows a new interaction term

\begin{equation}
\mathcal{L}_5
=
\beta\,\bar{\Psi}\gamma^A n_A \Psi ,
\end{equation}

representing a direct coupling between fermions and the condensate
orientation.

The coupling constant follows from the hierarchy of scales in the
theory,

\begin{equation}
\beta \sim \frac{m_f}{M_{Pl}},
\end{equation}

making the interaction naturally Planck-suppressed for all known
particles.

Although extremely weak in laboratory conditions, this interaction
couples matter to spatial variations of the condensate field.
At galactic scales the $\phi$-sector develops a critical branch
whose characteristic acceleration scale is
\begin{equation}
g_\dagger(\phi_{\rm env}) \sim 10^{-10}\,\mathrm{m\,s^{-2}},
\end{equation}
coinciding with the empirical MOND/RAR scale.

In this picture the radial acceleration relation, flat rotation
curves, and the baryonic Tully--Fisher relation all arise from
the nonlinear response of the ordered-branch amplitude variable $\phi$ to
baryonic matter.  The critical acceleration
$g_\dagger(\phi_{\rm env})$ is controlled by the ambient
ordered-branch amplitude background, so that a single macroscopic
amplitude variable links several astrophysical phenomena.

The microscopic fifth interaction and the macroscopic $\phi$-sector
closure are both rooted in the same condensate structure, but they are
physically distinct regimes: the first is a genuine particle-physics
two-body coupling, while the second is a collective vacuum-order response
of the gravitational sector.
The same condensate framework therefore underlies both, even though
they should not be identified with each other.

Several observational tests may probe this sector of the theory:

\begin{itemize}

\item spin-dependent precession in precision atomic experiments,

\item violations of the equivalence principle at the level
$\eta \sim 10^{-15}$,

\item polarization-dependent propagation of electromagnetic waves,

\end{itemize}

Future high-precision measurements in both laboratory experiments and
astrophysical observations may therefore provide important constraints
on the existence of this interaction and on the underlying condensate
structure predicted by ECT.

\begin{figure*}[!htb]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/fig_fifth_force.png}

(a)~Microscopic fifth-force coupling strength
$\beta = m_f/\bar{M}_{\rm Pl}$: all values are
Planck-suppressed.  This coupling is too weak to
produce rotation curves; it is detectable only via
spin precession and equivalence-principle experiments.
(b)~Environmental modulation of $g_\dagger$: the
ambient $\phi_{\rm env}$ screens the critical branch
in dense environments.
(c)~The same $\phi$-field, in its nonperturbative
critical regime, modifies the effective gravitational
coupling $G_{\rm eff}(X) = G_N\,e^{-\beta\phi}$ and produces
rotation-curve enhancement (shown as $g_{\rm obs}/g_{\rm bar}$
for five SPARC galaxies).  This is \emph{not} the fifth
force of panel~(a) but the scalar-tensor modification
of gravity itself.
(d)~Fitted $\phi_{\rm env}$ values versus stellar mass.
\emph{Important distinction:} the microscopic fifth
force~(a) and the galactic $\phi$-closure~(c,d) arise
from the same ordered-branch amplitude variable but operate in
different regimes --- perturbative (Planck-suppressed)
versus nonperturbative (critical branch, $O(1)$ effect);
panels~(b)--(d) show its \emph{macroscopic manifestations}
through the ordered-branch amplitude variable.}
\label{fig:fifth_force}
\end{figure*}


\begin{figure*}[!htb]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/fig_fifth_force_scale.png}
\caption{Scale dependence of the $\phi$-closure gravitational modification (not the microscopic fifth force).
(a)~Relative strength of the condensate contribution 
the fractional gravity enhancement as a function of 
distance from the galaxy centre, for three representative galaxy 
types.  At high accelerations ($g_{\rm bar} \gg g_\dagger$) the $\phi$-effect
is negligible and Newtonian gravity dominates; at low accelerations
($g_{\rm bar} \ll g_\dagger$) the $\phi$-branch produces flat rotation
curves.  The transition occurs near $g_{\rm bar} \sim g_\dagger$, which is 
galaxy-dependent.
(b)~Acceleration budget for a Milky Way--type galaxy 
($M_{\rm bar} = 5.5\times10^{10}\,M_\odot$, $\phi_{\rm env} = +2.15$):
Newtonian gravity (dashed), ECT total including condensate

(dotted).  The horizontal line marks the empirical MOND 
acceleration scale $g^\dagger \approx 1.2\times10^{-10}$\,m/s$^2$.
The condensate force becomes comparable to Newtonian gravity

the fifth interaction is undetectable in laboratory experiments 
but manifests clearly on galactic scales.}

\subsection{ECT interaction architecture: five sectors with different status levels}
\label{sec:five_interactions}

\textit{Note on the title.
The previous formulation ``ECT predicts exactly five fundamental interactions''
overstated the current derivation status.
The correct statement is that ECT organises the interaction structure
into five sectors---but not all five have the same derivational standing.
This subsection now presents the interaction architecture with honest status labels.}

\paragraph{First: the five sectors.}
ECT naturally distinguishes five interaction sectors arising from
different aspects of the condensate structure:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item \textbf{Gravity} (Level~A/B): macroscopic condensate geometry and
    branch-variation response; the strongest derived interaction.
  \item \textbf{Electromagnetic-like $U(1)$ sector} (Level~B): coherent
    phase sector of the condensate; strongest gauge-embedding result.
  \item \textbf{Weak-interaction-like $SU(2)$ sector} (Level~B/C):
    structural embedding; requires second condensate transition~(OP-EW).
  \item \textbf{Strong-interaction-like $SU(3)$ sector} (Level~C):
    structural embedding only; colour origin not identified.
  \item \textbf{Microscopic fifth interaction} (Level~B): fermion--condensate
    orientation coupling; physically distinct from the macroscopic
    $\phi$-sector.
\end{enumerate}

\paragraph{Second: not all five are equally derived.}
This classification is not a theorem that the Standard Model gauge
group must emerge uniquely from the minimal ECT postulates.
In particular:
\begin{itemize}
  \item the exact SM gauge group $SU(3)_c\times SU(2)_L\times U(1)_Y$
    is an \emph{embedding input}, not a derivation~(OP-SM);
  \item gauge coupling constants, charge assignments, and fermionic
    representation content are not yet derived;
  \item the $SU(2)$-like sector requires a second transition whose
    dynamical origin remains open~(OP-EW).
\end{itemize}

\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{p{3.5cm}p{3.0cm}p{3.0cm}p{2.8cm}}
\hline
Interaction & Origin in ECT & Status & Key open issue \\
\hline
Gravity & Condensate geometry & Level~A/B & Nonlinear closure \\
Electromagnetic & Phase $U(1)$ sector & Level~B & Full QED derivation \\
Weak-like & $SU(2)$ embedding & Level~B/C & Second transition \\
Strong-like & $SU(3)$ embedding & Level~C & Colour origin \\
Fifth force & Orientation coupling & Level~B & Coupling derivation \\
\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\paragraph{Third: what ECT currently provides.}
ECT provides a \emph{five-sector interaction architecture} with a
nontrivial structural correspondence to the observed interaction pattern.
This is stronger than a loose analogy: the sectors arise from
qualitatively different aspects of the condensate (geometry,
phase, internal symmetry, direct coupling), and their relative
suppression scales can be organised consistently within
the condensate framework.
But it is weaker than a complete first-principles derivation
of the Standard Model plus gravity from P1--P5 alone.

\bigskip
\noindent
\textbf{Transition to Part~II.}
The preceding sections of Part~I have established the microscopic
foundations of ECT: the condensate postulates, the $O(4)\to O(3)$
symmetry-breaking mechanism, the emergence of Lorentzian geometry,
quantum dynamics, fundamental constants, the gravitational and gauge
sectors, and the particle spectrum.  All of these results emerge from
the single Euclidean action~\eqref{eq:ECT_action} at varying levels
of rigour (see Table~\ref{tab:ect-levels}), with SM gauge fields
structurally embedded rather than derived from the minimal bosonic action.

Part~II now turns to the macroscopic observable consequences.
On galactic scales the ECT condensate develops a macroscopic amplitude
variable of the ordered branch
$\phi=\frac{1}{\beta}\ln(u/u_\infty)$ that measures the local condensate amplitude
Lorentzianness of the vacuum.  This field couples geometrically to
baryonic matter and, in the critical infrared regime where the condensate
stiffness becomes nonlinear, produces MOND-like dynamics and the
baryonic Tully--Fisher relation as exact algebraic consequences.
On cosmological scales the condensate provides a natural mechanism
for inflation, dark energy, and possible condensate-sector excitations.
The observational outputs throughout Part~II remain linked to the same
underlying condensate framework, but in the present disciplined reading
the relevant parameters are no longer compressed into one legacy
notation layer.
Instead, macroscopic gravity is organised through the tensor stiffness
scale $M_G$, coherent quantum structure through the action scale $S_0$,
ordered-background structure through $u_0$, and galactic weak-field
phenomenology through the $\phi$-branch closure and the associated
scale $g_\dagger$.
This reflects an internal consistency structure of the framework,
not a single direct derivational chain from one condensate amplitude.
