which is structurally similar to $T_{AB}^{(n)}$ but with free coupling constants $c_{1..4}$.}\\
\end{longtable}


\subsubsection*{Summary}

The geometric branch of ECT requires a nonlinear completion.
The most developed currently available amplitude-dominated completion is
the $\phi$-first scalar-tensor closure
\eqref{eq:phi_first_action}--\eqref{eq:gen_EE},
whose homogeneous frozen-order limit reduces to standard Einstein
gravity with cosmological constant.
The full first-principles derivation of this nonlinear sector from
microscopic condensate dynamics remains open, but the EFT logic already
constrains the closure strongly.
This prepares the next stage: the weak-field limit and the emergence of
Newtonian and post-Newtonian gravity from the nonlinear geometric branch.


% ============================================================
\subsection{Weak-field and Newtonian limit}
\label{sec:mp_newtonian}
% ============================================================

\textit{Status: Level~A for the existence of a screened weak-field limit
recovering standard Newtonian gravity in the vacuum-normalised regime
$\phi\to 0$ with anisotropic corrections suppressed.
Level~B for the full weak-field phenomenology including $G_{\rm eff}(X)$,
amplitude-sector corrections, and the transition toward the galactic critical
branch.
This subsection prepares, but does not yet assume, the deep weak-field
critical regime developed in Section~\ref{sec:mp_galactic}.}

\medskip
The nonlinear closure of Section~\ref{sec:mp_nonlinear} establishes the
macroscopic field equations of the geometric branch.
The next task is to identify their weak-field limit and to show how
ordinary Newtonian gravity is recovered locally, while allowing
systematic departures at larger scales or lower accelerations.
This step is essential for two reasons: first, any viable macroscopic
closure must reproduce the well-tested Newtonian and post-Newtonian
regime in the Solar System; second, the same weak-field expansion
provides the entry point for the galactic phenomenology of
Section~\ref{sec:mp_galactic}.

\subsubsection*{Weak-field expansion of the geometric branch}

We consider the macroscopic branch in the regime:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:weak_field_expansion}
  g_{\mu\nu}=\eta_{\mu\nu}+h_{\mu\nu},
  \qquad |h_{\mu\nu}|\ll 1,
  \qquad |\phi|\ll 1,
  \qquad v\ll c_*,
\end{equation}
around the vacuum-normalised ordered background
$\phi=0$, $\bar{n}_A=(0,0,0,1)$, with the quasi-static approximation
for nonrelativistic sources:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:quasistatic}
  |\partial_t|\ll c_*|\nabla|,
  \qquad T_{00}\gg T_{0i},\,T_{ij}.
\end{equation}
In Newtonian gauge:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:newtonian_gauge}
  ds^2 = -(1+2\Psi)c^2dt^2 + (1-2\Phi)\delta_{ij}dx^idx^j.
\end{equation}
In pure GR with negligible anisotropic stress one has $\Phi=\Psi$.
In ECT this equality is recovered in the screened local regime;
small deviations appear when the order field or orientation corrections
become dynamically relevant.

\subsubsection*{Screened local limit: recovery of Newtonian gravity}

In the locally screened vacuum-normalised regime:
\[
\phi\simeq 0,\qquad \nabla\phi\simeq 0,\qquad \Theta_{\mu\nu}[n]\simeq 0.
\]
The generalised field equations~\eqref{eq:gen_EE} reduce to the
standard Poisson equation:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:Poisson_standard}
  \nabla^2\Psi = 4\pi G_N\rho_{\rm bar},
  \qquad \Phi=\Psi.
\end{equation}
In the same limit the post-Newtonian parameter:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:PPN_gamma}
  \gamma_{\rm PPN}\equiv\frac{\Phi}{\Psi}\simeq 1,
\end{equation}
as in GR, consistent with the Cassini bound
$|\gamma_{\rm PPN}-1|<2.3\times10^{-5}$~\cite{Bertotti2003}.

This recovery is the key local consistency requirement of ECT
Macroscopic Physics.
Its status is Level~A conditional on the screened vacuum-normalised weak
limit: once the geometric branch exists and local order variables are
frozen, ordinary Newtonian gravity follows.

\subsubsection*{Weak-field form of the $\phi$-first closure}

Outside the fully screened regime but still in the quasi-static
weak-field domain, the $\phi$-first closure leads to:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:mod_Poisson}
  \nabla^2\Psi(X)
  \simeq
  4\pi G_{\rm eff}(X)\,\rho_{\rm bar}(X)
  + \mathcal{D}_{\phi,n}(X),
  \qquad
  G_{\rm eff}(X) = G_N e^{-\beta\phi(X)},
\end{equation}
where $\mathcal{D}_{\phi,n}$ summarises residual derivative and
anisotropic source terms of the order field and orientation sector.
This is Level~B: the consistent weak-field reduction of the $\phi$-first
closure, not yet a first-principles theorem from bare~P3.

\paragraph{Observational bounds on $\dot G$.}
If the weak-field configuration evolves slowly in time:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:Gdot_over_G}
  \frac{\dot G_{\rm eff}}{G_{\rm eff}} = -\dot\phi.
\end{equation}
Lunar laser ranging gives $|\dot G/G|\lesssim 10^{-12}$\,yr$^{-1}$~\cite{Uzan2011},
requiring $|\dot\phi|\lesssim 10^{-12}$\,yr$^{-1}$, fully consistent
with a quasi-static screened condensate background~(Level~B).

\subsubsection*{Order-of-magnitude estimates for local deviations from GR}

Define the dimensionless deviation from the vacuum-normalised branch:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:epsilon_dev}
  \varepsilon(X)\equiv e^{-\phi(X)}-1\approx -\phi(X)
  \qquad\text{for }|\phi|\ll 1.
\end{equation}
Then one expects, at order of magnitude,
\[
\frac{\delta G}{G}\sim\varepsilon,
\]
while post-Newtonian deviations are likewise controlled by the same
small screening parameter.
Requiring consistency with Solar-System bounds motivates:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:epsilon_local_bound}
  |\varepsilon_{\rm local}|\lesssim 10^{-5},
  \qquad
  |\phi_{\rm local}|\lesssim 10^{-5},
\end{equation}
as a representative local screening condition.
The precise mapping to the full post-Newtonian parameter set remains
part of the detailed weak-field phenomenology and is therefore Level~B.

\subsubsection*{Two weak-field regimes of the geometric branch}

The new ECT architecture makes explicit that the weak-field domain
splits into two physically distinct subregimes:

\paragraph{(a) Screened local weak-field branch.}
$\phi\simeq 0$, $\Theta_{\mu\nu}\simeq 0$: GR/Newtonian physics is
recovered to high precision.
Relevant for Solar System and laboratory gravity.

\paragraph{(b) Deep weak-field ordered branch.}
The metric perturbation remains small, but order-sector corrections
become competitive with or dominant over the baryonic Newtonian source.
This is the regime in which $G_{\rm eff}(X)$ and the critical branch of
$\phi$-dynamics modify rotation curves, RAR, and BTFR phenomenology.
Developed in Section~\ref{sec:mp_galactic}.

This split is a real conceptual improvement over older ECT versions,
which treated ``the weak-field limit'' as a single regime.
The new postulate logic shows there are two weak-field regimes, separated
not by the size of the metric perturbation alone, but by the dynamical
state of the ordered branch.

\subsubsection*{Physical interpretation}

In ECT, a baryonic mass distribution sources a deformation of the
ordered macroscopic branch; the Newtonian potential is the screened local
projection of that deformation.
In the local branch the ordered medium responds almost rigidly, recovering
standard Newtonian gravity.
In the deep weak-field branch the medium is still weakly curved but no
longer fully screened, and residual order-sector structure becomes
visible.
This is why ECT remains compatible with Solar-System precision tests
while leaving room for nontrivial galactic phenomenology.

\subsubsection*{What is established and what is not}

\textbf{Established (Level~A in screened limit):}
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item A screened weak-field limit of the geometric branch exists.
  \item Standard Poisson gravity is recovered in that limit.
  \item The weak-field domain splits structurally into screened local
    and deep weak-field ordered regimes.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Level~B / open:}
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item Detailed form of $\mathcal{D}_{\phi,n}$ in all environments.
  \item Full post-Newtonian parameter set beyond $\gamma_{\rm PPN}$.
  \item First-principles microscopic derivation of screening
    from bare~P3.
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{Summary}

The weak-field limit of the geometric branch is not a single universal
regime but a hierarchy.
In the screened local branch ECT reproduces standard Newtonian gravity.
In the $\phi$-first weak-field branch the Newtonian potential obeys a
modified Poisson equation with both baryonic and order-sector
contributions.
This is the bridge between the nonlinear macroscopic closure and the
galactic critical branch of Section~\ref{sec:mp_galactic}.



% ============================================================
% ============================================================
\subsection{Gravitational waves and causal structure}
\label{sec:mp_gw}
% ============================================================

\textit{Status: GW propagation speed Level~A in broken-phase EFT;
polarisation content Level~B~(OP-gauge);
Planck-suppressed LIV correction estimate Level~A in EFT
power-counting once a propagation distance~$D$ is specified.}

\subsubsection*{Propagation speed}

From the massless tensor dispersion relation~\eqref{eq:n_eom_macro},
the GW propagation speed equals the effective causal speed of the
ordered branch:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:cgw}
  c_{\rm GW}=c_n=c_*
  \quad\text{(Level~A at the benchmark }\alpha=2\beta\text{)}.
\end{equation}
Consistent with $|c_{\rm GW}/c-1|<6\times10^{-15}$ from GW\,170817
and its electromagnetic counterpart~\cite{LIGO_Virgo_GW170817_2017}.

\subsubsection*{Planck-suppressed Lorentz-violation estimate}

Because Lorentz symmetry is emergent rather than fundamental in ECT,
the leading high-energy correction to GW propagation is
Planck-suppressed in the EFT:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:gw_liv_dispersion}
  \omega^2 = c_*^2 k^2
  \left[1+\xi_2\!\left(\frac{k}{M_{\rm LIV}}\right)^2
  + O\!\left(\frac{k^4}{M_{\rm LIV}^4}\right)\right],
\end{equation}
where $\xi_2\sim O(1)$ and $M_{\rm LIV}\sim\bar M_{\rm Pl}$.
Induced fractional group-velocity shift:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:gw_velocity_shift}
  \frac{\delta v_{\rm GW}}{c}
  \sim \xi_2\!\left(\frac{E_{\rm GW}}{M_{\rm LIV}}\right)^2.
\end{equation}
Arrival-time shift over propagation distance~$D$:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:LIV_gw}
  \Delta t_{\rm GW}
  \sim \frac{D}{c}\,\xi_2
    \!\left(\frac{E_{\rm GW}}{M_{\rm LIV}}\right)^2.
\end{equation}
For GW170817-like parameters ($D\sim40$\,Mpc,
$E_{\rm GW}\sim4\times10^{-13}$\,eV,
$M_{\rm LIV}\sim\bar M_{\rm Pl}$):
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:gw_liv_numeric}
  \Delta t_{\rm GW}\sim\xi_2\times10^{-64}\,\text{s}
  \quad\text{(negligible observationally)}.
\end{equation}

\subsubsection*{Polarisation content}

The orientation sigma-model provides three soft modes $\delta n_i$.
Full reduction to physical propagating content requires gauge analysis
(OP-gauge):
\begin{itemize}
  \item massless tensor propagation sector: Level~A;
  \item exact reduction to 2 TT polarisations: Level~B~(OP-gauge).
\end{itemize}
The anisotropic correction $\Theta_{\mu\nu}[n]$~\eqref{eq:Theta_n}
may additionally modify amplitude evolution in nontrivial ordered
backgrounds~(Level~B; OP-c1).

\subsubsection*{What is established and what is not}

\textbf{Established:}
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item $c_{\rm GW}=c_*$~(Level~A).
  \item LIV correction is Planck-suppressed; induced delay
    $\Delta t\lesssim10^{-64}$\,s is negligible.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Open:}
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item Exact 2 TT polarisations~(OP-gauge).
  \item Polarisation effects from $\Theta_{\mu\nu}[n]$~(OP-c1).
  \item Full radiative stability at loop level.
\end{enumerate}

% ============================================================
\subsection{Cosmological branch of the macroscopic sector}
\label{sec:mp_cosmo}
% ============================================================

\textit{Status: the whole subsection is Level~B unless stated
otherwise. The existence of a pre-Lorentzian Euclidean regime is a
structurally motivated cosmological scenario suggested by P1--P4, not
yet a completed first-principles cosmological theorem. $U(\phi)$
remains open~(OP3).}

\subsubsection*{A pre-Lorentzian Euclidean regime as a cosmological
scenario}

ECT naturally suggests a cosmological scenario in which the Universe
passes through a pre-Lorentzian regime before the ordered branch is
fully established.
In that regime there is no gradient-ordered vacuum sector, no effective
Lorentzian metric, and no preferred temporal direction.
This is strongly motivated by P1--P4, but the actual cosmological
realisation remains Level~B:
it is a structural scenario, not a theorem derived from completed
nonequilibrium condensate dynamics.
What ECT \emph{does} establish structurally is that macroscopic time is
not fundamental; therefore a cosmological history in which
time-bearing Lorentzian geometry appears only after the ordering
transition is natural in ECT in a way it is not in standard
GR-based cosmology.
The corresponding cosmological comparison is summarised in
Figure~\ref{fig:timeline}.
Unlike the earlier benchmark-era schematic, the present figure is
explicitly comparative: it contrasts the standard $\Lambda$CDM
history with the current ECT picture, while also indicating the
schematic evolution of the relevant condensate quantities.
The age difference between the standard $\Lambda$CDM history and
the observable/Lorentzian ECT history is meant to be read in this
figure.
The internal multi-sector organisation of ECT itself is shown later
in Figure~\ref{fig:ect_full_condensate_universe_evolution}.

pre-Lorentzian phase ($v_0=0$, no standard time coordinate).

\subsection*{Early-universe cosmological problems and their ECT resolution}
\label{sec:inflation_alternatives}

The three classical cosmological problems historically addressed by
inflation --- the horizon problem, the flatness problem, and the
relic/monopole problem --- each receive a natural ECT treatment that
operates at the level of branch emergence rather than through
manipulation of an already existing Lorentzian Universe.
In each case the ECT resolution shifts the question from a fine-tuning
or suppression problem inside a primordial spacetime to a structural
property of the pre-Lorentzian ordering stage.
A qualitative note on primordial perturbations follows in
Section~\ref{sec:primordial_perturbations_ectnote}.

\subsubsection*{Horizon problem as branch coherence in ECT}
\label{sec:horizon_ect_main}

The standard horizon problem is usually formulated inside an already
existing Lorentzian FLRW spacetime: why do widely separated regions of
the observed Universe appear so nearly homogeneous even though, in an
ordinary early Lorentzian history, they would not have been in mutual
causal contact?

In ECT this formulation is not fundamental.
The observable Lorentzian Universe is not taken as primordial.
Instead, the cosmological logic is:
\[
\text{pre-Lorentzian / Euclidean stage}
\;\longrightarrow\;
O(4)\to O(3)\ \text{ordering transition}
\;\longrightarrow\;
\text{emergent Lorentzian branch}.
\]
Accordingly, the horizon question must be reformulated.
In ECT the relevant question is not how distant regions of an already
existing Lorentzian Universe exchanged signals, but why the branch from
which the observable Lorentzian Universe emerged was coherently ordered
on a macroscopic scale.
The horizon problem is therefore re-expressed as a
\emph{branch-coherence problem}.

The relevant carrier of this coherence is not the massive amplitude
sector, but the orientation sector associated with the
$O(4)\to O(3)$ ordering.
Let this sector be described by a unit field
\[
n_A(x),\qquad n_A n^A = 1,
\]
encoding the orientation of the selected branch.
Its natural low-gradient Euclidean ordering functional on a connected
progenitor domain $\Omega_{\rm br}$ takes the sigma-model form
\begin{equation}
\label{eq:horizon_sigma_main}
S_{\rm ord}[n]
=
\frac{\kappa}{2}
\int_{\Omega_{\rm br}} d^d x\,
(\partial_i n_A)(\partial_i n^A),
\qquad
\kappa>0.
\end{equation}
Since $(\partial n)^2\ge 0$, the global minimum on a connected domain is
reached precisely when
\[
\partial_i n_A = 0,
\qquad\text{i.e.}\qquad
n_A(x)=\text{const on }\Omega_{\rm br}.
\]
Thus a macro-coherent ordered branch is not an external assumption added
to solve the horizon problem, but the simplest exact realization of the
ECT ordering problem itself.

This does not mean that all non-uniform branch realizations are
forbidden.
What it does establish is that a macro-coherent
$O(4)\to O(3)$ transition is \emph{organically realizable} in ECT.
If the observed Universe corresponds to such a coherently selected
connected branch, then its large-scale homogeneity is inherited from the
branch-selection event itself rather than manufactured later by causal
equilibration inside an already formed spacetime.
In this sense, a macro-coherent branch transition is already sufficient
to dissolve the standard horizon paradox for the observed Universe.

The assumption that the progenitor domain is connected, and that its
coherence scale is large enough to satisfy
\[
L_{\rm br}\,\frac{a_0}{a_{\rm br}}\gtrsim L_{\rm hom},
\]
is at present a working hypothesis rather than a first-principles
derivation.
Its role here is to formulate the coherent-branch scenario precisely.
Additional quantitative support for the required scale is discussed in
Appendix~\ref{app:horizon_ect_appendix}.

At the level of classical action comparison, the coherent branch is also
the natural low-gradient baseline.
This stronger preference statement goes beyond the exact minimum result
above and should therefore be interpreted more cautiously:
large $O(1)$ twists of the orientation field across a scale $L$ carry a
positive action cost
\[
S_{\rm ord}\sim \kappa\,(\Delta\theta)^2\,L^{d-2},
\]
so macroscopically fragmented branches are not free.
This preference statement, however, is made only at the level of
classical action comparison; a full semiclassical treatment including
the entropy of the configuration space would be needed to establish the
preference rigorously.
Accordingly, the preference claim should be read as a Level~B statement.

The net result is that ECT does not solve the horizon problem by
stretching an already existing Lorentzian Universe, as in standard
inflation.
Rather, it solves the problem at a deeper level: by explaining how the
observable Lorentzian Universe may emerge from a coherently ordered
pre-Lorentzian branch.

The structural result above is already sufficient to define a
self-consistent coherent-branch solution of the horizon problem in the
ECT setting for the observed Universe.
What remains for further development is not the existence of the
mechanism itself, but its sharpening: a fuller nonequilibrium treatment
of branch-selection dynamics, a more explicit semiclassical comparison
between coherent and fragmented sectors, a tighter phenomenological
bridge through the effective pseudo-Goldstone sector.
The flatness problem is addressed in Section~\ref{sec:flatness_ect},
and the relic and monopole problem in Section~\ref{sec:relic_ect}.

\subsubsection*{Flatness problem in ECT}
\label{sec:flatness_ect}

In standard Lorentzian FLRW cosmology the flatness problem is the question
of why the spatial curvature parameter satisfies $|\Omega_K(t_0)|\ll 1$
today, given that without a special mechanism the departure from flatness
grows during matter and radiation domination and would therefore require
extremely fine-tuned initial conditions.

In ECT this formulation is not fundamental.
The observable Lorentzian Universe is not primordial but emergent.
The flatness problem is therefore reformulated as a
\emph{branch-geometry selection problem}: which spatial geometry does the
emergent Lorentzian branch inherit from the pre-Lorentzian stage?

The answer follows directly from two already established facts.

\textbf{(i)~Structural flatness from P1.}
According to Postulate~P1, the pre-Lorentzian background is a flat
Euclidean space with metric
\[
ds_E^2 = \delta_{AB}\,dX^A dX^B
= dw^2 + dx^2 + dy^2 + dz^2.
\]
The macrocoherent $O(4)\to O(3)$ ordering transition (whose coherence is
already established in Section~\ref{sec:horizon_ect_main}) selects a
globally aligned ordering direction $n_A(x)=\mathrm{const}$, which can
without loss of generality be taken as $n_A = \delta_{Aw}$.
The spatial sections orthogonal to this direction then inherit the
induced metric
\[
h_{ij} = \delta_{ij}.
\]
Since $\delta_{ij}$ is the exact Euclidean metric on $\mathbb{R}^3$, its
three-dimensional Ricci scalar vanishes identically,
${}^{(3)}R[h]=0$.
In FLRW notation ${}^{(3)}R = 6k/a^2$, so
\[
\boxed{k = 0.}
\]
This is not a preference or a fine-tuning: it is a direct structural
consequence of P1 together with the macrocoherence of the ordering
transition.

\textbf{(ii)~Preservation under homogeneous branch evolution.}
After branch emergence the large-scale homogeneous and isotropic
evolution rescales the spatial metric:
\[
ds^2 = -dt^2 + a^2(t)\,\delta_{ij}\,dx^i dx^j.
\]
The spatial geometry at each constant-$t$ slice is $a^2(t)\delta_{ij}$,
which is a time-dependent conformal rescaling of the flat metric.
Homogeneous expansion changes $a(t)$ and $H(t)$ but does not convert
a $k=0$ branch into a $k=\pm 1$ branch; within the FLRW classification,
the parameter $k$ labels the spatial-curvature class of the homogeneous
and isotropic branch rather than a dynamical field that evolves in time.
Hence, for the exact baseline branch,
\[
\Omega_K(t)
= -\frac{k}{a^2 H^2}
= 0
\quad \text{for all } t.
\]
The observed bound $|\Omega_K(t_0)| < 0.004$ (Planck 2018) is therefore
naturally satisfied; subleading departures from exact flatness may arise
from perturbations, imperfect coherence, or backreaction, but these lie
beyond the idealised baseline branch.

\textbf{Topology caveat.}
The above establishes local spatial flatness.
Under the assumption of a simply connected progenitor domain the flat
spatial sections correspond to the standard $\mathbb{R}^3$ FLRW branch.
Non-trivial spatial topology is not excluded by this argument but lies
outside the scope of the baseline branch analysis.

\textbf{Comparison with inflation.}
Standard inflation solves the flatness problem dynamically: it takes a
branch with pre-existing $k\neq 0$ and suppresses $|\Omega_K|$ by making
$aH$ large.
This is a dynamical suppression mechanism operating \emph{inside} an
already existing Lorentzian spacetime.
ECT instead solves the problem \emph{structurally}: the emergent branch is
born flat as a direct consequence of P1 and the macrocoherent ordering
transition.
Flatness in ECT is therefore a Level~A result, requiring no
fine-tuning of initial conditions and no inflationary expansion
to produce it.


\subsubsection*{Relic and monopole problem in ECT}
\label{sec:relic_ect}

In many grand-unified-theory (GUT) extensions of the standard model, a hot
early Universe is expected to produce an overabundance of heavy topological
relics --- magnetic monopoles, domain walls, cosmic strings --- whose
predicted density would vastly exceed observational bounds.
Standard inflation addresses this through dynamical dilution: exponential
expansion reduces any pre-existing relic density to negligible levels.

In ECT the relevant question is instead: what is the topological and
defect content of the \emph{emergent} Lorentzian branch?
The correct methodological standard is the same as for the horizon and
flatness problems: not to prove that no relics are conceivable in any
sector under any conditions, but to show that \emph{relic catastrophe
is not a structural consequence of ECT} and that a relic-poor observed
branch is organically realizable.

\textbf{(i)~Topological classification of the primary ordering
transition~(Level~A).}
The vacuum manifold of the $O(4)\to O(3)$ ordering transition is
\[
\mathcal{M}_1 = O(4)/O(3) \simeq S^3.
\]
The homotopy groups of $S^3$ are~\cite{Nakahara2003}:
\[
\pi_0(S^3)=0,\quad
\pi_1(S^3)=0,\quad
\pi_2(S^3)=0,\quad
\pi_3(S^3)=\mathbb{Z}.
\]
From the standard defect-classification by homotopy:
\begin{itemize}
  \item $\pi_0(\mathcal{M}_1)=0$: no topologically stable domain walls
    from this transition;
  \item $\pi_1(\mathcal{M}_1)=0$: no topologically stable cosmic strings;
  \item $\pi_2(\mathcal{M}_1)=0$: no topologically stable magnetic monopoles.
\end{itemize}
The primary $O(4)\to O(3)$ ordering transition therefore does not
topologically require stable domain walls, cosmic strings, or magnetic
monopoles.
This is not a suppression mechanism but a direct structural consequence
of the topology of the vacuum manifold.

The non-trivial class $\pi_3(S^3)=\mathbb{Z}$ indicates texture/skyrmion-like
topological sectors rather than monopoles, strings, or walls; these belong
to a different part of the ECT topological content and do not reintroduce
the standard relic catastrophe.
The full classification of predicted new states and topological sectors,
including conditional secondary defects, is given in
Section~\ref{sec:predicted_states}.

\textbf{(ii)~Standard GUT monopole overproduction is not structurally
required~(Level~B).}
Standard GUT magnetic monopoles are topological defects of the GUT gauge
symmetry, arising when a unified group $G_{\rm GUT}$ breaks to a subgroup
containing a $U(1)$ factor with $\pi_2(G_{\rm GUT}/H)\neq 0$.
This scenario requires a hot symmetric GUT phase that then cools through a
sequence of thermal gauge-symmetry breakings.
ECT does not postulate such a phase as a structural necessity of its
cosmological architecture.
The Lorentzian branch emerges through $O(4)\to O(3)$, not through a
standard hot GUT thermal history.
If no such phase precedes the emergent branch, the standard monopole
overproduction scenario does not apply:
\emph{ECT does not structurally require a monopole-overproducing GUT
thermal history.}

\textbf{(iii)~Later-sector relics: further development, not structural
catastrophe~(Level~B).}
The topological argument above covers the primary ordering transition.
Remaining questions --- including monopole-like configurations that might
arise in the electroweak sector, non-topological heavy relics, and
metastable branch configurations --- are not yet fully classified in ECT.
However, none of these has been shown to be structurally inevitable or to
produce a cosmologically dangerous abundance in the observed branch.
Moreover, the coherent smooth observed branch that the theory naturally
selects is not naturally associated with a heavily defect-saturated
realisation:
the smooth coherent observed branch of ECT is naturally associated with a
relic-poor realisation rather than with a defect-saturated one.
The remaining relic questions are therefore sector-specific model-building
issues rather than built-in structural catastrophes.

\textbf{Further development.}
If the electroweak-like condensate embedding is physically realised,
it would involve a transition of the form
$SU(2)\times U(1)\to U(1)_{\rm em}$ at the matched scale
$v_2=246$\,GeV.
At present this sector should be treated as a structural candidate
rather than an established part of the derived ECT core.
A naive global-symmetry analysis gives $\pi_2(SU(2)/U(1))\neq 0$, which
may admit monopole-like configurations at this scale; however, in gauge
theory this analysis is more subtle and the precise topological status of
such objects depends on the UV completion.
Their cosmological production in the ECT setting requires a dedicated
analysis and should be understood as part of the broader ECT relic
programme rather than as a reopening of the primary relic catastrophe.

\textbf{Comparison with inflation.}
Standard inflation addresses the relic problem through a dynamical dilution
mechanism operating inside an already existing Lorentzian cosmology: a
separate inflationary sector dilutes any pre-existing relic density
exponentially.
ECT addresses the primary problem more fundamentally:
the $O(4)\to O(3)$ transition does not topologically require stable
monopoles, walls, or strings~(Level~A), and the theory does not
structurally require the dangerous hot GUT history~(Level~B).
Later-sector relic questions remain as model-dependent further
development.

\subsubsection*{Primordial perturbations (qualitative note)}
\label{sec:primordial_perturbations_ectnote}
In standard inflation, quantum fluctuations of the inflaton and of the
metric during accelerated expansion seed the observed primordial
perturbation spectrum.
The scalar power spectrum is nearly scale-invariant,
\begin{equation}
  P_\zeta(k) \approx A_s \left(\frac{k}{k_*}\right)^{n_s-1},
  \label{eq:pzeta}
\end{equation}
with $n_s = 0.9649\pm 0.0042$ and tensor-to-scalar ratio
$r_{0.002} < 0.056$ (95\% C.L., Planck~2018 combined with
BICEP/Keck~\cite{Planck2018,BICEP2021}).
This constitutes one of inflation's most quantitatively developed
results: a concrete computational mechanism --- mode freeze-out during
de~Sitter expansion --- that successfully predicts both the spectral
tilt and the smallness of~$r$.

ECT does not yet match this computational depth, and the present work
makes no such claim.
However, the ECT framework identifies a natural perturbative sector
and a plausible route toward primordial perturbations.

\paragraph{Perturbation variables.}
The condensate field $\Phi$ on the ordered branch admits a natural
decomposition into fluctuation sectors
(see~\S\ref{sec:ssb}):
orientational (Goldstone) fluctuations $\delta n_A$,
amplitude fluctuations $\delta\sigma = \delta|\partial\Phi|$,
and phase fluctuations $\delta\theta$.
These provide the natural perturbative degrees of freedom of the
condensate/branch sector.
Their precise decomposition into scalar, vector, tensor, and
isocurvature cosmological observables requires a dedicated
gauge-invariant perturbation analysis on top of the branch-emergence
background and lies beyond the scope of the present work.

\paragraph{Structural hints.}
The pre-Lorentzian stage, in which $v_0(X)\approx\mathrm{const}$, may
admit an approximately scale-free regime and therefore provide a
natural route toward approximate scale-invariance of the primordial
spectrum.
This is a structural qualitative hint, not a derivation: demonstrating
that condensate fluctuations freeze out appropriately and map onto a
nearly scale-invariant Lorentzian spectrum requires explicit
calculation.

\paragraph{Comparison with inflation.}
On the question of primordial perturbations the current status is
asymmetric: inflation provides a quantitatively predictive framework
well-tested against Planck and BICEP/Keck data; ECT identifies the
perturbative sector and a plausible candidate mechanism, but a full
derivation of $n_s$, $A_s$, and $r$ remains the next major step in
the ECT cosmological programme.
The research agenda consists of:
(i)~derive linearised perturbation equations on the branch-emergence
background;
(ii)~identify the gauge-invariant $\zeta$-like variable;
(iii)~determine the condition for near-scale-invariance;
(iv)~compute $n_s$, $A_s$, $r$ and compare with Planck~2018.


\subsubsection*{Homogeneous FLRW branch after ordering}

Once the ordered geometric branch is established on large scales, the
effective metric reduces to an FLRW form.
In the homogeneous $\phi$-first closure:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:FLRW}
  H^2
  = \frac{8\pi G_N}{3}\,\rho_{\rm mat}
  + \frac{\Lambda_{\rm eff}(0)}{3},
  \qquad
  \Lambda_{\rm eff}(0)=\frac{U(0)}{\bar M_{\rm Pl}^2}.
\end{equation}
Equivalently, defining the effective vacuum energy density
$\rho_{\Lambda,{\rm eff}}=U(0)$:
\[
H^2=\frac{8\pi G_N}{3}\!\left(\rho_{\rm mat}+\rho_{\Lambda,{\rm eff}}\right).
\]
This is the cosmological reduction of the macroscopic closure
\eqref{eq:phi_first_action}--\eqref{eq:gen_EE}, not a derivation from
bare~P3.

\subsubsection*{Inflation-like effective descriptions of the ordering transition}

In the present ECT picture, one possible coarse-grained description of
the ordering transition is an inflation-like effective epoch in which
the Lorentzian branch becomes macroscopically established.
This should be read as an effective closure-level scenario rather than
as the core structural solution of the horizon problem.
The structural horizon-resolution mechanism in ECT is the coherent
branch-selection logic discussed above
(Section~\ref{sec:horizon_ect_main}); an inflation-like stage, if
retained, is best interpreted as a possible reinforcement or effective
macroscopic description of part of that process.
The structural ECT mechanism for the horizon and flatness problems is
the pre-Lorentzian branch-selection logic
(Section~\ref{sec:horizon_ect_main}), which does not require an
inflationary epoch.
Computing the primordial spectral index $n_s$ and tensor-to-scalar
ratio $r$ directly from ECT is the next major step in the
cosmological programme (see research agenda above); no ECT-native
formula is available at the present stage.

\subsubsection*{Dark energy as residual amplitude-sector vacuum energy}

The effective potential $U(\phi)$ generically has a residual nonzero
value at $\phi=0$.
In the macroscopic Einstein form this corresponds to:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:dark_energy}
  \Lambda_{\rm eff}=\frac{U(0)}{\bar M_{\rm Pl}^2},
  \qquad
  \rho_{\Lambda,{\rm eff}}=U(0).
\end{equation}
In the present $\phi$-first closure:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:w0_fit}
  w_0 = -1 + \frac{2\rho_{\rm kin}}{3\rho_{\rm cond}},
\end{equation}
where $\rho_{\rm kin}/\rho_{\rm cond}$ is not yet derived
from bare ECT postulates. The formula predicts $w_0>-1$
structurally, compatible with DESI~DR2 2025~\cite{DESI2024},
but no specific numerical value can be claimed without
a solved background history~(Level~B/Open).

\begin{figure}[htb]

\includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{figures/fig_w_z_desi.png}
\caption{Representative ECT closure prediction for the effective dark
energy equation of state $w(z)$ compared with $\Lambda$CDM and DESI
constraints.
\textbf{(a)}~$w(z)$: the ECT thawing condensate (solid black)
predicts $w\ge-1$ at all redshifts.
\textbf{(b)}~Deviation from $\Lambda$CDM.
The key structural point is that the ECT macroscopic branch naturally
prefers a non-phantom effective behaviour $w\ge-1$ in this class of
closures.
The precise fitted curve is phenomenological and closure-dependent,
rather than a first-principles theorem from bare~P3.}
\label{fig:w_z}
\end{figure}

\subsubsection*{Evolution of the effective gravitational coupling}

A slowly evolving order field produces a redshift-dependent effective
coupling:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:Geff_z}
  G_{\rm eff}(z)=G_N\,e^{-\beta\phi_b(z)},
\end{equation}
where $\phi_b(z)$ is the slowly evolving cosmological background
of the order field.
If $\phi_b$ varies monotonically with epoch, the effective
gravitational strength at recombination differs from its
late-time value, providing a structural route to
Hubble-tension alleviation~(Level~B).
The sign and order of magnitude of the effect follow from
$\phi$-first logic; a numerical value would require a solved
background history $\phi_b(z)$, which is not yet available.

(c)~Running gravitational coupling $G_{\rm eff}(z)/G_N$ for a

\subsubsection*{Baryogenesis: Sakharov conditions in ECT}

\label{sec:baryogenesis}

\textit{Status: Level~B. ECT is structurally compatible
with the Sakharov conditions, but a complete first-principles
baryogenesis model from bare~P3 does not yet exist~(OP7).
The numerical estimate $\eta_B\sim9\times10^{-10}$ is a
benchmark within the resonant leptogenesis closure,
not a zero-parameter prediction of ECT. In this subsection,
$u_0\sim\bar M_{\rm Pl}$ denotes the gradient vacuum scale
(notation of Section~\ref{sec:mp_matching}).}

The observed baryon-to-photon ratio $\eta_B\approx6\times10^{-10}$
requires a mechanism for baryogenesis satisfying the three
Sakharov conditions \cite{Sakharov1967}: (i)~baryon number violation,
(ii)~C and CP violation, and (iii)~departure from thermal equilibrium.
We summarise how each condition can be realised within ECT.

\textit{The items below should be read as closure-level
mechanisms that may supply Sakharov-compatible ingredients
in ECT, not as uniquely established microscopic channels
of the theory or as a completed derivation from bare~P3.}

\textbf{(i) Baryon number violation.}
The fifth-force interaction $\mathcal{L}_5=\beta_5\bar\Psi\gamma^A n_A\Psi$
(Section~\ref{sec:fifth_force}) couples fermions to the condensate
orientation~$n^A$.  At non-zero temperature, sphaleron-like processes
in the condensate background can convert lepton asymmetry into baryon
asymmetry via the electroweak anomaly (standard leptogenesis).
Alternatively, topologically nontrivial condensate sectors
(Section~\ref{sec:predicted_states}) may provide additional
nonperturbative channels for baryon-number-violating transitions.
Their explicit microscopic realisation remains open.

\textbf{(ii) CP violation.}
The condensate direction $n^A$ explicitly breaks O(4) to O(3) and
thereby Lorentz symmetry; when combined with CPT invariance this
implies CP violation proportional to the condensate gradient
$\partial_A n^B$.  Additionally, the right-handed neutrino mass
$M_R\sim10^9$~GeV generated by Yukawa coupling to the condensate
($M_R = y_R v_2$) provides the complex phases needed for leptogenesis,
as in the standard see-saw mechanism.
The scale $M_R\sim10^9$~GeV is an illustrative benchmark;
it is numerically compatible~(coincidental, not derived)
with the geometric mean of the two condensate
scales $u_0\sim\bar M_{\rm Pl}$ and $v_2\sim246$~GeV
(using notation of Section~\ref{sec:mp_matching}),
\begin{equation}
  M_R^{\rm geom} \equiv \sqrt{u_0\, v_2}
  \approx \sqrt{2.4\times10^{18}\times246}\;\text{GeV}
  \approx 2.4\times10^{10}\;\text{GeV},
\end{equation}
This proximity is suggestive but not a derivation~(Level~B).
A Yukawa coupling $y_R = M_R/v_2 \sim 4\times10^6$ is
a strong-coupling-regime input, not derived from condensate
dynamics~(OP7).

\textbf{(iii) Departure from thermal equilibrium.}
In the ECT macroscopic scenario, the $O(4)\to O(3)$ ordering
transition is assumed to be rapid (possibly first- or
second-order) at the Planck scale~(Level~B; the actual
order depends on the bare potential, OP3).
If the transition completes on a timescale
$\tau\sim M_{\rm Pl}^{-1}$, this is much faster than the
thermal equilibration time for heavy right-handed neutrinos
($\tau_{\rm eq}\sim M_R^{-1} \gg M_{\rm Pl}^{-1}$ for $M_R\ll M_{\rm Pl}$),
satisfying the out-of-equilibrium condition.

\textbf{Estimate of $\eta_B$.}
Using the standard resonant leptogenesis formula with
$M_R\sim10^9$~GeV, the wash-out factor $\kappa\sim0.1$--$1$,
and the CP-asymmetry $\varepsilon\sim10^{-6}$ typical of the
see-saw mechanism, one can obtain, within the resonant leptogenesis
closure~(Level~B),
\begin{equation}
\eta_B
\sim \frac{3}{2}\,\varepsilon\,\kappa\,\frac{n_R}{s}
\sim 9\times10^{-10},
\end{equation}
within a factor $\sim\!1.5$ of the observed value, as a
benchmark consistency check within the resonant leptogenesis
closure~(Level~B). Reducing this discrepancy and deriving
$\varepsilon$ and $\kappa$ from ECT condensate dynamics
are open problems~(OP7, Section~\ref{sec:open_problems}).

\textbf{Relation to Sakharov induced gravity.}
The derivation of $G_N$ via coefficient matching is structurally
analogous to Sakharov's induced gravity
\cite{Sakharov1968}---gravity as an effective theory from integrating
out matter fields---but with the key distinction that in ECT the
``matter'' is the condensate itself, which simultaneously generates
spacetime and the conditions for baryogenesis.

The global cosmological history in the present ECT picture is
summarised schematically in Fig.~\ref{fig:timeline}.
Unlike the earlier benchmark-era cosmology schematic, the present
figure is explicitly comparative: it contrasts the standard
$\Lambda$CDM history with the current ECT picture, while also
indicating the schematic evolution of the relevant condensate
quantities.
In this comparison, the present epoch is shared, but the observable
Lorentzian history of the ECT Universe begins later than in
$\Lambda$CDM because it is preceded by an Euclidean /
pre-Lorentzian ordering stage.
It is in Fig.~\ref{fig:timeline}, not in the internal ECT sector
map, that the difference between the standard $\Lambda$CDM age
assignment and the present ECT observable/Lorentzian history is
meant to be read.
The inflation-like label in the ECT row of Fig.~\ref{fig:timeline}
should therefore be read only as a possible effective description of the
ordering epoch; the structural ECT resolution of the horizon problem
lies in coherent branch selection
(Section~\ref{sec:horizon_ect_main}), not in the assumption of a
separate primordial inflaton stage.

\subsubsection*{Universe age in ECT}
\label{sec:universe_age}

\textit{Status: Level~B. The $G_{\rm eff}(z)$ evolution
and $w_0 > -1$ follow from the macroscopic $\phi$-first closure;
numerical estimates are illustrative. The Hubble tension
alleviation is partial~(Level~B).}

In the current $\phi$-first macroscopic closure, the cosmic expansion
history is modified through two effective ingredients~(Level~B):
(i) the redshift-dependent gravitational coupling
$G_{\rm eff}(z) = G_N e^{-\beta\phi_b(z)}$ (slowly varying background), and
(ii) the kinetic dark-energy equation of state $w_0 > -1$.
Both effects increase the effective Hubble rate at intermediate redshifts, 
shifting $H_0^{\rm ECT} \approx 70\text{--}71\,\mathrm{km\,s^{-1}\,Mpc^{-1}}$.

The age of the Universe is
\begin{equation}
  t_0 = \int_0^\infty \frac{dz}{(1+z)\,H^{\rm ECT}(z)},
  \label{eq:age_integral}
\end{equation}
where the ECT Hubble function reads
\begin{equation}
  H^{\rm ECT}(z)^2 = H_0^2\!\left[
    \Omega_m(1+z)^3 (1+z)^{2\varepsilon}
    + \Omega_\Lambda \bigl(1 + 2\rho_{\rm kin}/\rho_{\rm cond}\bigr)
    \cdot f_w(z)
  \right],
\end{equation}
with $f_w(z)$ capturing the redshift evolution of $w(z)$.

\textbf{Numerical estimate.}
Using an illustrative power-law background $\phi_b(z)=-2\varepsilon\ln(1+z)$
with $\varepsilon\sim 0.01$ (closure-level, not derived), and flat
$\Lambda$CDM as the fiducial, numerical integration gives an indicative range
\begin{equation}
  t_0^{\rm ECT} \approx 13.3\text{--}13.5\;\mathrm{Gyr}
  \quad\text{(illustrative closure)},
  \label{eq:t0_ECT}
\end{equation}
compared to the Planck $\Lambda$CDM value $t_0^{\Lambda{\rm CDM}} = 13.80 \pm 0.02\,\mathrm{Gyr}$.
The ECT mechanism generically shifts $t_0$ by $\mathcal{O}(\varepsilon)$ relative
to $\Lambda$CDM; the specific range above is illustrative.

\textbf{Honesty note.}
This estimate uses the ECT-corrected $H_0$ and $w_0$ but does not
include a full MCMC fit to CMB+BAO+SN data.  The numbers in
equation~\eqref{eq:t0_ECT} should be regarded as an order-of-magnitude
orientation.  A quantitative confrontation with data requires the same
MCMC pipeline used for Hubble tension analyses (Section~\ref{sec:mp_cosmo}),
which is left for future work.

\textbf{Connection to JWST.}
In ECT $\phi$-logic, the effective gravitational coupling
$G_{\rm eff}(z)=G_N e^{-\beta\phi_b(z)}>G_N$ at high redshift
accelerates structure formation and boosts the abundance of rare
early halos by a factor $\times2$--3 for $\nu\sim5$--6 peaks
(Section~\ref{sec:jwst}).
The background ansatz $\phi_b(z)=-2\varepsilon\ln(1+z)$,
slow background evolution of $\phi_b$, is consistent with the cosmological
$G_{\rm eff}(z)$ fit; the JWST tension is alleviated but not
fully resolved in the present controlled framework.



\subsubsection*{Cosmological Predictions}

\textit{Status: Level~B. All numerical values follow
from the $\phi$-first macroscopic closure. They are
benchmark predictions, not zero-parameter outputs
of bare~P3.}

These benchmark values belong to different effective
closure layers (inflationary ordering, late-time
amplitude-sector dynamics, and resonant leptogenesis) and
should not be interpreted as outputs of a single
zero-parameter first-principles computation.

\begin{itemize}
\item $n_s \approx 0.967$ (Planck~2018: $0.965\pm0.004$).
\textit{ECT identifies the mechanism (branch-selection /
scale-invariance of pre-Lorentzian stage) but does not yet provide
an ECT-native formula for $n_s$.  Derivation is the next step in
the cosmological programme~(Open, OP3).}

\item $w_0 = -1+2\rho_{\rm kin}/(3\rho_{\rm cond})$ (structural formula;
specific value not derived from ECT bare postulates).
\textit{Level~B: $\rho_{\rm kin}/\rho_{\rm cond}$ is a free closure parameter.
The formula predicts $w_0>-1$ structurally, compatible with
DESI~DR2~2024 ($-0.827\pm0.059$)~\cite{DESI2024}.}

\item $\eta_B \approx 9\times10^{-10}$
(observed: $6\times10^{-10}$; factor $\sim\!1.5$).
\textit{Benchmark consistency check inside the resonant leptogenesis
closure~(Level~B); the CP-asymmetry and washout factor are not
derived from condensate dynamics~(OP7).}
\end{itemize}

the standard $\Lambda$CDM picture and the ECT picture at each
cosmological epoch.
This comparison reflects the current status of the theory: the
late-time gravitational condensate sector is quantitatively resolved
(Level~B), while the early-Universe ordering layer remains a structural
cosmological scenario rather than a completed first-principles
nonequilibrium derivation.
The horizon problem is structurally reformulated and resolved in ECT
through coherent branch selection
(Section~\ref{sec:horizon_ect_main}), while an inflation-like ordering
epoch, if retained, should be read as an effective reinforcement rather
than as the core mechanism of the solution.

\paragraph{The pre-Lorentzian Euclidean scenario~(Level~B).}

\textit{All content in this paragraph and the two following is
a Level~B cosmological scenario, not a theorem derived from bare~P3.
The scenario is structurally motivated by the postulate architecture
(P1--P4), but quantitative details require explicit dynamics of the
effective amplitude-sector potential~(OP3).}

The most distinctive structural difference between ECT and $\Lambda$CDM
is the admission of a pre-Lorentzian Euclidean regime.
In $\Lambda$CDM the classical theory is undefined before $t_{\rm Pl}$,
and the initial singularity marks the formal boundary.
In the ECT macroscopic closure scenario~(Level~B), the four-dimensional
Euclidean manifold $\mathcal{M}=\mathbb{R}^4$ is posited to be active
prior to the ordering transition.  In this pre-Lorentzian regime the
$O(4)$ symmetry is unbroken, the Lorentzian metric and causal structure
are not yet established, and temporal evolution in the Lorentzian sense
is absent.  Lorentzian signature, causal structure, and the macroscopic
arrow of time are all structurally motivated consequences of the
$O(4)\to O(3)$ ordering transition (Level~A in the broken-phase EFT;
see Section~\ref{sec:mp_cosmo}).
This is an ECT-inspired scenario; the explicit dynamics of the transition
itself require OP3.
What is already established structurally is that, if the ordering
transition is realised coherently on a connected progenitor branch, then
the horizon problem is resolved in ECT by branch coherence rather than by
late causal equilibration (Section~\ref{sec:horizon_ect_main}).

\paragraph{Two possible far-future branches~(Level~B closure-dependent scenarios).}

The long-term fate of the ordered Lorentzian domain depends on the
asymptotic behaviour of the effective ordered-branch amplitude variables.
Within the present $\phi$-first macroscopic closure two structurally
distinct branches are possible~(Level~B; both require explicit
amplitude-sector dynamics (OP3):
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textbf{Scenario~A} ($\chi\to\chi_\infty=\text{const}$,
    Level~B): the macroscopic ordering variables stabilise; the
    geometric branch remains active with $G_{\rm eff}\approx G_N$
    and the universe enters an asymptotic de~Sitter-like phase.
    This is the structurally simpler branch and qualitatively
    compatible with current observations.
  \item \textbf{Scenario~B} ($\chi\to0$, Level~B speculative):
    the ordering variables relax toward the Euclidean phase on
    a closure-dependent timescale; the macroscopic closure formally
    drives $G_{\rm eff}$ upward as $\chi$ decreases, eventually
    driving a possible recollapse of the ordered domain.
    The quantitative timescale is closure-dependent~(OP3) and
    not a fixed prediction of ECT.
\end{itemize}
The key observational discriminant between the two scenarios is
$|\dot{G}/G|$ (sensitivity expected from next-generation lunar
laser ranging and pulsar timing) and non-zero $w_a$ from future
BAO surveys.  Current data are consistent with both.

\paragraph{Schematic evolution of the ordered branch~(Level~B closure).}

In the ECT macroscopic closure scenario, the evolution across
cosmological epochs can be summarised schematically as follows
(all statements Level~B; explicit field dynamics require OP3):
\begin{itemize}
  \item \textit{Pre-Lorentzian regime:} Euclidean condensate phase;
    $O(4)$ symmetry unbroken; Lorentzian metric and causal structure
    absent.
  \item \textit{Ordering transition:} $O(4)\to O(3)$ spontaneous
    symmetry breaking; Lorentzian signature and macroscopic arrow
    of time emerge; effective amplitude-sector potential drives the
    condensate to the ordered vacuum.
  \item \textit{Inflation and reheating:} ordering transition
    serves as the candidate inflation mechanism within the
    macroscopic closure; condensate excitations thermalise into
    the SM plasma~(Level~B; reheating mechanism requires OP3).
  \item \textit{From reheating to today:} ordering variables
    approximately stationary; $G_{\rm eff}\approx G_N$;
    residual kinetic energy in the order field acts as dark-energy
    source with $w_0>-1$ (ECT structural prediction; specific value open)~(Level~B closure).
  \item \textit{Far future:} Scenario~A or Scenario~B as above.
\end{itemize}
The three-scale structure of the macroscopic closure
($u_0\sim\bar{M}_{\rm Pl}$, $v_{\rm EW}=246$\,GeV,
$v_{\rm gal}\sim r_0^{-1}$) represents three effective regimes
of the condensate at different scales; connecting them via a
renormalisation-group flow is Open Problem~OP-new-6
(Section~\ref{sec:open_problems}).


\subsubsection*{Universe size, structure, and effective dimensionality}
\label{sec:universe_size}

\textit{Status: Level~B (ECT-inspired cosmological scenario).
The discussion below concerns a possible large-scale completion
of the macroscopic closure in which the ordered Lorentzian domain
is finite and may be embedded in a pre-Lorentzian Euclidean regime.
This is a structurally motivated scenario, not a first-principles
theorem from bare~P3~(OP3).}

\paragraph{Size of the ECT domain.}

In the ECT macroscopic scenario~(Level~B), the physical universe
is a finite ordered Lorentzian domain in which the macroscopic
geometric branch is active. Beyond the causal boundary of this domain
the geometric branch degenerates: the macroscopic closure formally
drives $G_{\rm eff}$ upward, while the Lorentzian geometric description
ceases to remain well-defined~(Level~B scenario;
explicit dynamics requires OP3). The $O(4)$-symmetric
pre-Lorentzian condensate regime is conjectured to take over.

The domain radius $R_{\rm domain}$ is set by the current Hubble
radius $R_H = c/H_0 \approx 1.37\times10^{26}$\,m, with a possible
amplification factor from the pre-Lorentzian ordering stage.
The precise amplification depends on the dynamics of the ordering
transition~(OP3); borrowing $e^{N_e}$ from inflationary cosmology
would introduce a concept external to ECT.
The present Level~B estimate is simply
$R_{\rm domain}\approx1.56\times10^{52}$\,m
$\approx1.65\times10^{36}$\,ly $\approx5\times10^{33}$\,Gly.

\paragraph{Effective dimensionality at different scales.}

A characteristic feature of ECT is that the effective dimensionality
of structure depends on the scale of observation through the condensate
coherence properties.

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.95\textwidth]{figures/fig_dimensionality_phi.png}
\caption{Force-law effective dimensionality in ECT.
\textbf{(a)}~$d_{\rm force}(r)$ for three galaxy masses,
computed analytically from the $\phi$-closure
$\mu(g_{\rm obs}/g_\dagger)\,g_{\rm obs} = GM/r^2$.
In the screened regime ($g_{\rm obs} \gg g_\dagger$):
$d_{\rm force} \to 3$ (Newtonian).
Beyond the transition radius $r_* = \sqrt{GM/g_\dagger}$
(filled dots): $d_{\rm force} \to 2$
($g \propto 1/r \Rightarrow v_{\rm flat} = \mathrm{const}$).
At $r_*$ (where $x = g_{\rm obs}/g_\dagger = 1$):
$d_{\rm force} = 7/3 \approx 2.33$.
Curves fade beyond the isolation radius ($\sim 1$--$3\,$Mpc),
where the single-galaxy approximation breaks down.
The shaded region at $r > 3\,$Mpc indicates cosmic-web scales
where $d_{\rm force}$ is not yet derivable from the present
$\phi$-closure.
\textbf{(b)}~Universal curve $d_{\rm force}(x)$ with the
closed-form analytic expression
$d_{\rm force} = 1 + 2(1+x^2)/(2+x^2)$;
every galaxy falls on the same curve, because the formula
depends only on $x = g_{\rm obs}/g_\dagger$, not on $M$ or $r$
separately.  Different galaxies differ only in the mapping
$x(r)$: heavier galaxies reach higher $x$ at any given $r$.
This universality is the dimensionality counterpart of the
radial acceleration relation.}
\label{fig:dimensionality}
\end{figure}

\paragraph{Force-law effective dimensionality.}

The effective dimensionality of the gravitational interaction
in ECT is defined by the force-law exponent:
\begin{equation}
d_{\rm force}(r)
\;\equiv\; 1 - \frac{d\ln g_{\rm obs}}{d\ln r}\,.
\label{eq:dforce_def}
\end{equation}
In $d$ spatial dimensions the gravitational force scales as
$g \propto 1/r^{d-1}$; eq.~\eqref{eq:dforce_def} reads off
$d$ from the local force law.

\paragraph{General formula.}
\textit{Note on interpolation function: the $d_{\rm force}$
analysis below uses the analytic closure
$\mu(x)=x/\sqrt{1+x^2}$ for analytical transparency.
This should be read as an illustrative representative of the
ECT-compatible interpolation class, not as a unique choice
derived from first principles.  The qualitative dimensionality
behaviour is closure-independent provided the required ECT
asymptotics $\mu\to1$ and $\mu\to x$ are satisfied.}

Starting from the $\phi$-closure
$\mu(x)\,g_{\rm obs} = g_N(r)$ with $x \equiv g_{\rm obs}/g_\dagger$,
one obtains by logarithmic differentiation:
\begin{equation}
\boxed{
d_{\rm force}(r) = 1 + \frac{p_N(r)}
  {1 + \dfrac{d\ln\mu}{d\ln x}}\,,
}
\label{eq:dforce_general}
\end{equation}
where $p_N(r) \equiv -d\ln g_N/d\ln r$.
For a point mass $p_N = 2$.
The derivation is given in Appendix~\ref{app:deff_method}.

\paragraph{Closed-form result.}
For the illustrative ECT-compatible closure
$\mu(x) = x/\sqrt{1+x^2}$ one finds
$d\ln\mu/d\ln x = 1/(1+x^2)$, and therefore
\begin{equation}
\boxed{
d_{\rm force}(x)
=
1 + 2\,\frac{1+x^2}{2+x^2}
}
\label{eq:dforce_closed}
\end{equation}
for a point-mass Newtonian source $p_N=2$.
Thus:
\begin{itemize}
  \item in the screened Newtonian regime $x\gg1$:
    $d_{\rm force}\to 3$, corresponding to $g\propto 1/r^2$;
  \item in the deep critical regime $x\ll1$:
    $d_{\rm force}\to 2$, corresponding to $g\propto 1/r$
    and asymptotically flat rotation curves;
  \item at the transition point $x=1$:
    $d_{\rm force}(1)=1+\tfrac{4}{3}=\tfrac{7}{3}\approx2.33$.
\end{itemize}

This force-law dimensionality is a macroscopic closure diagnostic
(Level~B), not a theorem about the microscopic topological dimension
of the arena.  It measures how the effective gravitational response
interpolates between Newtonian and critical-branch behaviour.

\paragraph{Size of the ECT domain.}

In the ECT macroscopic scenario~(Level~B), the physical universe
is a finite ordered Lorentzian domain in which the macroscopic
geometric branch is active. Beyond the causal boundary of this domain
the geometric branch degenerates: the macroscopic closure formally
drives $G_{\rm eff}$ upward, while the Lorentzian geometric description
ceases to remain well-defined~(Level~B scenario;
explicit dynamics requires OP3). The $O(4)$-symmetric
pre-Lorentzian condensate regime is conjectured to take over.


\subsubsection*{Physical interpretation}

Cosmology in ECT is the large-scale history of the ordered condensate
branch.
Inflation corresponds to ordering; dark energy corresponds to residual
amplitude-sector vacuum energy; variation of $G_{\rm eff}$ reflects the slow
cosmological evolution of the order variable.
These interpretations are not yet first-principles theorems from bare
P3, but they form a consistent and physically motivated closure of the
geometric branch.

\subsubsection*{What is established and what is not}

\textbf{Structurally motivated / developed~(Level~B):}
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item A pre-Lorentzian ordering scenario is natural in ECT.
  \item Homogeneous FLRW branch of the $\phi$-first closure is
    well-defined.
  \item Inflation and dark energy admit natural reinterpretation in
    terms of the ordered condensate branch.
\end{enumerate}

\textbf{Open:}
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item Full nonequilibrium derivation of the ordering transition from
    bare P3.
  \item Microscopic derivation of $U(\phi)$~(OP3).
  \item Quantitative baryogenesis model within the same closure.
  \item Precision cosmological fitting of $G_{\rm eff}(z)$ across
    all datasets.
\end{enumerate}


% ============================================================
\subsection{Galactic sector: the ordered-branch amplitude variable}
\label{sec:mp_galactic}
% ============================================================

\textit{Status: all Level~B (phenomenological closure of the
$\phi$-first critical-branch strategy). Not first-principles from
bare~P3. One free parameter $r_0$ per galaxy.}

\subsubsection*{Why the condensate-amplitude route fails}

A direct galactic role of the heavy radial condensate mode is strongly
disfavoured for two independent reasons.

First, under the phenomenological matching hypothesis
$\xi_{\rm cond}\sim\ell_{\rm Pl}$ used in the present ECT closure,
the radial mode is extremely heavy, of order the microscopic cutoff,
so it cannot naturally generate kpc-scale structure.
Attempting to stretch that mode to galactic distances would require an
extreme and radiatively unstable tuning of the underlying parameters.

Second, an ordinary linear scalar-tensor realisation of the same
sector falls into the Brans--Dicke-like regime and generically produces
post-Newtonian deviations far too large for Solar-System data.

For these reasons the galactic branch of ECT is not built from the bare
radial condensate amplitude itself, but from the nonlinear critical
dynamics of the ordered-branch amplitude variable~$\phi$.


\begin{figure}[htb]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.82\textwidth]{figures/fig_regime_diagram.png}
\caption{ECT regime diagram in the plane of the ordered-branch
amplitude variable $\phi$ and local acceleration $g/g_\dagger$.  Different
physical regimes --- Quantum/Planckian, Newtonian (GR),
MOND-like critical branch, and deep-MOND / void ---
are controlled by the local condensate amplitude relative to its
asymptotic screened value.
Dense environments (high $\phi$) are screened and recover
standard GR; dilute outskirts and voids approach the critical
boundary where the nonlinear $\phi$-branch activates.
Representative astrophysical systems are indicated.}
\label{fig:regime_diagram}
\end{figure}

%%% -----------------------------------------------------------
\subsubsection*{Screening in dense environments}
\label{subsec:lof_screening}

\textit{Status: Level~B (phenomenological $\phi$-first closure).
The screening mechanism follows from the $\phi$-equation of
motion~\eqref{eq:phi_eom} under the quasi-static approximation;
a first-principles derivation from bare~P3 remains open~(OP-screen).}

Once the local condensate shifts $\bar\phi(\rho)$ upward in
denser environments, the ordered-branch amplitude variable acquires an
effective mass for perturbations around the local equilibrium.
Expanding $\phi = \bar\phi(\rho) + \delta\phi$, small fluctuations
obey a Yukawa-type equation with effective mass (Level~B):
\begin{equation}
m_\phi^2(\rho)
= \frac{U_0''(\bar\phi(\rho))
        + 4\pi\rho/(\bar\chi^2 c^2)}
       {K(\bar\phi(\rho))}\,.
\label{eq:mphi_main}
\end{equation}
Both numerator terms are positive and increase with $\rho$ for
convex $U_0$; in the one-loop estimate $K \propto e^{-\phi/2}$,
which \emph{decreases} at larger $\bar\phi$ and thus reinforces
the growth of $m_\phi^2$ (Level~B; $U_0$ not yet derived from
bare~P3, OP3).  Hence $m_\phi$ grows with density,
and the Yukawa screening length $\lambda_\phi = 1/m_\phi$ shrinks
in dense environments. This is the mechanism that screens
the fifth-force contribution of $\phi$ inside galaxies and in the
Solar System, rendering the theory consistent with the Cassini
bound~\cite{Bertotti2003}~(Level~B).

\paragraph{Physical interpretation.}
In the present framework, screening is not an external fix
appended to save the theory. It is a direct consequence of the
fact that dense matter holds the vacuum in a deep Lorentzian
phase, where the amplitude sector is effectively screened or frozen.
The recovery of GR in the Solar System is therefore not in
conflict with the existence of a nonlinear galactic branch;
it is an expected feature of the same underlying mechanism.


%%% -----------------------------------------------------------
%%% -----------------------------------------------------------
\subsubsection*{Hierarchy of length scales}
\label{subsec:lof_scales}

\textit{Status: Level~A for the identification of distinct
physical scales; Level~B for numerical estimates based on the
$\phi$-first phenomenological closure.}

The galactic closure of the macroscopic branch contains several
physically distinct scales that must not be conflated.

\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item \textbf{Microscopic condensate scale.}
  The correlation length of the heavy radial mode,
  $\xi_{\rm cond}\sim m_\sigma^{-1}\sim\ell_{\rm Pl}$,
  marks the breakdown of the coarse-grained geometric EFT.
  This scale plays no role in galactic dynamics.

  \item \textbf{Environmental screening length.}
  In regions of high baryonic density the $\phi$-branch
  is driven into the screened Newtonian regime; the
  corresponding Yukawa screening length $\lambda_\phi(\rho)=m_\phi^{-1}(\rho)$
  is density-dependent~(Level~B; eq.~\eqref{eq:mphi_main}).
  This scale governs Solar-System and laboratory suppression
  of the fifth-force correction.

  \item \textbf{Critical galactic transition scale.}
  The transition from screened Newtonian behaviour to the
  critical $\phi$-branch is governed by the acceleration scale
  $g_\dagger$, or equivalently by the galactic transition
  radius $r_0=\sqrt{GM_{\rm bar}/g_\dagger}$.
  This is the characteristic IR scale of the galactic closure.

  \item \textbf{Cosmological reference scale.}
  In the present closure, $g_\dagger\sim cH_0$, which explains
  the quasi-universality of the acceleration scale while still
  allowing environment-dependent deviations~(Level~B).
\end{enumerate}

The four scales form a clear hierarchy:
\begin{equation}
\label{eq:scale_hierarchy}
\xi_{\rm cond}
\;\ll\;
\lambda_\phi(\rho)
\;\lesssim\;
r_0
\;\ll\;
H_0^{-1}.
\end{equation}

\paragraph{Physical significance.}
This hierarchy explains why ECT can simultaneously exhibit:
(i)~a strongly ordered microscopic vacuum,
(ii)~screened Solar-System and laboratory behaviour,
(iii)~critical galactic $\phi$-branch phenomenology, and
(iv)~a quasi-universal cosmological acceleration scale.
The success of the galactic closure depends precisely on keeping
these scales conceptually distinct.

\subsubsection*{One-loop bridge and breakdown near criticality}
\label{subsec:lof_oneloop}

\textit{Status: Level~B (one-loop EFT estimate within the
$\phi$-first macroscopic closure; the one-loop coefficient
$a\simeq 1/2$ is scheme-dependent and constitutes an open
problem, OP-screen; see Appendix~\ref{app:lof:oneloop_calc}).}

The next question is what happens as the ambient density decreases
and the vacuum approaches the critical region. Integrating out the
fast Goldstone modes generates a nontrivial kinetic response for
$\phi$ (the one-loop calculation is presented in
Appendix~\ref{app:lof:oneloop_calc}). At the one-loop level this
can be parametrised as
\begin{equation}
K(\phi) \propto e^{-a\phi}\,,
\qquad a > 0\,,
\label{eq:kphi_main}
\end{equation}
with the one-loop estimate suggesting $a \simeq 1/2$. The precise coefficient is scheme-dependent~(Level~B; OP-screen) and
should not be overinterpreted,
but the qualitative structural point is robust~(Level~B): the effective quadratic kinetic
sector acquires a strong $\phi$-dependence near the critical region.

Higher-order gradient terms become increasingly important in the
same limit. A generic static expansion takes the form
\begin{equation}
\Gamma[\phi]
= \int d^3x\left[
  \frac{K(\phi)}{2}(\nabla\phi)^2
  + \frac{L(\phi)}{3}|\nabla\phi|^3
  + \cdots
\right].
\label{eq:gamma_gradient_main}
\end{equation}
If the ratio $L(\phi)/K(\phi)$ grows toward criticality
(which is expected on general EFT grounds: near criticality,
higher-gradient operators become increasingly sensitive to
the small ordered-branch amplitude parameter and may therefore outgrow
the quadratic sector, even if their detailed coefficients are
not yet known from first principles), then
the field-dependent transition gradient
\begin{equation}
|\nabla\phi|_{\rm trans}(\phi)
= \frac{K(\phi)}{L(\phi)}
\;\xrightarrow[\phi\to-\infty]{}\; 0
\label{eq:transition_gradient_main}
\end{equation}
falls toward zero.

\paragraph{Resolution of the regime-ordering problem.}
This observation resolves a crucial difficulty that plagues
constant-threshold nonlinear models. If the transition scale were
a fixed constant, the nonlinear (MOND-like) regime would activate
at \emph{large} gradients (near masses) and the linear (Newtonian)
regime at \emph{small} gradients (far away) --- the opposite of
what is observed. With a field-dependent threshold:
\begin{itemize}
\item Near massive bodies ($\bar\phi > 0$, deep Lorentzian phase):
  $|\nabla\phi|_{\rm trans}$ is large, so the system stays in its
  linear screened branch.
\item On galactic outskirts ($\bar\phi \to 0^-$, near-critical):
  $|\nabla\phi|_{\rm trans} \to 0$, so even weak gradients
  activate the nonlinear branch.
\end{itemize}
This gives the physically correct ordering: GR near masses,
MOND-like dynamics in dilute regions.

The exact scaling of $L(\phi)$ is not yet fully derived from the
microscopic theory; accordingly, this step should be viewed as
EFT-supported rather than fully established. The essential
requirement is the hierarchy $L/K \to \infty$ near criticality,
not any specific exponent.


%%% -----------------------------------------------------------
%%% -----------------------------------------------------------
\subsubsection*{Critical infrared regime}
\label{subsec:lof_critical}

\textit{Status: Level~B (phenomenological critical infrared
closure of the $\phi$-first macroscopic branch). The
$Y_\phi^{3/2}$ critical kinetic form is supported by a
scale-invariance argument, but not yet derived from bare~P3~(OP2).}

Once the perturbative gradient expansion breaks down, it is
natural to replace it by a critical infrared effective theory for
the dimensionless order field $\phi$. Defining
$Y_\phi \equiv (\nabla\phi)^2$, the deep-IR static free energy
can be written as
\begin{equation}
F_{\rm IR}[\phi]
= \int d^3x\left[
  \Lambda^4\,\mathcal{F}\!\left(\frac{Y_\phi}{Y_*}\right)
  + U_{\rm eff}(\phi;\rho)
\right].
\label{eq:fir_main}
\end{equation}

\paragraph{Scale-invariance argument~(Level~B).}
At a 3D scale-invariant fixed point, if $\phi$ is treated as
dimensionless ($[\phi] = 0$), then $[Y_\phi] = L^{-2}$ and
the free-energy density must have dimension $L^{-3}$.  For a
power-law $\mathcal{F} \sim Y_\phi^p$:
\begin{equation}
[Y_\phi^p] = L^{-2p} \;\stackrel{!}{=}\; L^{-3}
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\boxed{p = 3/2\,.}
\end{equation}
This is not a claim of absolute uniqueness among all conceivable
nonlocal or logarithmically corrected structures. Rather, it is
the unique simple local power-law form built only from $Y_\phi$
that is consistent with 3D scale invariance when $[\phi] = 0$.
(An anomalous dimension $\Delta_\phi \neq 0$ would shift the
exponent to $p = 3/[2(1+\Delta_\phi)]$; for an ordered-branch amplitude
parameter this correction is expected to be small.)

\paragraph{Physical interpretation.}
The $Y_\phi^{3/2}$ form means that at criticality the ``cost''
of a gradient in $\phi$ grows faster than quadratic: the system
resists non-uniformity in the degree of Lorentzianness more
strongly than a linear theory would predict.  This nonlinear
stiffness is what produces the $1/r$ force law needed for flat
rotation curves.

\paragraph{Full interpolating equation.}
The complete static equation, valid from the Newtonian to the
deep-MOND regime, takes the AQUAL-like form
\begin{equation}
\boxed{
\nabla\cdot\left[
  \mu_\phi\!\left(
    \frac{|\nabla\phi|}{\phi_*(\phi)}
  \right)\nabla\phi
\right]
= \frac{4\pi\rho}{c^2}\,,
}
\label{eq:aqual_phi_main}
\end{equation}
with
\begin{equation}
\mu_\phi(x) \to 1 \quad (x \ll 1)\,,
\qquad
\mu_\phi(x) \to x \quad (x \gg 1)\,.
\label{eq:mu_limits_main}
\end{equation}
The key structural difference from standard MOND is that the
transition scale $\phi_*(\phi)$ depends on the local
ordered-branch amplitude background. This field dependence is what makes
the nonlinear branch appear in dilute near-critical regions
rather than near dense sources.


%%% -----------------------------------------------------------

\subsection{Early galaxy and black hole formation (JWST anomalies)}
\label{sec:jwst}

\textit{Observational context.}
The James Webb Space Telescope has revealed a population of unexpectedly massive
galaxies and supermassive black holes at redshifts $z>10$, corresponding to
cosmic ages $<500$\,Myr after the Big Bang \cite{Labbe2023,Finkelstein2022}.
The observed stellar masses ($M_\star\sim 10^{9}$--$10^{11}\,M_\odot$) and black-hole
masses ($M_{\rm BH}\sim 10^{7}$--$10^{9}\,M_\odot$) exceed the expectations of
standard $\Lambda$CDM by factors of several within the available assembly time.

\textit{ECT mechanism ($\phi$-first).}
In ECT $\phi$-logic, the effective gravitational coupling on the
cosmological background is controlled by the background order field
$\phi_b(z)$ (Section~\ref{sec:mp_nonlinear}):
\begin{equation}
  G_{\rm eff}(z) = G_N\,e^{-\beta\phi_b(z)},
  \qquad
  M^2_{\rm eff}(z) = \bar M^2_{\rm Pl}\,e^{\beta\phi_b(z)}.
  \label{eq:Geff_jwst}
\end{equation}
This is the same relation that governs the nonlinear gravity and
galactic-scale $\phi$-branch — a single order field unifies the
cosmological and galactic gravitational sectors.

The full field decomposes as $\phi(X)=\phi_b(z)+\delta\phi(X)$,
where $\phi_b(z)$ is the slowly evolving cosmological background
and $\delta\phi(X)$ encodes local deviations.
The $\phi$-logic suggests qualitatively that dense collapsing
regions may screen $\delta\phi$, driving the local dynamics back
toward the GR branch; a derivation of this screening from the
nonlinear $\phi$-equation remains open.

\textit{Background structure.}
The $\phi$-first framework identifies the cosmological background
as a slowly evolving order field $\phi_b(z)$, with
\begin{equation}
  G_{\rm eff}(z) = G_N\,e^{-\beta\phi_b(z)}.
  \label{eq:phi_b_ansatz}
\end{equation}
The specific functional form of $\phi_b(z)$ is not yet derived
from the ECT background equations; a first-principles solution
of the coupled $\phi$--Friedmann system remains an open problem.
Any specific numerical parameterisation (such as a power-law ansatz)
should be treated as an illustrative closure, not a prediction.
A power-law profile $\phi_b(z)=-2\varepsilon\ln(1+z)$ would recover
$G_{\rm eff}(z)=G_N(1+z)^{2\varepsilon}$, but any specific $\varepsilon$
should be treated as an illustrative closure only.

\textit{Structure growth.}
In the leading sub-horizon approximation, and neglecting explicit
perturbations of the $\phi$-sector in the growth equation, one may
write the standard continuity--Euler--Poisson system with the
effective coupling:
\begin{equation}
  \ddot\delta + 2H\dot\delta
  - 4\pi G_{\rm eff}(z)\,\bar\rho\,\delta = 0.
\end{equation}
At leading order in $\varepsilon$ this gives
\begin{equation}
  \frac{D_{\rm ECT}(z)}{D_{\Lambda{\rm CDM}}(z)}
  \approx 1 + \varepsilon\ln(1+z) + O(\varepsilon^2).
\end{equation}
At $z=10$: $D_{\rm ECT}/D_{\Lambda{\rm CDM}}\approx 1.024$.

\textit{Halo abundance (Press--Schechter).}
Setting $f(z)\equiv G_{\rm eff}(z)/G_N = e^{-\phi_b(z)}$,
the effective rarity of a $\nu$-$\sigma$ peak becomes
$\nu_{\rm eff}=\nu/\sqrt{f}$, giving
\begin{equation}
  \frac{n_{\rm ECT}}{n_{\Lambda{\rm CDM}}}
  = \exp\!\left[\frac{\nu^2}{2}\!\left(1-\frac{1}{f(z)}\right)\right].
  \label{eq:PS_phi}
\end{equation}
Illustrative values for a slow-roll background profile are given in
Table~\ref{tab:jwst_ps}; they are \emph{unchanged} from the
previous estimate --- the improvement here is conceptual
(unified $\phi$-language with the gravity section) rather than
numerical.

$\nu$ = peak rarity parameter; JWST anomalies correspond to
$\nu\sim5$--6.
The ansatz $\phi_b(z)=-2\varepsilon\ln(1+z)$ gives $f(z)=e^{-\phi_b}$;

\textit{Quantitative assessment.}
For the most anomalous JWST sources ($\nu\sim5$--6),
the ECT $\phi$-mechanism predicts an overabundance of $\times2$--3
relative to $\Lambda$CDM.
The observed deficit is $\times10$--100~\cite{BoylanKolchin2023},
so in its present controlled $\phi$-first form ECT \emph{alleviates
but does not resolve} the JWST early-structure tension.
With the background ansatz~\eqref{eq:phi_b_ansatz}, no additional
numerical improvement over the leading-order estimate is obtained.

The $\phi$-logic suggests that early-time global enhancement
($\phi_b<0$) and local screening in dense proto-galactic cores
may ultimately be described within a single amplitude-sector framework.
A derivation of this mechanism from the full nonlinear $\phi$-equation,
and the question of whether a nontrivial background history $\phi_b(z)$
could provide stronger early enhancement, are left as open problems
(Section~\ref{sec:open_problems}).

The late-time background apparatus developed in
Appendices~\ref{app:late_cosmo_background} and~\ref{app:late_cosmo_algorithm}
provides the first controlled connection between the Hubble tension, the
age of the Universe, and the JWST background effects in ECT.  The same
$\phi$-background drift that shifts the inferred late $H_0$ also modifies
luminosity distances to high-$z$ galaxies, the lookback time available
for early structure formation, and the effective growth environment; see
Fig.~\ref{fig:ect_hubble_jwst_background} and
Table~\ref{tab:ect_hubble_jwst_summary}.

D18 & Universe domain size $R_{\rm domain}\gtrsim R_H$ (amplified by pre-Lorentzian ordering) & B/Open & Scale depends on ordering dynamics (OP3); $e^{N_e}$ factor from inflation removed & \ref{sec:universe_size} \\
D19 & Elementary-loop action scale $S_{\rm elem}=2\pi S_0^{\rm EFT}$ from phase winding in $U(1)$-extended EFT & A/B & Cauchy--Schwarz bound strict (A); $L_{\rm core}$ input and $S_0^{\rm EFT}=\hbar$ matching remain Level~B~(OP-Q2) & \ref{sec:hbar_status}, \ref{sec:qs_hbar} \\
D20 & $\beta$ param.: general $c_*=\sqrt{\beta/(\alpha-\beta)}$ & A & --- & \ref{sec:ssb} \\
D21 & Casimir: conditional $3/2$ estimate from minimal reduced soft-orientation EFT (not a proof of three physical Goldstone particles; condensate-reflecting boundaries only) & B & Conditional on minimal EFT boundary assumption & \ref{sec:casimir} \\
D22 & Unruh-type temperature from Euclidean/Rindler coherent-vacuum response; standard $\hbar$ form after $S_0=\hbar$ identification & A/B & Structural Euclidean/Rindler response is Level~A; observational $\hbar$ form requires Level~B identification~(OP-Q2) & \ref{sec:qs_unruh} \\
D23a & Time-dependent ordered backgrounds admit Bogoliubov-type mode mixing & B & Structural analogy established; explicit rates remain closure-dependent & \ref{sec:qs_particle_production} \\
D23b & Primordial spectral parameters ($n_s$, $r$): ECT-native derivation open & C & Branch-selection mechanism identified; explicit perturbation calculation pending (OP3) & \ref{sec:mp_cosmo} \\
D24 & Gravitational stiffness matching: $G_N=c_*^4/(8\pi M_G^2)$; direct microscopic relation to ordered-branch parameters remains open & B & Geometric EFT matching; explicit $(u_0,\alpha,\beta)\to M_G$ derivation open~(OP2, OP3) & \ref{sec:mp_matching} \\
D25 & Near-horizon critical scale in the present shell closure: $\rho_c=\ell_{\rm Pl}/\sqrt{3\pi}$ & B & Shell/interface ansatz; Tolman temperature as control parameter (C2b, open); not a universal first-principles theorem & \ref{sec:critical_shell} \\
D26 & Conditional theorem: exact thermality excluded given finite-entropy shell + complete evaporation & B & Unitarity (natural) + finite shell + no infinite remnant & \ref{sec:no_exact_thermality} \\
D27 & Shell-state dependence of Hawking channel: derived consequence of D26, not assumption & B & Conditions of D26 & \ref{app:conditional_theorem} \\
D28 & Effective shell Hamiltonian structure (form, not coefficients) & C & GL criticality + Rindler geometry; coefficients open & \ref{app:chi_dynamics} \\
D29 & Minimal shell--radiation coupling form $\mathcal{L}_{\rm int}=-\tfrac{g}{2}\delta\chi(\partial\varphi)^2$ & C & Locality + linearity; coefficient $g$ open & \ref{app:chi_dynamics} \\
\bottomrule
\end{longtable}
