\subsection{Cluster-merger lensing test}
\label{sec:bullet_cluster}

\textit{Status: Level~B (algebraic $\phi$-closure; morphology
robust; amplitude order-of-magnitude estimate).
Full quantitative conclusions require a 3D metric--$\phi$
solver~(OP22).}

The Bullet Cluster~\cite{Clowe2006,Markevitch2004} ($z=0.296$)
provides the canonical test of the matter distribution in a
post-collision system: hot X-ray gas, decelerated by ram
pressure, is spatially offset from the compact collisionless
BCG/galaxy concentrations by $\Delta\approx100$--$150\,$kpc.
Gravitational-lensing reconstruction places the convergence
peaks near the BCG positions rather than the gas
clouds~\cite{Paraficz2016}, traditionally interpreted as
evidence for a collisionless dark-matter component.

In ECT, the same morphology arises from the universal
$\phi$-branch enhancement without invoking a hidden species.
The effective convergence is (Appendix~\ref{app:cluster_lensing}):
\begin{equation}
  \Sigma_{\rm eff}(x,y)
  = \nu\!\left(\frac{g_N}{g_\dagger^{\rm cl}}\right)\Sigma_b(x,y)\,,
  \label{eq:Sigma_eff_cluster}
\end{equation}
where $g_N = 2\pi G\Sigma_b$ is the projected Newtonian field,
$\nu(y)=\sqrt{(1+\sqrt{1+4/y^2})/2}$~\eqref{eq:nu_cluster}
is the algebraic $\phi$-closure enhancement, and
$g_\dagger^{\rm cl}$ is the cluster-scale critical acceleration.
In the present analysis $g_\dagger^{\rm cl}$ is treated as the
cluster counterpart of the same critical-branch threshold that
governs the galactic sector, held fixed across the four-cluster
suite rather than fitted per system.
Because $\nu$ depends on local surface density rather than on
total enclosed mass, the ECT lensing signal preferentially
traces the more compact component.  In Bullet, MACS~J0025, and
El~Gordo this is the BCG/stellar core, whereas in Abell~520 it
is the central gas core.
This is not a post-hoc adjustment: the same functional form of
$\nu$ that organises the galactic sector is carried over to the
cluster geometry, with the cluster-scale threshold
$g_\dagger^{\rm cl}$ held fixed across the four-cluster suite.

\paragraph{Four-cluster suite.}
The same protocol is applied without modification to four
post-collision systems spanning $z=0.20$--$0.87$
(Table~\ref{tab:cluster_params}).
The key numerical results are
(Figures~\ref{fig:bullet_main},~\ref{fig:cluster_suite_budget}):
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
\item \textbf{All four systems are reproduced at the qualitative
  morphological level}, with the correct identification of the
  peak-bearing component (BCG-peaked or gas-peaked, depending on
  which component is locally more compact).
  For Bullet, MACS~J0025~\cite{Bradac2008}, and
  El~Gordo~\cite{Diego2023} the $\kappa$-peaks are associated with
  the compact BCG concentrations
  ($d_\star^{\rm ECT}\lesssim37\,$kpc;
  observed $d_\star^{\rm obs}<25$--$35\,$kpc; order-of-magnitude
  consistent).
  For Abell~520~\cite{Mahdavi2007,Jee2012} the $\kappa$-peak falls
  on the \emph{gas core}, because in this system the central gas is
  more compact than the dispersed BCG distribution.
  Both morphological regimes arise from the single
  function~\eqref{eq:Sigma_eff_cluster} with no per-system tuning.
  We therefore regard the four-cluster suite as a qualitative
  morphology test rather than a precision fit.

\item \textbf{Abell~520 as a unified discriminant.}
  Abell~520 is challenging for collisionless dark-matter models
  because its lensing peak coincides with the dark-matter-depleted
  gas core~\cite{Mahdavi2007}.
  In ECT this is the expected result: the gas core is locally more
  compact ($\Sigma_{\rm gas}(0)>\Sigma_{\rm BCG}(0)$), so $\nu$
  enhancement is largest there.
  A single rule generates the BCG-peaked morphology in Bullet,
  MACS~J0025, and El~Gordo, and the gas-peaked morphology in
  Abell~520---a unified prediction absent in models requiring a
  fixed collisionless species.

\item \textbf{Amplitude.}
  The baseline $\phi$-closure gives
  $M_{\rm ECT}/M_{\rm obs}\approx0.42$--$0.71$, depending on
  baryonic concentration.
  After applying uniform ECT-specific corrections
  (Table~\ref{tab:cluster_corrections}), the estimates reach
  $\approx0.54$--$0.87$.
  These numbers are order-of-magnitude estimates from a projected
  algebraic closure; they should be read as rough indicators, not
  precision predictions.
  In particular, MACS~J0025 and Abell~520 reach
  $M_{\rm ECT}/M_{\rm obs}\approx0.86$ and $0.87$ respectively,
  approaching full closure within the accuracy of these
  order-of-magnitude estimates.
  Bullet and El~Gordo retain a deficit of $\times1.87$ and
  $\times1.79$, identifying dynamically extreme high-redshift
  mergers as the sharpest remaining test cases.
  The residual deficit is comparable to the well-known MOND
  cluster mass discrepancy (factor $\sim\!2$,
  \cite{Sanders2003,Angus2008}), suggesting a common open
  problem for baryon-based modified-gravity frameworks at merger
  scales rather than a failure specific to ECT.
\end{enumerate}

\paragraph{Summary.}
Taken together, the cluster-merger suite supports the following
picture: ECT already reproduces the observed qualitative merger
morphologies in both BCG-peaked and gas-peaked systems through a
single universal surface-density response rule.
What remains open is the quantitative amplitude problem at the
level of a residual factor $\sim1.1$--$1.9$, depending on the
system.
We therefore regard cluster mergers not as a no-go result for ECT,
but as a sharply defined Level~B qualitative success in morphology
together with a concrete quantitative target for the next
generation of 3D metric--$\phi$ calculations~(OP22).

\paragraph{Observational corrections.}
Four ECT-specific effects partially reduce the apparent amplitude
deficit (summarised and discussed in
Appendix~\ref{app:cluster_lensing}):
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\alph*)]
  \item $G_{\rm eff}(z)$ modifies $\Sigma_{\rm crit}$ by
    $\approx+0.5\%$ at $z=0.30$~(Level~B).
  \item The redshift anomaly $\delta z=\tfrac{1}{2}\delta\phi$
    shifts the spectroscopic gas temperature by $\lesssim1\%$.
  \item Post-merger non-equilibrium and non-thermal pressure
    uncertainties contribute $+15$--$25\%$ to the inferred gas
    mass~(observational~systematics).
  \item The anisotropic orientation stress
    $\Theta_{\mu\nu}[n]$~\eqref{eq:Theta_n} provides a natural
    ECT source of gravitational slip.
    A minimal Einstein-aether-like mapping with density-dependent
    $c_1(\bar\phi)=-a\bar\phi<0$ yields a rough Level~B estimate
    $E_3\approx+0.3$--$+8.5\%$ for $a\sim0.5$--$2$
    (parametrised, not derived; OP-c1).
\end{enumerate}

  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figures/fig_bullet_main}

  ($z=0.296$; parameters from Table~\ref{tab:cluster_params}).
  \textbf{(a)} Baryonic surface density $\Sigma_b$; gas

  \textbf{(b)} Enhancement factor $\nu(g_N/g_\dagger^{\rm cl})$; dashed

  \textbf{(c)} Effective convergence

    vs $d_{\rm gas}=83\,$kpc from the gas peak.
  \textbf{(d)} 1D merger-axis profile.
  \textbf{(e)} Amplitude budget: successive corrections raise
    $M_{\rm ECT}/M_{\rm obs}$ from $0.42$ (baseline) to
    $\approx0.54$; residual deficit $\times1.9$~(OP22).
  \textbf{(f)} Gravitational slip $E_3$ from the orientation
    stress $\Theta_{\mu\nu}[n]$ with $c_1(\bar\phi)=-a\bar\phi$;
    shaded band marks the cluster range
    $\bar\phi\simeq0.03$--$0.08$.
  Filled circles: BCG (collisionless).
  Open circles: X-ray gas (collisional).}
  \label{fig:bullet_main}
\end{figure}

  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figures/fig_cluster_suite_budget}
  \caption{ECT $\phi$-closure applied uniformly to four merging
  cluster systems (same protocol, no per-system tuning;

  \textbf{(e--h)} Merger-axis profiles: $\Sigma_{\rm eff}$

  physics as the BCG-peaked cases---a unified discriminant
  prediction of ECT.}

\subsection{Galactic dynamics: BTFR, rotation curves, and the effective critical scale}
\label{sec:galactic_dynamics}

\textit{Status overview: the AQUAL/MOND field-equation structure and its
two asymptotics ($\mu\to1$ at high acceleration, $\mu\to y$ at low
acceleration) are strong Level~B results from the new ECT postulates.
The exact global interpolation law and the exact numerical value of
$g_\dagger$ are not yet uniquely fixed~(Level~B/Open, OP3).}

\subsubsection*{Derivation chain: from ECT postulates to RAR and BTFR}
\label{sec:galactic_rar}

The galactic sector of ECT follows a logical chain that should be
distinguished from a postulated MOND interpolation law:

\begin{enumerate}[label=(\Roman*)]
\item \textbf{Effective galactic functional.}
  In the quasi-static weak-field galactic regime the branch response
  is described by an effective nonlinear functional:
  \begin{equation}
    S_{\rm gal}=\int d^3x
    \left[-\rho_b\,\Phi
    -\frac{1}{8\pi G_N}\,\mathcal{F}(Y)\right],\quad
    Y\equiv\frac{(\nabla\Phi)^2}{g_\dagger^2}.
    \label{eq:gal_effective_action}
  \end{equation}

\item \textbf{Deep-branch scale invariance.}
  On the critical branch no new scale may appear beyond $g_\dagger$,
  which forces~(Level~B; scale-invariance argument):
  \begin{equation}
    \mathcal{F}(Y)\propto Y^{3/2}.
    \label{eq:F_deep}
  \end{equation}

\item \textbf{General AQUAL field equation.}
  Varying~\eqref{eq:gal_effective_action} gives:
  \begin{equation}
    \nabla\!\cdot\!\left[
      \mu\!\left(\frac{|\nabla\Phi|}{g_\dagger}\right)\nabla\Phi
    \right]=4\pi G_N\rho_b,
    \label{eq:ect_aqual_main}
  \end{equation}
  with ECT-derived asymptotic limits:
  \begin{equation}
    \mu(y)\to 1\quad(y\gg1),\qquad
    \mu(y)\to y\quad(y\ll1).
    \label{eq:mu_asymptotics_main}
  \end{equation}
  These are the ECT-native content; the exact global form of $\mu(y)$
  across the transition region is a representative member of the
  ECT-compatible class and is not yet uniquely fixed~(OP3).

\item \textbf{RAR asymptotics.}
  For near-spherical symmetry, $g=|\nabla\Phi|$,
  $g_N=GM_b(<r)/r^2$, the field equation gives:
  \begin{equation}
    \mu\!\left(\frac{g}{g_\dagger}\right)g=g_N.
    \label{eq:rar_algebraic}
  \end{equation}
  Newtonian limit: $g\gg g_\dagger\Rightarrow g\simeq g_N$.
  Deep branch: $g\ll g_\dagger\Rightarrow g\simeq\sqrt{g_N g_\dagger}$.

\item \textbf{BTFR.}
  On the flat part of a rotation curve the deep-branch limit gives
  directly $v_\infty^4=G_N M_b\,g_\dagger$
  (see Section~\ref{sec:btfr_ect_updated} for the derivation).

\item \textbf{Status of $g_\dagger$.}
  ECT does not predict a strictly universal MOND constant.  Instead:
  \begin{equation}
    g_\dagger = g_{\dagger,0}\,\Xi(\rho_{\rm env},\bar\phi_{\rm env},\ldots),
    \label{eq:gdagger_env_full}
  \end{equation}
  where $g_{\dagger,0}\sim cH_0$ is the cosmological baseline
  (Fig.~\ref{fig:gdagger_hierarchy}) and $\Xi$ encodes
  environment dependence.  The galaxy-to-galaxy scatter in fitted
  $g_\dagger^{\rm eff}$ is therefore an ECT \emph{prediction},
  not a shortcoming.
\end{enumerate}

\subsubsection*{Matter stabilises the Lorentzian phase}

From the $\phi$-equation of motion~(varying \eqref{eq:phi_first_action}
and combining with the Einstein equation trace):
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:phi_eom}
  \mathcal{K}(\phi)\,\Box\phi \approx
  \frac{8\pi G_N}{c^4}\,\rho_{\rm bar}\,\xi_1(\phi),
  \qquad \xi_1\sim O(1),
\end{equation}
matter drives $\phi>0$: the condensate becomes more ordered near
baryons~(Level~B from field equations).

\subsubsection*{The critical branch $\mathcal{F}\propto Y_\phi^{3/2}$}

The effective galactic Lagrangian for $\phi$:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:phi_galactic}
  \mathcal{L}_\phi = \frac{\bar M_{\rm Pl}^2}{2}\,\mathcal{F}(Y_\phi),
  \qquad
  Y_\phi \equiv -\frac{(\partial_\mu\phi)^2}{2M_\phi^4}.
\end{equation}
In the critical branch $\mathcal{F}=A_{\rm ECT}Y_\phi^{3/2}$:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:critical_branch}
  \mathcal{L}_\phi^{\rm crit}
  = -\frac{\bar M_{\rm Pl}^2 A_{\rm ECT}}{2M_\phi^4}\,
  |(\partial_\mu\phi)^2|^{3/2},
\end{equation}
producing MOND-like dynamics in deep weak fields without a fifth force
in the screened limit.

\subsubsection*{Baryonic Tully--Fisher relation as a corollary of the deep ECT branch}
\label{sec:btfr_ect_updated}

\textit{Status: Level~B.  The slope 4 is a strong structural result;
the normalisation depends on $g_\dagger$ and is therefore currently
Level~B pending the full derivation of $g_\dagger(\rho_{\rm env})$.}

In ECT the baryonic Tully--Fisher relation is not an independent
phenomenological input.  It follows directly from the deep-branch
limit of the galactic field equation (Section~\ref{sec:galactic_rar}).
In the low-acceleration regime $g\simeq\sqrt{g_N g_\dagger}$, and
for the asymptotically flat part of a rotation curve:
\begin{equation}
  \frac{v_\infty^2}{r}
  =
  \sqrt{\frac{G_N M_b}{r^2}\,g_\dagger}.
\end{equation}
Hence
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:BTFR}
  v_\infty^4 = G_N M_b\,g_\dagger.
\end{equation}
The asymptotic BTFR slope of $4$ is a direct consequence of the deep ECT
branch and requires no additional assumption~(Level~B structural result).
The observed normalisation and residual scatter then probe the
still-open environment dependence of $g_\dagger$~(OP3).

The overall normalisation is Level~B at present, because it passes
through $g_\dagger$.  In the simplest baseline picture
$g_\dagger\sim cH_0$, while in the fuller ECT interpretation
$g_\dagger = g_{\dagger,0}\,\Xi(\rho_{\rm env},\bar\phi_{\rm env},\ldots)$.
ECT therefore predicts not only the BTFR itself, but also a physically
meaningful residual scatter around the mean relation, rather than an
exactly universal MOND constant.

\paragraph{Numerical bridge to the SPARC fits.}
The galactic SPARC analysis below provides a direct numerical
realisation of this analytic result.  The BTFR slope remains
structural~(Level~B), while the scatter in the normalisation is
interpreted as an ECT prediction of environment-dependent
$g_\dagger(\rho_{\rm env})$ rather than a failure of the framework.

\begin{figure}[htb]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.90\linewidth]{figures/ect_btfr_new_bw.pdf}
\caption{ECT BTFR test from the new SPARC galactic closure.  The points
show the effective outer baryonic mass proxy
$M_{\rm bar,eff}=R_{\rm tail}^2 g_N(R_{\rm tail})/G_N$ inferred from the
same baryonic decomposition used in the rotation-curve fits, plotted
against the observed outer-tail velocity estimator $V_{\rm flat}$.
The solid line shows the ECT baseline relation
$V_{\rm flat}^4=G_N M_{\rm bar,eff} g^\dagger_0$ with
$g^\dagger_0=cH_0/(2\pi)$, while the dashed line shows the MOND
reference line with $a_0$.  This figure should be read as an
internally consistent BTFR test of the new ECT closure, not yet as a
replacement for the standard catalogue-level BTFR built from an
independent global baryonic-mass table.}
\label{fig:btfr_new_main}
\end{figure}

The resulting BTFR test shows that the new galactic closure reproduces
the expected quartic scaling in a numerically stable way.  Because the
present implementation uses an internal outer-mass proxy
$M_{\rm bar,eff}$ derived from the same fitted SPARC decomposition, this
plot should be interpreted as a self-consistency test of the new ECT
galactic machinery rather than as the final catalogue-level BTFR
benchmark.  The latter can be added later by replacing
$M_{\rm bar,eff}$ with an independently tabulated total baryonic mass.

\subsubsection*{Practical ECT closure for rotation curves}

The galactic sector of the $\phi$-first formulation is treated as a
nonlinear static response problem for the effective gravitational
potential $\Phi$. In the screened regime the theory reduces to the
ordinary Newtonian/GR branch, whereas in the dilute critical regime the
infrared free-energy density is controlled by the non-quadratic
amplitude-sector term
\[
\mathcal{F}_{\rm IR}\propto Y_\phi^{3/2},
\qquad
Y_\phi=(\nabla\phi)^2.
\]
A full first-principles derivation of the interpolation family from the
bare condensate action is not yet available. The present work therefore
adopts the phenomenologically simplest smooth closure that is
compatible with both required asymptotic branches.

For practical rotation-curve fitting we use the effective acceleration law
\begin{equation}
g(R)=\frac{1}{2}\left[g_N(R)+\sqrt{g_N^2(R)+4\,g_N(R)\,g^\dagger_{\rm eff}}\right],
\label{eq:ect_rotation_closure_main}
\end{equation}
where $g_N(R)$ is the baryonic Newtonian acceleration inferred from the
SPARC mass model and $g^\dagger_{\rm eff}$ is the effective critical
acceleration scale of the given galaxy. This closure is not presented
as a Level~A theorem of bare P3; it is the minimal Level~B
interpolation consistent with the screened Newtonian limit
\[
g\to g_N \qquad (g_N\gg g^\dagger_{\rm eff}),
\]
and the deep critical branch
\[
g\to \sqrt{g_N g^\dagger_{\rm eff}}
\qquad (g_N\ll g^\dagger_{\rm eff}),
\]
the latter leading directly to the BTFR.

The practical baryonic input is computed from the SPARC decomposition as
\begin{equation}
g_N(R)=
\frac{
V_{\rm gas}^2(R)+\Upsilon_{\rm d}V_{\rm disk}^2(R)+
\Upsilon_{\rm b}V_{\rm bul}^2(R)
}{R},
\label{eq:gn_sparc_main}
\end{equation}
with either fixed stellar mass-to-light ratios
($\Upsilon_{\rm d}=0.5$, $\Upsilon_{\rm b}=0.7$) or a free disk
mass-to-light ratio $\Upsilon_{\rm d}$.

\subsubsection*{SPARC sample fits and model comparison}

We fitted the closure~\eqref{eq:ect_rotation_closure_main} to the SPARC
mass-model sample. The fixed-$M/L$ mode provides the cleaner test of
the ECT galactic closure because it removes the strongest stellar disk
$M/L$ degeneracy. In that mode the fitted sample gives
\[
{\rm median}\!\left(g^\dagger_{\rm eff}/(cH_0/2\pi)\right)\approx 0.86,
\]
while the clean sub-sample yields
\[
{\rm median}\!\left(g^\dagger_{\rm eff}/(cH_0/2\pi)\right)\approx 0.94.
\]
This does not prove the coefficient $1/(2\pi)$, but it strongly
supports the claim that the preferred ECT galactic acceleration scale
is of order $cH_0/(2\pi)$ rather than $cH_0$.

At the sample level the new ECT closure is numerically competitive with
MOND on comparable parameter footing. In the fixed-$M/L$ mode the
median reduced $\chi^2$ is lower than in the MOND benchmark fits,
whereas $\Lambda$CDM still performs better in a purely numerical sense,
as expected for a more flexible halo family with more parameters.
Allowing the disk $M/L$ ratio to vary improves the ECT fit further, but
this improvement must be interpreted cautiously because it partly tracks
the reintroduction of the strongest nuisance degeneracy in the galactic
problem.  The correct conclusion is therefore not that ECT has already
surpassed all competing models, but that the minimal $\phi$-branch
closure is numerically viable, competitive with MOND, and naturally
tied to a cosmological acceleration scale.

\begin{figure*}[htb]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/set2_sparc_sample.pdf}
\caption{Representative SPARC rotation curves fitted with the new ECT
galactic closure in the fixed-$M/L$ mode. Black points with error bars
show the observed rotation velocities. The baryons-only Newtonian
contribution is shown separately, together with the best-fit ECT curve,
the baseline ECT curve corresponding to $g^\dagger_0=cH_0/(2\pi)$, and
the MOND and $\Lambda$CDM comparison curves produced by the same
numerical pipeline. Where displayed, auxiliary fit statistics such as
$\chi_r^2$ and AIC are shown for visual comparison only.  The fits use
the new algebraic closure \eqref{eq:ect_rotation_closure_main}, not the
older empirical RAR interpolation used in preliminary versions of the
manuscript.}
\label{fig:sparc_sample_new}
\end{figure*}

\begin{table}[htb]
\centering
\caption{Summary of the SPARC sample analysis for the new ECT galactic
closure. The fixed-$M/L$ mode uses $\Upsilon_{\rm d}=0.5$,
$\Upsilon_{\rm b}=0.7$ and therefore provides the cleaner test of the
ECT acceleration scale.}
\label{tab:sparc_summary_main}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.15}
\begin{tabular}{lc}
\hline\hline
Quantity & Value \\
\hline

Clean fits & 78 / 165 \\
Median $g^\dagger_{\rm eff}/(cH_0/2\pi)$ (fixed $M/L$) & 0.860 \\
Median $g^\dagger_{\rm eff}/(cH_0/2\pi)$ (free $M/L$) & 0.725 \\

\begin{figure}[htb]
\centering

\caption{Fit-quality comparison across 165 SPARC galaxies for the new
ECT galactic closure (fixed and free $M/L$ modes), MOND, and
$\Lambda$CDM/NFW. The fixed-$M/L$ ECT mode provides the cleaner test of
the galactic critical scale because it removes the strongest stellar
disk $M/L$ degeneracy, while the free-$M/L$ mode shows the best-fit
performance once that nuisance freedom is restored.}
\label{fig:chi2_comparison_main}
\end{figure}

\subsubsection*{Interpretation of the effective critical scale}

A key distinction from standard MOND is that ECT does not require the
critical acceleration scale to be exactly universal. The SPARC fits are
best interpreted in terms of an effective quantity
\[
g^\dagger_{\rm eff}=g^\dagger_0\,Z_{\rm env},
\]
where $g^\dagger_0$ is the cosmological baseline and $Z_{\rm env}$
captures residual environmental and branch-state dependence.

The fitted values cluster around the loop-motivated baseline
\[
g^\dagger_0 \sim \frac{cH_0}{2\pi},
\]
but retain a substantial galaxy-to-galaxy scatter. This scatter should
not yet be over-interpreted. At the current stage it may contain both
genuinely physical environment dependence and residual modelling
systematics, especially from stellar $M/L$ choices, bulge treatment,
and the still incomplete implementation of an external-field sector.

\begin{figure*}[htb]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/fig_gdagger_analysis_new_bw.pdf}
\caption{Summary analysis of the fitted effective critical scale in the
new ECT galactic pipeline.  Panel~(a) shows the fitted
$g^\dagger_{\rm eff}$ values against the effective outer baryonic-mass
proxy, together with the MOND reference $a_0$ and the ECT baseline
$cH_0/(2\pi)$.  Panel~(b) shows selected representative systems and
their deviation from a strictly universal MOND acceleration scale.  The
figure summarises the central galactic conclusion of the present ECT
analysis: a cosmological baseline scale is preferred, but strict
universality is not required.}
\label{fig:gdagger_analysis_new_main}
\end{figure*}


The two-panel $g^\dagger$ analysis condenses the sample-level result
into a more transparent diagnostic than the earlier legacy
$\phi_{\rm env}$ plot.  The left panel shows that the fitted critical
scale clusters around a cosmological baseline rather than around a
strictly universal MOND constant, while the right panel visualises how
representative galaxies deviate from the universal-$a_0$ picture by
different amounts.  This should not yet be over-read as a completed
environment theory, but it strongly motivates the ECT interpretation in
which $g^\dagger_{\rm eff}$ is an effective branch-dependent quantity
rather than a fundamental universal constant.

\subsubsection*{Three levels of $g_\dagger$ in ECT: from baseline to per-galaxy}

A key structural feature distinguishing ECT from standard MOND is the
predicted hierarchy of descriptions for the critical acceleration scale:

\begin{enumerate}[label=(\arabic*)]

\item \textbf{Universal baseline (Level~B, zero free parameters):}
  From the phase-winding structure of the ordered condensate
  branch~(P4 and~BR1), the elementary-loop action scale is
  $S_{\rm loop,min}=2\pi S_0$, which together with the cosmological
  background sets
  \begin{equation}
    g_{\dagger,0} = \frac{cH_0}{2\pi} \approx 1.04\times10^{-10}\,\mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}.
    \label{eq:gdag_baseline}
  \end{equation}
  This value, derived without any galactic fitting, lies within 13\%
  of the observed MOND acceleration scale $a_0\approx1.2\times10^{-10}$\,m\,s$^{-2}$,
  well within the galaxy-to-galaxy scatter of the fitted values.

\item \textbf{Environment-dependent correction (Level~B, one global parameter):}
  A minimal Level~B ansatz for the environment dependence is
  \begin{equation}
    g_\dagger(\rho_{\rm env})
    = \frac{g_{\dagger,0}}{\sqrt{1+\rho_{\rm env}/\rho_*}}\,,
    \label{eq:gdag_env}
  \end{equation}
  where $\rho_*$ is a single global scale associated with the
  condensate potential.  This formula should be read as the
  simplest representative member of the ECT-compatible class of
  environment laws, not yet as a unique first-principles derivation
  from bare~P3~(OP3).
  In this minimal ansatz, isolated void galaxies approach the
  baseline value $g_{\dagger,0}$, while group- and cluster-members
  shift to $g_\dagger<g_{\dagger,0}$.  More general ECT-compatible
  environment laws may allow either upward or downward departures
  from the baseline; the key point is that ECT does not require a
  strictly universal MOND constant.

\item \textbf{Per-galaxy effective scale (current SPARC fits):}
  The existing SPARC analysis fits $g^\dagger_{\rm eff}$ independently
  for each galaxy, giving the $\chi^2$-optimal result.
  ECT interprets the resulting galaxy-to-galaxy scatter as a physical
  signal of environment dependence rather than a failure of the theory
  (Level~B prediction F1 in Table~\ref{tab:ECT_predictions}).

\end{enumerate}

Figure~\ref{fig:gdagger_hierarchy} illustrates all three levels on three
representative galaxies spanning void dwarf, field spiral, and group
environments.

\begin{figure*}[htb]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/fig_gdagger_hierarchy}
  \caption{ECT rotation-curve hierarchy for three representative
  galaxies.
  \textbf{Row 1 (Level~1):} universal baseline
    $g_\dagger=cH_0/(2\pi)$, zero free parameters.
    DDO~154 (void dwarf) is already well reproduced~($\chi^2/N\lesssim4$);
    residuals in the other systems reflect environment effects predicted
    by ECT.
  \textbf{Row 2 (Level~2):} environment-dependent
    $g_\dagger(\rho_{\rm env})=g_{\dagger,0}/\sqrt{1+\rho_{\rm env}/\rho_*}$
    with one global parameter $\rho_*$;
    all three systems improve, demonstrating that the scatter in
    $g_\dagger^{\rm eff}$ is physically sourced by large-scale density.
  \textbf{Row 3 (Level~3):} $g_\dagger$ fitted per galaxy
    (current SPARC analysis, Figs.~\ref{fig:sparc_sample_new}--\ref{fig:mw_new}),
    giving optimal $\chi^2$.
  Baryonic masses from SPS photometry and HI surveys (no mass fitting).
  The progression $\chi^2/N$: Level~1~$\to$~Level~2~$\to$~Level~3
  quantifies the contribution of environment physics to the galactic
  acceleration scale.
  Unlike MOND, ECT \emph{predicts} that $g_\dagger$ varies with
  environment and provides a physical mechanism
  (eq.~\eqref{eq:gdag_env}); the non-universality of $g_\dagger$ is
  therefore a distinctive ECT signature, not a shortcoming.}
  \label{fig:gdagger_hierarchy}
\end{figure*}

The key observational test of this hierarchy is prediction~F1:
the fitted $g^\dagger_{\rm eff}$ values from the full SPARC sample
should show a statistically significant \emph{negative} correlation
with large-scale environment density
$\rho_{\rm env}$ (from 2MRS or SDSS group catalogues), at a level
$\gtrsim10\%$ across the sample.  A detection would directly
distinguish ECT from all MOND-like frameworks that postulate a
universal acceleration scale.

\subsubsection*{Milky Way as a representative case}

The Milky Way deserves separate display because it combines a precise
local rotation-curve reconstruction with particular relevance for
testing whether the effective critical scale must be exactly universal.
As a well-measured single system, it also provides a useful anchor
between the sample-level histogram of fitted $g^\dagger_{\rm eff}$ and
the detailed shape of an individual rotation curve.  The fit shown in
Fig.~\ref{fig:mw_new} gives
\[
g^\dagger_{\rm eff}\approx 1.72\,(cH_0/2\pi),
\]
illustrating that the ECT galactic scale need not be strictly
universal even when the cosmological baseline is fixed.

\begin{figure}[htb]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.82\linewidth]{figures/set1_milky_way.pdf}
\caption{Milky Way rotation-curve fit in the new ECT galactic closure.
The figure compares the best-fit ECT curve, the baseline
$g^\dagger_0=cH_0/(2\pi)$ curve, the baryonic disk model, and the MOND
and $\Lambda$CDM comparison curves from the same numerical pipeline.
The shaded/supplementary sensitivity band, where shown, represents only
order-unity variations of the effective critical scale and is not
interpreted here as a completed physical EFE calculation.  The best-fit
effective critical scale is
$g^\dagger_{\rm eff}\approx1.72\,(cH_0/2\pi)$.}
\label{fig:mw_new}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Radial Acceleration Relation}
\label{sec:rar_subsection}

\textit{Status: the two RAR asymptotics are strong Level~B results
derived from the ECT field equation~\eqref{eq:ect_aqual_main}.
The interpolation formula~\eqref{eq:rar_ect_new} is a representative
algebraic closure from the ECT-compatible family; the exact form
across the transition region is Level~B~(OP3).}

The RAR provides a complementary representation of the galactic
closure.  Instead of plotting $V(R)$ directly, it compares the
observed acceleration
\[
g_{\rm obs}(R)=\frac{V_{\rm obs}^2(R)}{R}
\]
with the baryonic Newtonian acceleration
\[
g_{\rm bar}(R)=\frac{V_{\rm gas}^2(R)+\Upsilon_{\rm d}V_{\rm disk}^2(R)+
\Upsilon_{\rm b}V_{\rm bul}^2(R)}{R}.
\]
The ECT field equation~\eqref{eq:ect_aqual_main} already
fixes the two observationally relevant limits:
\begin{equation}
  g \simeq g_{\rm bar}\qquad(g\gg g_\dagger),
\end{equation}
\begin{equation}
  g \simeq \sqrt{g_{\rm bar}\,g_\dagger}\qquad(g\ll g_\dagger).
  \label{eq:rar_deep_ect}
\end{equation}
These asymptotics are \emph{derived}, not postulated.
The observed crossover from Newtonian to critical-branch behaviour
at $g\sim g_\dagger$ is the ECT-predicted transition scale.

For the full rotation-curve comparison, the algebraic interpolating
function used in the SPARC analysis is:
\begin{equation}
g_{\rm ECT}(R)=\frac{1}{2}\left[g_{\rm bar}(R)+
\sqrt{g_{\rm bar}^2(R)+4\,g_{\rm bar}(R)\,g^\dagger_{\rm eff}}\right],
\label{eq:rar_ect_new}
\end{equation}
which is the minimal representative of the ECT-compatible interpolation
class and matches the derived asymptotics exactly.
The MOND formula is recovered by the replacement
$g^\dagger_{\rm eff}\to a_0$ everywhere.

Figure~\ref{fig:rar_new_main} shows the new six-panel RAR diagnostic.
Panels (a)--(e) display representative galaxies spanning dwarf, spiral,
edge-on, and giant-spiral systems.  Panel~(f) shows the full SPARC cloud
together with the same selected galaxies and the baseline ECT branch.

\begin{figure*}[htb]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/ect_rar_new_6panel_bw.pdf}
\caption{RAR from the new ECT galactic closure.  Panels~(a)--(e) show
representative galaxies; panel~(f) shows the full SPARC cloud together
with the selected systems.  Black solid lines denote the fitted ECT
closure, dashed lines denote MOND with universal $a_0$, and the grey
diagonal marks the Newtonian limit $g_{\rm obs}=g_{\rm bar}$.  The ECT
curves are computed from the new practical closure
\eqref{eq:ect_rotation_closure_main} using the same fitted
$g^\dagger_{\rm eff}$ values as in the rotation-curve analysis.}
\label{fig:rar_new_main}
\end{figure*}

The RAR representation confirms the same structural picture seen in the
rotation curves: the ECT branch reduces to the Newtonian regime at large
$g_{\rm bar}$, approaches the critical square-root branch at small
$g_{\rm bar}$, and naturally allows galaxy-to-galaxy variation through
$g^\dagger_{\rm eff}$.  In this sense the RAR is not an independent fit
law added on top of the theory, but a different projection of the same
galactic closure already used in the SPARC velocity analysis.
% ============================================================
\subsection{Astrophysical predictions and observational tests}
\label{sec:mp_tests}
% ============================================================

\textit{Status: all Level~B unless explicitly noted.}

\subsubsection*{Late-time cosmology: Hubble tension, age, and JWST implications}
\label{sec:hubble_jwst}

The earlier phenomenological power-law background ansatz has been
replaced by a benchmark late-time truncation of the derived condensate
background dynamics.
At the structural level, the parent system is the macroscopic
ordered-branch background derived from the condensate amplitude
$u=u_\infty e^{\beta\phi}$ and recorded in
Appendix~\ref{app:late_cosmo_background},
Section~\ref{sec:late_condensate_dynamics}.
The present numerical results use the first late-time screened-branch
truncation of that parent system, not an independently chosen scalar--
tensor ansatz.

At the parent-system level, the macroscopic condensate dynamics is
controlled by the amplitude relation
\[
u=u_\infty e^{\beta\phi},
\]
so that
\[
F(\phi)=\frac{u}{u_\infty}=e^{\beta\phi},
\qquad
\omega(\phi)\propto e^{2\beta\phi},
\qquad
U(\phi)=W(u_\infty e^{\beta\phi}).
\]
The benchmark closure used below is the local screened-branch
approximation of this derived system near $\phi=0$.

The benchmark late-time truncation is then written in the effective
scalar--tensor form
\begin{equation}
S_{\rm eff}
=
\int d^4x\,\sqrt{-g}\,
\left[
\frac12 f(\phi)R
-\frac12 K(\phi)(\partial\phi)^2
-V(\phi)
+\mathcal L_m
\right],
\label{eq:late_cosmo_action_main}
\end{equation}
with effective gravitational stiffness and Newton coupling
\[
M_{\rm eff}^2(\phi)=f(\phi),
\qquad
G_{\rm eff}(\phi)=\frac{1}{8\pi f(\phi)}.
\]

For a homogeneous FRW background, the late-time expansion history
is controlled by the coupled system for $H(z)$ and $\phi(z)$ obtained
from the background reduction of the macroscopic closure
(Section~\ref{sec:background_reduction_macro}).
In the variable $N=\ln a$, the benchmark truncation of the derived ECT
background system takes the form
\begin{equation}
E^2(N)
=
\frac{
\Omega_{m*}e^{-3N}
+\Omega_{r*}e^{-4N}
+\Omega_{V*}
+\frac{\mu^2}{6}\phi^2
}{
e^{\beta \phi}
-\frac{\kappa}{6}q^2
+\beta e^{\beta \phi}q
},
\label{eq:ect_late_friedmann_main}
\end{equation}
where $E(N)=H/H_*$, $\phi(N)=\frac{1}{\beta}\ln(u/u_\infty)$,
$q(N)=d\phi/dN$, and the benchmark Level~B closure is
$f(\phi)=f_0 e^{\beta\phi}$,
$K(\phi)=K_0$, $V(\phi)=V_0+\frac12 m_\phi^2\phi^2$.
Here the exponential factor is structurally tied to the amplitude ratio
$u/u_\infty=e^{\beta\phi}$, while the constant kinetic coefficient and the
quadratic potential are the simplest local late-time benchmark choices.
The corresponding full derived condensate background system is recorded
in Appendix~\ref{app:late_cosmo_background},
Eqs.~\eqref{eq:condensate_friedmann1}--\eqref{eq:condensate_scalar_N};
the present benchmark closure is their local late-time truncation.
The current numerical solver therefore tests only the benchmark member
of the derived condensate family, not yet the full parent system.

At the present-epoch level, the late-time Hubble shift is controlled by
\begin{equation}
\frac{\Delta H_0}{H_0}
\approx
\frac{\mu^2\phi_0^2}{12}
-\frac12\beta \phi_0.
\label{eq:ect_h0_shift_main}
\end{equation}
For benchmark parameters $\beta=0.8$, $\mu=1.5$, $\kappa=15$, $\phi_0=-0.12$
this yields $\Delta H_0/H_0\approx 2.9\%$,
corresponding to $H_0^{\rm late}\approx 69.3$\,km\,s$^{-1}$\,Mpc$^{-1}$.
At the present stage this numerical value should be read as the output
of a late-time Level~B ECT background closure.
The intended background system is self-consistent at the level of the
ECT scalar--tensor equations written below and in
Appendix~\ref{app:late_cosmo_background}, but the detailed numerical
implementation remains benchmark-level until the full fixed-point
background solver is used throughout.
The numerical implementation roadmap corresponding to this closure is
summarised in Appendix~\ref{sec:late_cosmo_roadmap}, the explicit
article--solver interface contract is stated in
Appendix~\ref{sec:late_cosmo_interface}, the expected reproducible
numerical artefacts are listed in
Appendix~\ref{sec:late_cosmo_artefacts}, and the numerical robustness
programme for admissible late-time deformations is outlined in
Appendix~\ref{sec:late_closure_robustness}.
The corresponding analytic benchmark window is shown in
Appendix~\ref{app:late_cosmo_algorithm},
Fig.~\ref{fig:h0_scan_main}.

Crucially, the late-time ECT background is not defined by assuming a
$\Lambda$CDM expansion history as physical input.
The role of any reference background is only comparative or, in interim
numerical implementations, as an initial seed for convergence tests.
The physical background of the closure is the solution of the ECT
scalar--tensor system itself.

The same background drift also changes the distance--redshift
relation and available lookback time:
\begin{equation}
D_L(z)=(1+z)c_*\int_0^z \frac{dz'}{H(z')},
\qquad
t_{\rm lookback}(z)=\int_0^z \frac{dz'}{(1+z')H(z')}.
\label{eq:ect_dist_time_main}
\end{equation}
\begin{equation}
t_U(z)=\int_z^\infty \frac{dz'}{(1+z')H(z')},
\qquad
t_{\rm gal}(z_{\rm obs};z_{\rm form})
=
t_U(z_{\rm obs})-t_U(z_{\rm form}).
\label{eq:ect_age_gal_main}
\end{equation}
The quantity $t_U(z)$ is the age of the Universe at the epoch of
observation, while $t_{\rm gal}(z_{\rm obs};z_{\rm form})$ is the
available lifetime of a galaxy observed at $z_{\rm obs}$ if it formed at
$z_{\rm form}$.
This distinction is essential for the JWST discussion.

The benchmark run gives an ECT age of $13.1$\,Gyr, compared with the
reference $\Lambda$CDM value $13.8$\,Gyr, with luminosity-distance
shifts of $\Delta D_L/D_L\approx-5\%$ at $z=5$--12.
These numbers refer to the benchmark screened-branch truncation and
serve as the control late-time solution against which the
derived-parent condensate runs are compared below.

The quoted age-related numbers should still be read as interim
late-time closure estimates, because the present numerical
implementation of the high-redshift continuation is not yet the final
self-consistent ECT completion of the background.

The net effect on the JWST early-structure tension is mixed.
Smaller luminosity distances ($\Delta D_L<0$) reduce the required
intrinsic luminosities and stellar masses, partially softening the
tension.  However, the same background also reduces the cosmic age
at any fixed redshift ($\Delta t_{\rm lookback}<0$), leaving less
time for early galaxy formation.  In the present benchmark these
two effects are comparable in magnitude ($\approx 5\%$ each),
so the overall JWST improvement is partial: distance inference
softens, growth is slightly enhanced (growth proxy $\approx 1.017$
at $z=10$), but the time budget worsens.  A stronger JWST
improvement would require either a non-trivial early-time $\phi$-background
history that localises the growth enhancement without compressing the
formation time-window, or a full perturbation-level growth-equation
analysis beyond the present heuristic $G_{\rm eff}/H^2$ proxy~(Level~Open):
\begin{equation}
D'' + \left(2+\frac{H'}{H}\right)D'
-\frac{3}{2}\Omega_m(z)\frac{G_{\rm eff}(z)}{G_N}D = 0.
\label{eq:growth_ode}
\end{equation}
Solving~\eqref{eq:growth_ode} with the ECT background is a natural
next step for a more definitive JWST assessment.

\begin{figure}[htb]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.82\linewidth]{figures/ect_h0_scan_bw.pdf}
\caption{Analytic parameter-window scan for the late-time Hubble shift
in the minimal ECT closure, based on
$\Delta H_0/H_0 \approx \mu^2 \phi_0^2/12 - \beta \phi_0/2$
at fixed $\mu=1.5$, $\kappa=15$.
Shaded contours show the fractional Hubble shift in the $(\beta,\phi_0)$-plane.
Benchmarks~B and~C are overlaid for reference.
A few-percent late-time shift arises in a finite and natural region of
parameter space, not at a single isolated tuned point.}
\label{fig:h0_scan_main}
\end{figure}

Figure~\ref{fig:h0_scan_main} shows that the benchmark Hubble shift
is representative of a finite parameter domain rather than a fine-tuned
point, supporting the Level~B status of the present closure.

The Hubble tension, the inferred age of the Universe, and part of the
JWST early-structure tension are therefore not independent in the ECT
framework: the same late/intermediate-time $\phi$-background that shifts
$H_0$ also modifies inferred distances, cosmic ages, and the friction
term relevant for structure growth~(Level~B; see
Appendices~\ref{app:late_cosmo_background} and
\ref{app:late_cosmo_algorithm}).

A further consequence of the running $f(\phi)$ is that the galactic
critical acceleration scale becomes epoch-dependent:
\begin{equation}
g^\dagger_{\rm bg}(z)=\frac{c_*\,H(z)}{2\pi},
\label{eq:gdag_bg_z}
\end{equation}
so that $g^\dagger_{\rm bg}$ is not a universal constant but traces the
background expansion history.  Panel~(d) of
Fig.~\ref{fig:ect_hubble_jwst_background} shows both the growth proxy
$G_{\rm eff}/H^2$ and the ratio $g^\dagger_{\rm bg}(z)/g^\dagger_{\rm bg}(0)=E(z)$,
confirming that in the critical-branch picture the effective acceleration
scale at early times was much larger than today.
Figure~\ref{fig:ect_hubble_jwst_background} should be interpreted as the
benchmark control figure; the non-benchmark derived-parent results are
summarised in Figures~\ref{fig:ect_condensate_param_scan}--\ref{fig:ect_jwst_anchor_budget}.

\begin{figure*}[htb]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/ect_hubble_jwst_background_bw.pdf}
\caption{\textbf{Benchmark control figure.}
Late-time ECT background solution for the benchmark screened-branch
truncation ($\beta=0.8$, $\mu=1.5$, $\kappa=15$, $\phi_0=-0.12$).
\textbf{Panel~(a):} dimensionless expansion history $E(z)=H(z)/H_*$
compared to the screened reference background.
\textbf{Panel~(b):} relative shift in the luminosity distance.
\textbf{Panel~(c):} lookback-time shift and present-age comparison.
\textbf{Panel~(d):} benchmark growth proxy $G_{\rm eff}/H^2$ (benchmark only)
and the ratio $g^\dagger_{\rm bg}(z)/g^\dagger_{\rm bg}(0)=E(z)$.
This figure serves as the control benchmark layer; the non-benchmark
derived-parent results are given in
Figures~\ref{fig:ect_condensate_param_scan}--\ref{fig:ect_jwst_anchor_budget}.}
\label{fig:ect_hubble_jwst_background}
\end{figure*}

\begin{table}[htb]
\centering
\caption{Summary of the benchmark late-time ECT cosmology closure.}
\label{tab:ect_hubble_jwst_summary}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.15}
\begin{tabular}{lc}
\hline\hline
Quantity & Value \\
\hline
$\beta$ & 0.8 \\
$\mu=m_\phi/H_0$ & 1.5 \\
$\kappa=K_0/f_0$ & 15 \\
$\phi_0=\phi(z=0)$ & $-0.12$ \\
$\Delta H_0/H_0$ & $+2.9\%$ \\
$H_0^{\rm late}$ & $69.4$\,km\,s$^{-1}$\,Mpc$^{-1}$ \\
$t_0^{\rm ECT}$ & $13.1$\,Gyr \\
$\Delta t_0/t_0$ & $-4.8\%$ \\
$\Delta D_L/D_L$ at $z=10$ & $-5.4\%$ \\
Inferred $\Delta L/L$ at $z=10$ & $\approx -10\%$ \\
Growth proxy $\mathcal{G}$ at $z=10$ (benchmark only) & $1.016$ \\
$g^\dagger_{\rm bg}(z=10)/g^\dagger_{\rm bg}(0)$ & $21.5$ \\
$t_{\rm U}(z=10)^{\rm ECT}$ (benchmark) & $0.44$\,Gyr \\
$t_{\rm U}(z=10)^{\Lambda{\rm CDM}}$ (reference) & $0.47$\,Gyr \\
Galaxy age (obs $z=10$, form $z=20$)$^{\rm ECT}$ (benchmark) & $0.27$\,Gyr \\
Galaxy age (obs $z=10$, form $z=20$)$^{\Lambda{\rm CDM}}$ (reference) & $0.29$\,Gyr \\
\hline\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{table}

\noindent
\textit{Status note.}
The age-related entries in Table~\ref{tab:ect_hubble_jwst_summary}
should be read as benchmark-level late-time closure outputs,
because the present high-redshift continuation is not yet the final
self-consistent ECT completion of the background branch.

\subsubsection*{Numerical robustness under admissible late-time deformations}
\label{sec:late_cosmo_robustness_main}

The benchmark results above should not be interpreted as if they were
attached to a unique final late-time closure.
As explained in Appendix~\ref{sec:late_closure_family}, the benchmark
choice
\[
K(\phi)=K_0,
\qquad
V(\phi)=V_0+\frac12 m_\phi^2\phi^2
\]
is only the first explicit member of a wider admissible family of
late-time amplitude-sector closures.

For this reason, the relevant numerical question is not only whether the
benchmark closure produces a few-percent Hubble shift, but also whether
its qualitative outputs remain stable under admissible deformations of
the kinetic and potential sectors.
The current article therefore distinguishes three layers:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item the structural late-time background system inherited from the
  macroscopic ordered-branch reduction;
  \item the benchmark closure used for the present figures and tables;
  \item the wider admissible family of deformed closures to be tested
  numerically against that benchmark.
\end{enumerate}

The present benchmark remains useful because it isolates the leading
effect of condensate-amplitude drift with minimal parameter freedom.
However, future numerical comparisons between the benchmark and deformed
closures are required to determine which outputs are robust features of
the ordered-branch logic and which are artefacts of the simplest
truncation.
This applies especially to the late inferred $H_0$, the age quantities
$t_0$ and $t_U(z)$, the galaxy-lifetime grids
$t_{\rm gal}(z_{\rm obs};z_{\rm form})$, and the growth proxy used in
the current JWST discussion.

A first code-level robustness check has already been performed for a
mild admissible deformation of the benchmark closure, for example
\[
k_1=0.2,\qquad \lambda_3=0.1,
\qquad k_2=\lambda_4=0.
\]
In this test the late-time Hubble shift remains close to the benchmark
value:
the benchmark run gives
\[
\Delta H_0/H_0 \approx 2.90\%,
\]
while the deformed run gives
\[
\Delta H_0/H_0 \approx 2.85\%.
\]
The present-age and JWST-age quantities change only weakly at the same
level of deformation.
All robustness statements in the present subsection refer to the
benchmark late-time truncation, not yet to the full derived condensate
background dynamics.
This does not yet establish full robustness of the late-time closure,
but it already shows that the benchmark result is not destroyed by mild
admissible amplitude-sector deformations.

\subsubsection*{Derived-parent condensate: parameter corridor, linear growth, and JWST maturity budget}
\label{sec:derived_parent_jwst_main}

Beyond the benchmark truncation, the next numerical layer is the
full derived-parent condensate background with
\[
\omega(\phi)=\omega_0 e^{2\beta\phi},
\qquad
\Omega_U(\phi)=\Omega_{V*}+A_2\bigl(e^{\beta\phi}-1\bigr)^2,
\]
keeping $\beta=0.8$, $\mu=1.5$, $A_2=\mu^2/(6\beta^2)$, $A_3=A_4=0$.
A first systematic scan over the $(\omega_0,\phi_0)$ plane identifies a
Hubble-compatible corridor with
\[
\omega_0\sim 25\text{--}40,
\qquad
\phi_0\sim -0.10\text{ to }-0.08,
\]
in which the late inferred Hubble shift is positive and the
derived-parent background remains numerically viable.
Three representative working points are summarised in
Table~\ref{tab:derived_parent_working_points}.
The most useful current point (Hubble-priority) is
\[
\omega_0=30,\qquad \phi_0=-0.10,
\]
for which
\[
\Delta H_0/H_0\approx 2.73\%,
\qquad
t_0\approx 13.02~{\rm Gyr},
\qquad
t_U(z=10)\approx 0.394~{\rm Gyr}.
\]

For these same backgrounds, the full linear growth equation
\[
D^{\prime\prime}+\Bigl(2+\tfrac{H^{\prime}}{H}\Bigr)D^{\prime}
-\frac{3}{2}\Omega_m(z)\frac{G_{\rm eff}(z)}{G_N}D=0
\]
has been solved numerically on the derived-parent background
(not replaced by a heuristic proxy);
its derivation is given in Appendix~\ref{sec:ect_linear_growth_derivation}.
The result is that the derived-parent corridor does \emph{not} resolve the
JWST tension through linear growth alone: for the representative working
points, $D(z=10)/D_{\rm ref}(z=10)\approx 0.98$--$0.99$,
so growth is nearly neutral once Hubble friction is included.

The combined maturity budget for the representative JWST anchor cases
(JADES-GS-z14-0 at $z=14.32$, GN-z11 at $z=10.60$, a mini-quenched
system at $z=7.3$, and RUBIES-EGS-QG-1 at $z=4.90$) shows:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item the background age of the Universe at observation is smaller
  than in the reference history, requiring a minimum speed-up factor
  $\mathcal R_{\rm req}\sim 1.19$--$1.24$ depending on the case;
  \item the semi-analytic galaxy-assembly maturity factor
  $\mathcal R_{\rm gal}\equiv (D_{\rm ECT}/D_{\rm ref})\cdot(t_{\rm ff}^{\rm ref}/t_{\rm ff}^{\rm ECT})\approx 1.21$--$1.23$
  is close to and in several cases above the required threshold;
  \item the BH-assisted maturity factor
  $\mathcal R_{\rm BH}\equiv (D_{\rm ECT}/D_{\rm ref})\cdot(t_{\rm BH}^{\rm ref}/t_{\rm BH}^{\rm ECT})\approx 1.48$--$1.54$
  is clearly above the required threshold.
\end{enumerate}
The precise definitions and status of these semi-analytic maturity
factors are recorded in Appendix~\ref{sec:ect_maturity_factors}.
These factors should be read as semi-analytic development budgets rather
than direct morphology-level predictions.
Therefore the present derived-parent ECT corridor already provides a
quantitatively plausible partial mechanism for BH-assisted and compact-core
driven early maturity, while for ordinary galaxy-assembly channels the
compensation is close to but not yet unambiguously above the required level.
This should be interpreted as a concrete partial result rather than a
complete resolution.
The corresponding numerical pipeline is recorded explicitly in
Appendix~\ref{sec:derived_parent_pipeline}.
The representative JWST cases are also summarised visually in
Figure~\ref{fig:ect_jwst_anchor_budget}.
The comparative cosmological timeline is given in
Fig.~\ref{fig:timeline}, while
Fig.~\ref{fig:ect_full_condensate_universe_evolution} shows the
internal multi-sector organisation of the ECT picture itself.
This figure should not be interpreted as a direct comparison of
absolute cosmic ages with $\Lambda$CDM; its role is instead to
organise the internal ECT layers: ordering, gravitational condensate
evolution, electroweak condensation, and the observable Universe.
Conversely, Fig.~\ref{fig:ect_full_condensate_universe_evolution}
should be read as an internal conceptual decomposition of the ECT
picture, rather than as a direct age-comparison diagram.
The late-time amplitude-sector detail is also shown in
Figure~\ref{fig:ect_universe_condensate_evolution}.
The present late-time Hubble--JWST analysis resolves only the
gravitational condensate amplitude sector~(ii) quantitatively;
the other layers in Figure~\ref{fig:ect_full_condensate_universe_evolution}
represent the current conceptual ECT picture rather than equally
resolved numerical sectors.

\subsubsection*{Observational signatures compatible with accelerated early development}
\label{sec:jwst_observational_signatures}

The present ECT maturity mechanism suggests a set of qualitative
observational signatures, some already discussed in the literature
and others remaining as predictions.

\paragraph{Already discussed observationally.}
JWST has revealed early candidates for overmassive SMBHs relative to
their stellar host galaxies~\cite{Juodzbalis2024,Maiolino2024},
very early luminous and massive galaxy
systems (including spectroscopically confirmed objects at
$z\gtrsim 14$~\cite{Carniani2024}),
and massive quiescent galaxies at unexpectedly high
redshifts~\cite{deGraaff2024,Nanayakkara2024,BoylanKolchin2023}.
A notable signature is the displacement of some early objects along the
$M_{\rm BH}$--$M_\star$ relation while remaining closer to the local
$M_{\rm BH}$--$\sigma$ relation~\cite{Juodzbalis2024}, which is
qualitatively consistent with a maturity channel where central compact
objects develop faster than the stellar host component.
This complements earlier evidence of unusually luminous and active
objects at $z\sim 10$--$11$, such as GN-z11~\cite{Maiolino2024,Bunker2023}.
These observations do not prove ECT, but they motivate precisely the
accelerated maturity channels identified here.

\paragraph{Qualitatively consistent with the present ECT mechanism.}
The current derived-parent maturity budget particularly favours
BH-assisted and compact-core driven maturity channels rather than a
purely linear-growth solution.
Objects that appear unusually mature or quiescent at high redshift are
thus the most natural testing ground for the present mechanism.

\paragraph{Predictions still to be tested.}
The framework also predicts possible systematic shifts in velocity
dispersions or virial speeds at fixed host mass, compact rapidly
assembled massive hosts, and a potential dependence on environment
through the background condensate history.
These remain predictions rather than established results at the
present stage.

\paragraph{Status.}
The present derived-parent results constitute the first non-benchmark
numerical layer of the theory: they go beyond the earlier heuristic
proxy analysis, but fall short of a full morphology-level calculation.
Full linear growth has been solved on the derived-parent background;
semi-analytic maturity factors have been combined with the background
age budget; but a complete N-body or hydrodynamical structure-formation
program remains an open next step.
These signatures should therefore be interpreted as a mixture of
already-discussed observational tendencies and explicit ECT predictions,
not as a closed confirmation set.

\textit{ISW excess.}
In voids, larger $G_{\rm eff}$ in low-density regions amplifies the
ISW effect~(Level~B).  The CMB quadrupole deficit has a structural
ECT argument from horizon-scale suppression (coherent-branch boundary
conditions), but a quantitative derivation of $\delta C_2/C_2$ remains
open~(Level~Open).

\subsubsection*{Black holes and compact objects}

Black-hole implications of the ordered condensate branch are
important but exploratory~(Level~B); they are not yet part of the
present controlled macroscopic derivation and should be treated as
a future subsection.
For GW-related LIV effects the estimate of Section~\ref{sec:mp_gw}
applies: the expected propagation-time shift
$\Delta t_{\rm GW}\lesssim 10^{-64}$\,s is negligible for all
currently observed sources.



\subsection{Dark Sector Phenomenology in ECT}
\label{sec:dark_sector}

\textit{Status: Level~B throughout. The primary macroscopic-branch
result is that galactic mass discrepancy can be addressed by the
$\phi$-first closure without particle dark matter. Collective and
topological dark-sector candidates are structurally motivated by
P4, BR1, and the ordered vacuum, but require the Quantum Sector for full development.}

% -------------------------------------------------------
\subsubsection*{Dark-sector interpretation at the macroscopic level}
% -------------------------------------------------------

At the macroscopic level, ECT separates two conceptually distinct
questions often conflated under the name ``dark matter''.

\textit{First,} the galactic mass-discrepancy phenomenology can be
addressed within the present $\phi$-branch closure without introducing
a standard particle-dark-matter halo~(Level~B;
Section~\ref{sec:mp_galactic}).

\textit{Second,} ECT does not exclude additional dark-sector degrees
of freedom. Collective condensate excitations, Goldstone-like modes,
and topological sectors may contribute to the cosmic dark sector.
These possibilities probe the coherent-topological branch and require
the Quantum Sector for full treatment.

Thus the macroscopic branch addresses the \emph{phenomenology} of
missing gravitational response; the deeper ontology of possible
dark-sector excitations is developed in the Quantum Sector
(Section~\ref{sec:qs_dark_sector}).

% -------------------------------------------------------
\subsubsection*{Collective excitations as possible dark-sector candidates}
% -------------------------------------------------------

\textit{Status: Level~B structural possibility. Relic abundance,
stability, and production history not yet computed from bare~P3.}

The ordered branch supports a constrained soft orientation sector
(one longitudinal scalar mode $\chi$ in the scalar-only basis at
linear order; Level~B).
Its role as a dark-sector candidate is not established in the
present formulation; the main astrophysical successes of ECT
do not require any particle dark matter~(see
Section~\ref{sec:goldstone_integrability_main}).

% -------------------------------------------------------
\subsubsection*{Topological sectors and the dark sector}
\label{sec:ds_topological}
% -------------------------------------------------------

\textit{Status: Level~B/C structural possibility; detailed
treatment deferred to the Quantum Sector.}

ECT also admits the possibility that part of the dark sector is tied
to smooth coherent topological configurations~(BR1): winding sectors,
vortex-like structures, or topological configurations
(see Section~\ref{sec:predicted_states} for the full classification).
Their definition and dynamics depend on winding numbers and defect
stability beyond the coherent approximation. They are noted here
as a conceptual bridge to the Quantum Sector~(Section~\ref{sec:qs_dark_sector});
their detailed treatment is deferred.

\subsection{Mass-discrepancy phenomenology in ECT}

Within the revised ECT framework the dark-matter phenomenology
is organised into two sharply distinct levels:

\begin{enumerate}
\item \textbf{Galactic mass discrepancy} (flat rotation curves,
  RAR, BTFR): attributed entirely to the nonlinear
  $\phi$-branch of the ordered-branch amplitude variable
  (Section~\ref{sec:mp_galactic}).
  No particle dark matter and no kpc-scale variation of the
  heavy condensate amplitude are invoked.

\item \textbf{Possible non-galactic dark-sector contributions}:
  ultra-light Goldstone modes and topological condensate
  configurations may exist as additional excitations,
  but they are \emph{not required} to explain galactic
  rotation curves.  Whether they contribute to cluster or
  cosmological dark-sector phenomenology remains an open
  problem.
\end{enumerate}



\subsection{Dark energy and the dark sector in ECT}
\label{sec:dark_energy_phi}

\subsubsection{Dark energy as condensate vacuum energy}

In ECT the late-time accelerated expansion is not driven by
a cosmological constant added to the action.  Instead, it
arises from the residual vacuum energy of the condensate
after $O(4)\to O(3)$ symmetry breaking:
\begin{equation}
\rho_{\rm DE} = V(\Phi_0) + \tfrac{1}{2}(\partial_w\Phi)^2
= \rho_{\rm pot} + \rho_{\rm kin}\,.
\end{equation}
The equation of state is
\begin{equation}
w = \frac{p_{\rm DE}}{\rho_{\rm DE}}
= -1 + \frac{2\rho_{\rm kin}}{3\rho_{\rm cond}}\,.
\label{eq:w_ect}
\end{equation}
Since $\rho_{\rm kin} \geq 0$, ECT \emph{predicts}
$w(z) \geq -1$ at all redshifts (no phantom crossing).
This is a falsifiable structural prediction.

\paragraph{Thawing condensate model.}
If the condensate rolls slowly toward its potential
minimum, the kinetic fraction decays:
$\rho_{\rm kin}(z)/\rho_{\rm cond} = f_0\,e^{-\kappa z}$,
giving
\begin{equation}
w(z) = -1 + \frac{2f_0}{3}\,e^{-\kappa z}\,.
\label{eq:w_thaw}
\end{equation}
This is a \emph{thawing quintessence} model: $w \to -1$
in the past (condensate near potential minimum),
$w > -1$ today (condensate rolling).

\subsubsection{Observational status: DESI BAO measurements}

The Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI) has
published two data releases providing the most precise
BAO distance measurements to date:

\begin{itemize}
\item \textbf{DESI DR1} (April~2024~\cite{DESI2024}):
  6~million galaxy and quasar redshifts.
  Combined with Planck~CMB and type~Ia supernovae
  (Pantheon+, Union3, or DESY5), a $w_0w_a$CDM fit
  gives $w_0 \approx -0.83$, $w_a \approx -0.75$,
  disfavouring $\Lambda$CDM at $2.5$--$3.9\sigma$
  depending on the supernova dataset.

\item \textbf{DESI DR2} (March~2025~\cite{DESIDR2_2025}):
  14~million redshifts (three years of operation),
  doubling DR1.  The preference for evolving dark energy
  \emph{strengthened}: $w_0w_a$CDM is preferred over
  $\Lambda$CDM at $2.8$--$4.2\sigma$.  The best-fit
  CPL parameters indicate $w_0 > -1$ today and
  $w_0 + w_a < -1$, formally implying a phantom crossing
  near $z \approx 0.5$.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Is the phantom crossing real?}
The apparent phantom crossing ($w < -1$ at $z > 0.5$) is
a feature of the \emph{CPL parametrisation}
$w(z) = w_0 + w_a z/(1+z)$, which is a simple two-parameter
approximation --- not a physical model.  Several independent
analyses have shown~\cite{Lodha2025,Calderon2024}:

\begin{enumerate}
\item The CPL form is too rigid to correctly describe both
  low-$z$ and high-$z$ behaviour simultaneously.

\item \emph{Thawing quintessence} models (where
  $w \geq -1$ at all times) are compatible with DESI data
  at the $2\sigma$ level~\cite{Shlivko2024,Gomez2025}.

\item Non-parametric reconstructions (Gaussian processes,
  crossing statistics) also find evolving dark energy but
  with larger uncertainty on whether $w$ actually crosses
  $-1$~\cite{Calderon2024}.

\item The phantom crossing significance does not yet reach
  $5\sigma$ and depends on the choice of supernova
  dataset~\cite{Wang2025}.
\end{enumerate}

\noindent
The current observational situation can therefore be
summarised as follows:
\begin{center}
\renewcommand{\arraystretch}{1.2}
\begin{tabular}{lc}
\hline\hline
Statement & Status \\
\hline
$w \neq -1$ (dark energy evolves) & $2.8$--$4.2\sigma$ \\
$w$ crosses $-1$ (phantom) &
  preferred in CPL, but \\
 & $2\sigma$-compatible with $w \geq -1$ \\
$\Lambda$CDM ($w = -1$ exact) & increasingly challenged, not yet excluded \\
\hline\hline
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

\paragraph{ECT confrontation with DESI.}
The ECT thawing model~\eqref{eq:w_thaw} with
$f_0 \approx 0.26$ and $\kappa \approx 4.3$ (calibrated,
not derived from first principles) is consistent with
DESI data at the $2\sigma$ level
(Fig.~\ref{fig:w_z}).  It reproduces the key observational
feature --- $w > -1$ today, evolving toward $w = -1$
in the past --- while predicting that the phantom crossing
is \emph{apparent}, not real.

If future data (DESI Year~5, Euclid, Vera Rubin/LSST)
confirm the phantom crossing at $>3\sigma$, ECT would be
falsified or require fundamental modification.  If
$w \geq -1$ is confirmed, $\Lambda$CDM would be further
challenged and ECT remains viable.


\subsubsection{Mass discrepancy: $\phi$-modification vs.\ particle dark matter}

The galactic mass-discrepancy phenomenology (flat rotation
curves, RAR, BTFR) is explained in ECT by the nonlinear
response of the ordered-branch amplitude variable $\phi$
(Section~\ref{sec:mp_galactic}).  This mechanism does
\emph{not} require new particles.

\paragraph{Predictions for direct detection experiments.}
Since the galactic mass discrepancy arises from the
$\phi$-branch rather than from weakly interacting massive
particles, ECT predicts that direct dark-matter-particle detection
experiments (XENON, LZ, PandaX, DARWIN) will find
\emph{no WIMP signal} for the galactic mass discrepancy.
A robust detection of a particle species accounting for
the galactic mass discrepancy would falsify the
$\phi$-branch interpretation.  By contrast, the mere
existence of additional dark condensate excitations on
non-galactic scales would not.



\paragraph{Cosmological fractions.}
ECT does not yet derive the cosmological fractions
$\Omega_b$, $\Omega_{\rm DM}$, and $\Omega_\Lambda$ from
first principles.  What it presently offers is a
reinterpretation: the galactic mass discrepancy is attributed
to the nonlinear $\phi$-branch rather than to particle dark
matter, while the late-time dark-energy sector is interpreted
as residual condensate vacuum energy.  A first-principles
derivation of the effective cosmological fractions remains
an open problem.

\paragraph{Distinction from the fifth force.}
The galactic $\phi$-modification and the ECT fifth force
($\mathcal{L}_5 = \beta\bar\Psi\gamma^A n_A\Psi$,
$\beta \sim m_f/M_{\rm Pl}$) are \emph{different}
manifestations of the same condensate:
the $\phi$-branch operates on kpc scales;
the fifth force is Planck-suppressed and detectable at
laboratory scales through spin precession
($\omega_5 \sim 10^{-10}$--$10^{-15}$\,rad\,s$^{-1}$, model-dependent) and
E\"otv\"os-type experiments ($\eta \sim 10^{-15}$).
Neither is a consequence of the other.

\bigskip
\noindent
\textbf{Transition to Part~III.}
Part~II has established the macroscopic observable predictions of ECT:
galactic rotation curves from the ordered-branch amplitude variable $\phi$,
the baryonic Tully--Fisher relation as an exact algebraic consequence
of the critical infrared branch, cosmological inflation, dark energy,
and dark-matter candidates from the condensate spectrum.
All of these emerge from the variation of the condensate
amplitude $v_0(r)$ and the anisotropy parameter $\chi = \alpha-\beta$
on astrophysical and cosmological scales.

Part~III returns to the microscopic quantum structure.
Whereas Part~I derived key results of quantum mechanics from the condensate
(Schrödinger equation and CCR strictly; influence functional and second law
with Gaussian--Markov assumptions), Part~III examines
what the condensate \emph{additionally} predicts about the foundational
principles of physics: the uncertainty principle as a theorem,
the principle of extremal action from saddle-point condensate paths,
a structural route toward quantum statistics and the Pauli exclusion
principle via $SO(4)$ representation theory (sign structure compatible
with fermionic antisymmetry in the quadratic limit;
full PLZ theorem not yet derived),
and the structural organization of three quantum vacuum phenomena---Casimir
(at condensate boundaries), Unruh, and environmental decoherence---as
three observables controlled by related limits of the condensate
two-point function $N(\tau,\mathbf{r})$ (organizing proposition).

%==============================================================



% ============================================================

\paragraph{Bridge to the Quantum Sector.}
The macroscopic branch addresses gravitational phenomenology. Whenever
the discussion turns to coherent collective excitations, winding sectors,
or topological dark-sector configurations, the analysis enters the domain
of the coherent-topological branch~(BR1). Those aspects are developed in
Section~\ref{sec:qs_dark_sector} of the Quantum Sector.

\part{Quantum Sector: Coherent-Topological Branch}
\label{part:quantum}

\section{Quantum Sector}
\label{sec:quantum}

% The following backward-compatibility labels redirect to new QS sections.
% They allow external references from other parts of the article to resolve.

% ============================================================
\subsection{Quantum Sector as the development of the
coherent-topological branch}
\label{sec:qs_intro}
% backward-compatibility alias:
\label{sec:emergent_principles}
% ============================================================

\textit{Connection to ECT basics:
Sections~\ref{sec:branching}, \ref{sec:coh_branch},
\ref{sec:hbar_status}.
Status: conceptual bridge; all structural ingredients were
prepared in ECT basics and are now developed dynamically.}

\medskip
After ECT basics and Macroscopic Physics, the logical structure
of ECT has separated into two effective branches from the same
ordered condensate.
The first branch---developed in Macroscopic Physics
(Section~\ref{sec:macroscopic})---describes those observables
governed by the phase-insensitive, coarse-grained geometric
response: long-wavelength metric fluctuations, gravitational
stiffness, cosmological dynamics, and galactic phenomenology.
In that branch the global phase of the effective order parameter
is irrelevant to the observables of interest and can be averaged away.

The second branch---developed here---treats the same ordered
condensate through observables that \emph{remain sensitive} to
global phase structure, winding sectors, loop configurations,
and coherent correlators.
This is the coherent-topological branch of ECT, and its systematic
development is the task of the Quantum Sector.

\paragraph{Why a separate Quantum Sector is needed.}
The geometric branch is sufficient whenever local phase information
is washed out under coarse-graining.
It is not sufficient for phenomena in which the global phase of
the effective order parameter, the topology of loop sectors, and the
coherent structure of amplitudes remain physically active.
Those phenomena require a different effective description---one that
retains global phase data rather than discarding it.
The Quantum Sector provides this description.

\paragraph{Primary variables of the coherent-topological branch.}
In the coherent branch the ordered condensate is described by
variables that retain global phase information.
The primary objects are:
\begin{itemize}
  \item the effective order parameter
    \begin{equation}
      \Phi_{\rm eff} = \rho\,e^{i\theta}
      \label{eq:qs_order_parameter}
    \end{equation}
  \item the phase variable $\theta$, which is \emph{not}
    coarse-grained away;
  \item the winding quantisation condition,
    \begin{equation}
      \oint_\gamma \partial_A\theta\,dX^A = 2\pi n,
      \qquad n\in\mathbb{Z}
      \label{eq:qs_winding}
    \end{equation}
  \item the pre-quantum action scale $S_0$, introduced through
    the minimal nontrivial loop action
    \begin{equation}
      S_{\rm loop,min} = 2\pi S_0
      \label{eq:qs_Sloop}
    \end{equation}
  \item coherent correlators and loop amplitudes of the ordered sector.
\end{itemize}
These objects were already structurally prepared in ECT basics:
the coherent-topological branch was identified
(Section~\ref{sec:coh_branch}), the pre-quantum scale $S_0$ was
introduced (Section~\ref{sec:hbar_status}), and the existence
of winding sectors was established within~BR1.
None of them was fully developed dynamically; that development
belongs to the present section.

\paragraph{What ECT basics already established.}
The following structural ingredients are available before
entering the Quantum Sector:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item The ordered phase admits a genuine phase-sensitive sector,
    distinct from the geometric branch~(Level~A within the
    broken-phase EFT).
  \item Topologically quantised winding sectors exist in the
    coherent order parameter as a consequence of the smooth coherent-sector rule BR1~(Level~A).
  \item A pre-quantum action scale $S_0$ is structurally
    introduced by the loop quantisation condition~\eqref{eq:qs_Sloop}
    (Level~A for the structural existence of the scale;
    its explicit first-principles evaluation is deferred,
    see Appendix~\ref{app:Sloop_calc}).
  \item The variational/Noether foundation is common to both
    branches, so the Quantum Sector is not an independent add-on
    but a second effective development of the same condensate
    theory (Level~A: follows from the shared P1--P3 action).
\end{enumerate}

\paragraph{What has not yet been established at this point.}
ECT basics and Macroscopic Physics did \emph{not} yet prove:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\roman*)]
  \item That $S_0 = \hbar$.
    This is one of the first central tasks of the Quantum Sector:
    it requires an explicit calculation and identification,
    not merely the structural existence of $S_0$.
  \item That canonical commutation relations follow in their
    standard form.
  \item That the Born rule has been derived.
  \item That decoherence, probability interpretation, and
    measurement theory are complete.
  \item That topological defect sectors (vortices, domain walls,
    instantons) have been fully developed.
\end{enumerate}
All these belong to the present section or to its explicitly
listed open problems.

\paragraph{Why the phase is not coarse-grained away here.}
In Macroscopic Physics, local phase information was irrelevant
because the observables under study were governed by the coarse-grained
metric response and long-wavelength stiffness.
In the coherent-topological branch the relevant observables are
different: the global consistency of the phase, the existence of
nontrivial loop sectors, and the quantisation of winding numbers
carry direct physical content.
Therefore the phase variable $\theta$ is not discarded under
coarse-graining; it becomes one of the principal dynamical variables
of the effective description.

\paragraph{Physical meaning of the Quantum Sector.}
The Quantum Sector should \emph{not} be understood as importing
ordinary quantum mechanics into ECT from outside.
Its role is precisely the opposite: to show how phase-sensitive and
topological features of the ordered condensate can \emph{reproduce,
motivate, or constrain} the structures that ordinary quantum theory
usually postulates.
Both branches---geometric and coherent-topological---are consequences
of the same working postulates P1--P5 and the derived coherent-sector
rule~BR1; they differ only in which class of observables they target.

\paragraph{Logical structure of the Quantum Sector.}
The present section proceeds in the following order:
\begin{enumerate}[label=(\arabic*)]
  \item \textbf{Coherent phase dynamics and loop sectors}
    (Section~\ref{sec:qs_phase_dynamics}):
    the dynamics of $\theta$, loop configurations, and why
    phase dynamics is the correct effective language of the
    coherent branch.
  \item \textbf{Pre-quantum action scale and the status of $\hbar$}
    (Section~\ref{sec:qs_hbar}):
    development of Section~\ref{sec:hbar_status}
    and Appendix~\ref{app:Sloop_calc};
    conditions for $S_0 = \hbar$.
  \item \textbf{Wave dynamics, Wick rotation, and
    Schr\"odinger-type evolution}
    (Section~\ref{sec:qs_wave}):
    coherent amplitude language, Lorentzian continuation
    $w\to -ic_*t$, and the emergence of first-order
    coherent wave evolution.
  \item \textbf{Canonical structure and the status of CCR}
    (Section~\ref{sec:qs_ccr}):
    where and in what sense canonical commutation relations
    appear; what is already established and what is not.
  \item \textbf{Uncertainty principle in the coherent branch}
    (Section~\ref{sec:qs_uncertainty}):
    derived as a mathematical consequence of the canonical
    structure, not postulated.
  \item \textbf{Principle of extremal action and saddle dominance}
    (Section~\ref{sec:qs_extremal_action}):
    classicality as the large-action saddle limit of coherent
    amplitude weighting.
  \item \textbf{Conservation laws in the coherent sector}
    (Section~\ref{sec:qs_conservation}):
    Noether currents inherited from the variational structure;
    open: topological current algebra.
  \item \textbf{Quantum statistics: exchange sectors and
    spinorial compatibility}
    (Section~\ref{sec:qs_statistics}):
    structural route from exchange topology to the
    bosonic/fermionic dichotomy.
  \item \textbf{Representation-theoretic route to Dirac structure}
    (Section~\ref{sec:qs_dirac}):
    $O(4)\to O(3)$ SSB + Wick rotation give Lorentz
    algebra~(Level~A); conditional Dirac-type dynamics
    for spinorial excitations~(Level~B); Pauli exclusion
    as conditional consequence.
  \item \textbf{Quantum vacuum structure of the ordered condensate}
    (Section~\ref{sec:qs_vacuum_intro}):
    coherent vacuum as a structured ordered medium.
  \item \textbf{Boundary sensitivity, particle production,
    and accelerated-vacuum response}
    (Sections~\ref{sec:qs_casimir}--\ref{sec:qs_unruh}):
    Casimir-type effects, cosmological particle production,
    and Unruh-type response as consequences of the nontrivial
    coherent vacuum.
  \item \textbf{Unified vacuum-response logic and analogue-gravity
    continuity}
    (Section~\ref{sec:qs_vacuum_unified}):
    Casimir, particle production, Unruh, and Hawking as four facets
    of one coherent-vacuum response structure.
  \item \textbf{Summary of coherent-vacuum predictions}
    (Section~\ref{sec:qs_vacuum_summary}).
  \item \textbf{Influence functional, decoherence, and the
    arrow of time}
    (Section~\ref{sec:qs_decoherence}):
    reduced-state decoherence, low-entropy coherent initial sector,
    qualitative entropy monotonicity~\eqref{eq:qs_entropy_monotone},
    the $N_{\rm eff}\gg1$ versus $N_{\rm eff}\sim1$ distinction,
    and the emergence of classical alternatives from coherent dynamics.
  \item \textbf{Probability, Born-type interpretation, and
    measurement status}
    (Section~\ref{sec:qs_born}):
    what can be established; what remains Level~B or open.
  \item \textbf{Topological sectors, defect configurations, and
    the dark sector}
    (Section~\ref{sec:qs_defects}):
    full development of Section~\ref{sec:defect_sectors} and of the
    MP-to-QS dark-sector bridge: collective candidates, topological
    sectors, vortices, domain walls, instantons, and the distinction
    between smooth coherent dark-sector candidates and genuine defect
    ontology.
  \item \textbf{Black-hole thermodynamics, Hawking radiation, and
    the information problem}
    (Section~\ref{sec:qs_black_holes}):
    horizon thermodynamics as a coherent-vacuum phenomenon; apparent
    thermal mixedness from reduced-state tracing; critical ordered-branch amplitude
    shell closure of the strong-field information problem.
  \item \textbf{Status table and open problems}
    (Section~\ref{sec:qs_status}).
\end{enumerate}
A final consistency map of the Quantum Sector is shown in
Fig.~\ref{fig:qs_consistency}.
Figures~\ref{fig:qs_quantization_chain}, \ref{fig:qs_vacuum_unified},
\ref{fig:qs_decoherence_regimes}, and~\ref{fig:qs_bh_shell}
provide complementary schematic maps of the main QS logic.

\paragraph{Bridge from Macroscopic Physics.}
Macroscopic Physics answered the question:
\emph{how does the ordered condensate appear to a large-scale
observer insensitive to coherent phase data?}
The Quantum Sector asks the complementary question:
\emph{what effective dynamics remains when that phase data is
retained rather than averaged away?}
These are not two different theories but two branches of the
same ordered condensate, selected by two different classes of
observables.
The bridge between them is the branching structure established
in ECT basics (Section~\ref{sec:branching}).

\paragraph{Summary.}
The Quantum Sector is the systematic development of the
coherent-topological branch of ECT.
Its task is to turn the structural ingredients prepared in
ECT basics---phase variable $\theta$, winding sectors~(BR1),
loop quantisation, and the pre-quantum action scale $S_0$---into
a controlled framework for wave dynamics, decoherence,
probability, and topological dark-sector physics.
All results carry explicit Level~A/B/C markers.

The gain of the new postulate structure is not that it automatically
turns all quantum laws into unconditional Level~A theorems.
Its real gain is subtler: it sharpens the structural logic of the
Quantum Sector, removes earlier overclaims, excludes some
interpretations that were previously left open, and upgrades several
heuristic claims into stronger conditional statements.
The framework distinguishes clearly between structural emergence,
effective closure, and still-open microscopic completion.

% ============================================================
\subsection{Coherent phase dynamics and loop sectors}
\label{sec:qs_phase_dynamics}
% ============================================================

\textit{Status: Level~A for the existence of the phase variable,
winding sectors, and the topological loop classification inside the
coherent branch; Level~B for the specific effective dynamical closure
used to connect these structures to wave dynamics.
Connection to ECT basics: BR1, Section~\ref{sec:hbar_status},
Appendix~\ref{app:Sloop_calc}.}

\medskip
The first task of the Quantum Sector is to identify the correct
effective variables of the coherent-topological branch and to show
why their natural language is the language of phase dynamics.
This is not yet ordinary quantum mechanics.
Rather, it is the phase-sensitive effective description of the ordered
condensate in the regime where coherent loop sectors remain
physically relevant.

\paragraph{Phase as the natural variable of the coherent branch.}
