\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/fig_bh_information.pdf}
\caption{%
ECT black-hole information scenario.
\textbf{(a)} Tolman temperature profile $T_{\rm loc}(\rho)$ near a
non-extremal horizon, plotted in units of $T_c$ vs.\ $\rho/\rho_c$.
The condensate critical temperature $T_c = v_0\sqrt{6}$ (dashed horizontal
line) is reached at the universal proper distance
$\rho_c \approx 0.33\,\ell_{\rm Pl}$ (dotted vertical line), independently
of the black-hole mass.
The lightly shaded region is the well-ordered Lorentzian phase ($T_{\rm loc}<T_c$);
the darker shaded region marks the near-horizon critical shell ($T_{\rm loc}>T_c$).
\textbf{(b)} Schematic Page curve.
The coarse-grained radiation entropy (dashed) rises monotonically as
in the semiclassical Hawking approximation.
The fine-grained entropy (solid) follows the coarse-grained branch
until the Page time, then decreases as the shell entropy capacity becomes
finite and state-dependent corrections to emission become order-one.
The shaded area between the two curves represents entropy that is
``returned'' via shell-mediated correlations.
The figure is schematic; a quantitative curve requires the explicit shell
Hamiltonian.
\textbf{(c)} Black-hole parameters vs.\ mass.
The critical shell proper distance $\rho_c$ is mass-independent (not shown
separately); the ratio $\rho_c/r_s \propto M^{-1}$ (left axis, red) shows
how deeply the shell is buried inside the Schwarzschild radius.
The Hawking temperature $T_H \propto M^{-1}$ (right axis, dashed blue)
decreases with mass.
For all astrophysical black holes $T_H \ll T_c$; the critical shell is
firmly in the sub-Planckian regime.
}
\label{fig:bh_information}
\end{figure}

% ============================================================
% APPENDIX: Derivation of galactic phi-closure (new, v3f)
% ============================================================

\section{ECT derivation status for the galactic interpolation law}
\label{app:rar_derivation_status}

This appendix is a \emph{status appendix}: it summarises what is
already structurally derived in the galactic sector and what remains
open.  It is not the place where the practical fitting closure is
constructed; that construction is given separately in
Appendix~\ref{app:galactic_phi_closure}.

\textit{Status: Level~B.  The AQUAL field-equation structure and its
two asymptotics are the strongest currently derived results.  The exact
interpolation function and the exact environment law for $g_\dagger$
remain open~(OP3).}

In the current version of ECT, the strongest first-principles result
in the galactic sector is the derivation of the \emph{field-equation
structure and its asymptotic limits}, not yet a unique global
interpolation function.

\paragraph{Effective galactic functional.}
In the quasi-static weak-field limit the ECT condensate produces the
effective functional \eqref{eq:gal_effective_action}.
Varying with respect to $\Phi$ gives:
\begin{equation}
  \nabla\!\cdot\!\left[
    \mu\!\left(\frac{|\nabla\Phi|}{g_\dagger}\right)\nabla\Phi
  \right]=4\pi G_N\rho_b.
\end{equation}

\paragraph{Scale-invariance argument.}
The deep-branch scale invariance of the ordered ECT phase forces the
infrared nonlinear term to scale as $\mathcal{F}(Y)\propto Y^{3/2}$~(Level~B).
This implies $\mu(y)\propto y$ for $y\ll1$, while recovery of the
Newtonian branch gives $\mu(y)\to1$ for $y\gg1$.

\paragraph{RAR asymptotics as ECT consequences.}
\begin{equation}
  g\simeq g_N \quad(g\gg g_\dagger),\qquad
  g\simeq\sqrt{g_N g_\dagger}\quad(g\ll g_\dagger).
\end{equation}

\paragraph{BTFR as corollary.}
\begin{equation}
  v_\infty^4 = G_N M_b\,g_\dagger.
\end{equation}

\paragraph{What remains open.}
Two elements are not yet uniquely derived:
\begin{itemize}
\item \emph{Exact global interpolation function.}
  The functional form $\mu(y)=y/\sqrt{1+y^2}$ used in the SPARC
  analysis has the correct ECT-derived asymptotics but is not the
  unique choice; other functions with the same limits are also
  ECT-compatible.  The exact member of this class must follow from
  the full OP3 completion of $U(\phi)$.
\item \emph{Exact environment law for $g_\dagger$.}
  ECT predicts
  $g_\dagger=g_{\dagger,0}\,\Xi(\rho_{\rm env},\bar\phi_{\rm env},\ldots)$
  with $g_{\dagger,0}\sim cH_0$.  The specific form
  \eqref{eq:gdag_env} used in the present text is only a minimal
  Level~B ansatz for $\Xi$; the exact function remains open
  at the level of bare~P3~(OP3).
  The galaxy-to-galaxy scatter in $g_\dagger^{\rm eff}$ is a physical
  ECT prediction, not a shortcoming.
\end{itemize}

\section{Derivation of the galactic $\phi$-closure and the practical fitting law}
\label{app:galactic_phi_closure}

Unlike Appendix~\ref{app:rar_derivation_status}, which discusses the
derivation status at a conceptual level, the present appendix records
the concrete Level~B closure actually used in the SPARC fitting
pipeline.

This appendix derives the practical galactic fitting law used in the
SPARC analysis.

\subsection{Variational structure}

The galactic sector is treated as a nonlinear static response problem
for the effective potential $\Phi$.  The minimal effective functional is
taken to be
\begin{equation}
\Gamma_{\rm gal}[\Phi]
=
\int d^3x\,
\left[
\frac{g_0^{\dagger\,2}}{8\pi G_N}
F\!\left(\frac{|\nabla\Phi|}{g^\dagger_0}\right)
-\rho_b(\mathbf{x})\,\Phi
\right],
\label{eq:app_gal_functional}
\end{equation}
where $g^\dagger_0$ is the cosmological baseline critical acceleration
scale and $\rho_b$ is the baryonic source density.

The corresponding Euler--Lagrange equation reads
\begin{equation}
\nabla\!\cdot\!\left[
\mu_\phi\!\left(\frac{|\nabla\Phi|}{g^\dagger_0}\right)\nabla\Phi
\right]
=
4\pi G_N \rho_b,
\label{eq:app_gal_mu_eq}
\end{equation}
with
\begin{equation}
\mu_\phi(x)=\frac{1}{2x}\frac{dF}{dx}.
\label{eq:app_mu_from_F}
\end{equation}

\subsection{Required asymptotic branches}

The closure must reproduce two limiting regimes.

\paragraph{Screened Newtonian branch.}
At large accelerations one must recover ordinary gravity,
\begin{equation}
\mu_\phi(x)\to 1
\qquad (x\gg 1).
\end{equation}

\paragraph{Deep critical branch.}
At small accelerations the critical infrared branch must satisfy
\begin{equation}
\mu_\phi(x)\to x
\qquad (x\ll 1),
\end{equation}
which yields the characteristic ECT deep-regime law
\begin{equation}
g\to \sqrt{g_N g^\dagger}.
\end{equation}

\subsection{Minimal interpolation choice}

A full first-principles derivation of the interpolation family from the
microscopic condensate is not yet available.  The present work therefore adopts the phenomenologically simplest
smooth representative of the ECT-compatible interpolation class,
consistent with both asymptotic branches:
\begin{equation}
\mu_\phi(x)=\frac{x}{1+x}.
\label{eq:app_mu_choice}
\end{equation}
This is a Level~B closure assumption, not a unique first-principles
derivation.  Other smooth functions satisfying $\mu\to x$ ($x\ll1$)
and $\mu\to 1$ ($x\gg1$) are equally ECT-compatible at the present
stage; the exact member of this class must follow from the
full OP3 completion of $U(\phi)$.

Using eq.~\eqref{eq:app_mu_from_F}, one obtains
\begin{equation}
F(x)=x^2-2x+2\ln(1+x),
\label{eq:app_F_choice}
\end{equation}
up to an irrelevant additive constant.  One verifies immediately that
\[
F(x)\sim x^2 \quad (x\gg1),
\qquad
F(x)\sim \frac{2}{3}x^3 \quad (x\ll1),
\]
so the closure indeed interpolates between the quadratic screened branch
and the critical $Y^{3/2}$ branch.

\subsection{Practical algebraic fitting law}

In the quasi-radial fitting approximation, the modified Poisson
equation reduces to
\begin{equation}
\mu_\phi\!\left(\frac{g}{g^\dagger_{\rm eff}}\right) g = g_N.
\label{eq:app_mu_gn}
\end{equation}
With the choice \eqref{eq:app_mu_choice} this gives
\begin{equation}
\frac{g/g^\dagger_{\rm eff}}{1+g/g^\dagger_{\rm eff}}\,g=g_N,
\end{equation}
hence
\begin{equation}
g^2-g_N g-g_N g^\dagger_{\rm eff}=0.
\end{equation}
Selecting the positive branch yields the practical ECT fitting law
\begin{equation}
g(R)=\frac{1}{2}\left[g_N(R)+\sqrt{g_N^2(R)+4\,g_N(R)\,g^\dagger_{\rm eff}}\right].
\label{eq:app_final_g_law}
\end{equation}

\subsection{Baryonic input from SPARC}

The Newtonian input acceleration is computed from the SPARC baryonic
mass model as
\begin{equation}
g_N(R)=
\frac{
V_{\rm gas}^2(R)+\Upsilon_{\rm d}V_{\rm disk}^2(R)+
\Upsilon_{\rm b}V_{\rm bul}^2(R)
}{R}.
\label{eq:app_gn_sparc}
\end{equation}
The predicted circular velocity is then
\begin{equation}
V_{\rm ECT}(R)=\sqrt{R\,g(R)}.
\label{eq:app_vect}
\end{equation}

\subsection{RAR representation of the same closure}

The radial acceleration relation is not an independent phenomenological
law added on top of the velocity fit.  It is simply the same galactic
closure expressed in acceleration variables:
\begin{equation}
g_{\rm obs}(R)=\frac{V_{\rm obs}^2(R)}{R},
\qquad
g_{\rm bar}(R)=\frac{V_{\rm gas}^2(R)+\Upsilon_{\rm d}V_{\rm disk}^2(R)+
\Upsilon_{\rm b}V_{\rm bul}^2(R)}{R}.
\end{equation}
The corresponding ECT prediction is therefore
\begin{equation}
g_{\rm ECT}(R)=\frac{1}{2}\left[g_{\rm bar}(R)+
\sqrt{g_{\rm bar}^2(R)+4\,g_{\rm bar}(R)\,g^\dagger_{\rm eff}}\right].
\label{eq:app_rar_ect}
\end{equation}
Thus the RAR plots are only a different projection of the same fitted
galactic closure already used in the rotation-curve analysis.

\subsection{Deep branch and BTFR}

In the low-acceleration branch, eq.~\eqref{eq:app_final_g_law}
reduces to
\begin{equation}
g \simeq \sqrt{g_N g^\dagger_{\rm eff}}.
\end{equation}
For circular motion, $v_c^2/R=g$, and at large radii where
$M_b(R)\to M_{\rm bar}$,
\begin{equation}
v_c^4 = G_N M_{\rm bar} g^\dagger_{\rm eff}.
\label{eq:app_btfr_again}
\end{equation}
Thus the BTFR slope is structural, while its normalisation is governed
by the effective critical scale.

In the present numerical implementation we use an internal outer
baryonic-mass proxy
\begin{equation}
M_{\rm bar,eff}
=
\frac{R_{\rm tail}^2\,g_N(R_{\rm tail})}{G_N},
\label{eq:app_btfr_proxy_mass}
\end{equation}
where $R_{\rm tail}$ is an outer-tail radius estimator constructed from
the last measured SPARC points.  This provides a self-consistent BTFR
test inside the same galactic closure pipeline.  It should be read as an
internal ECT BTFR diagnostic, not yet as a substitute for the standard
catalogue-level BTFR constructed from an independent total baryonic-mass
table.


% ============================================================
% APPENDIX: Computational procedure for SPARC fits (new, v3f)
% ============================================================

\section{Computational procedure for the SPARC galactic fits}
\label{app:sparc_computation}

This appendix summarises the numerical procedure used to generate the
galactic figures and tables.

\subsection{Input data}

The analysis uses the SPARC mass-model catalogue
\cite{Lelli2016SPARC}, containing radius $R$, observed velocity
$V_{\rm obs}$ with uncertainty $\sigma_V$, and the baryonic Newtonian
components $V_{\rm gas}$, $V_{\rm disk}$, and $V_{\rm bul}$.

\subsection{Fit modes}

Two fitting modes are considered.

\paragraph{Fixed-$M/L$ mode.}
The stellar mass-to-light ratios are fixed at
\[
\Upsilon_{\rm d}=0.5,\qquad \Upsilon_{\rm b}=0.7,
\]
and only the effective critical scale $g^\dagger_{\rm eff}$ is fitted.
This is the cleaner ECT test because it removes the strongest disk
$M/L$ degeneracy.

\paragraph{Free-$M/L$ mode.}
The disk mass-to-light ratio $\Upsilon_{\rm d}$ is fitted jointly with
$g^\dagger_{\rm eff}$, while $\Upsilon_{\rm b}=0.7$ is kept fixed.

\subsection{Optimisation and quality control}

For each galaxy the fit minimises
\begin{equation}
\chi^2 = \sum_i
\frac{\left[V_{\rm obs}(R_i)-V_{\rm ECT}(R_i)\right]^2}{\sigma_i^2}.
\end{equation}
A minimum error floor of $2\,{\rm km\,s^{-1}}$ is imposed in the present
runs for robustness.

The output catalogue stores quality flags indicating:
\begin{itemize}
\item too few measured points,
\item poor reduced $\chi^2$,
\item fitted stellar $M/L$ at the scan boundary,
\item possible bulge sensitivity.
\end{itemize}
A clean sub-sample is defined by excluding low-quality flagged cases.

\subsection{Model comparison}

Four fit families are compared:
\begin{enumerate}
\item ECT fixed-$M/L$ closure ($k=1$ free parameter),
\item ECT free-$M/L$ closure ($k=2$ free parameters),
\item MOND with free stellar $M/L$ ($k=1$),
\item $\Lambda$CDM with an NFW-type halo fit ($k=3$).
\end{enumerate}
Because the number of free parameters differs between the models, both
AIC and BIC are computed in addition to reduced $\chi^2$.

\subsection{Outputs used in the paper}

The main text uses:
\begin{itemize}
\item a representative SPARC rotation-curve figure (Fig.~\ref{fig:sparc_sample_new}),
\item a compact sample-level fit-quality comparison figure (Fig.~\ref{fig:chi2_comparison_main}),
\item the updated effective critical-scale summary figure (Fig.~\ref{fig:gdagger_analysis_new_main}),
\item a Milky Way comparison figure (Fig.~\ref{fig:mw_new}),
\item a compact summary table over the SPARC sample (Table~\ref{tab:sparc_summary_main}),
\item an internal BTFR diagnostic figure (Fig.~\ref{fig:btfr_new_main}),
\item a six-panel RAR figure derived from the same closure (Fig.~\ref{fig:rar_new_main}).
\end{itemize}

Additional scatter diagnostics, fixed/free-$M/L$ comparisons, and
further machine-readable tables are retained as supplementary material.

\subsection{Derived plot quantities: BTFR, RAR, and effective critical-scale diagnostics}

Three additional observational representations are generated from the
same fitted galactic closure.

\paragraph{BTFR.}
The BTFR plot uses the outer-tail velocity estimator $V_{\rm flat}$
constructed from the outer SPARC points together with the internal
effective baryonic-mass proxy
\[
M_{\rm bar,eff}=R_{\rm tail}^2 g_N(R_{\rm tail})/G_N.
\]
This yields a self-consistent ECT BTFR diagnostic based on the same
pipeline as the rotation-curve fits.

\paragraph{RAR.}
The RAR figure is produced by plotting
$g_{\rm obs}=V_{\rm obs}^2/R$ against
$g_{\rm bar}=(V_{\rm gas}^2+\Upsilon_{\rm d}V_{\rm disk}^2+\Upsilon_{\rm b}V_{\rm bul}^2)/R$
for the same galaxies.  The ECT and MOND curves shown there are not
separate empirical fit functions, but direct projections of the same
closure used in the velocity analysis.

\paragraph{Effective critical-scale analysis.}
The updated $g^\dagger$ figure is built directly from the fitted
per-galaxy values of $g^\dagger_{\rm eff}$ produced by the new SPARC
pipeline.  It replaces the earlier legacy $\phi_{\rm env}$ plot and is
therefore the correct summary diagnostic for the present version of the
galactic sector.

\subsection{Diagnostic interpretation and the current EFE proxy}

The diagnostics retained in this appendix serve three distinct roles:
they separate fixed-$M/L$ from free-$M/L$ behaviour, they allow
information-criterion comparison (AIC/BIC) between ECT, MOND, and
$\Lambda$CDM, and they identify the clean sub-sample used in the main
text to reduce the strongest fitting pathologies.  Only the most
informative summary figures are promoted to the main narrative; the
remaining diagnostics are kept here in order to document the stability
and limitations of the present Level~B galactic closure.

A preliminary nearest-neighbour proxy for the external field was tested.
For the currently available environment inputs the resulting ratio
$g_{\rm ext}/g^\dagger_0$ is typically extremely small, so the proxy
correction is numerically negligible.  This does not show that EFE is
absent in ECT; it only shows that the present Level-1 proxy is too weak
to affect the displayed sample and is therefore not promoted to a main
result.


\subsection{Additional diagnostic outputs}

\begin{figure*}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{figures/diag_gdag_scatter4.pdf}
\caption{Additional scatter diagnostics for the fitted effective
critical scale $g^\dagger_{\rm eff}$ across the SPARC sample: dependence
on a baryonic mass proxy, a baryonic surface-density proxy, the fitted
disk mass-to-light ratio, and the fit quality itself.  These plots are
retained as supplementary diagnostics rather than promoted to the main
text, because the more compact summary is now given by
Fig.~\ref{fig:gdagger_analysis_new_main}.}
\label{fig:app_gdag_scatter}
\end{figure*}

\begin{figure}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.78\linewidth]{figures/diag_gdag_histogram.pdf}
\caption{Histogram of fitted effective critical scales
$g^\dagger_{\rm eff}$ across the SPARC sample.  The vertical markers
indicate the two natural ECT baselines $cH_0$ and $cH_0/(2\pi)$.  In
the present manuscript the compact two-panel summary of this information
is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:gdagger_analysis_new_main}; the histogram is
retained here as a supplementary diagnostic.}
\label{fig:app_gdag_hist}
\end{figure}

\begin{figure*}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.82\textwidth]{figures/diag_fixed_vs_free_chi2.pdf}
\caption{Fixed-$M/L$ versus free-$M/L$ comparison of fit quality across
the SPARC sample.  Each point corresponds to one galaxy, with the
horizontal axis showing the reduced $\chi^2$ of the fixed-$M/L$ ECT fit
and the vertical axis showing the reduced $\chi^2$ of the free-$M/L$
ECT fit.  The diagonal $y=x$ marks equal fit quality.  The dominant
population lies below this line, showing that allowing $\Upsilon_{\rm d}$
to vary improves the fit for most galaxies.  This figure is retained in
the appendix because it diagnoses the strength of the $M/L$ degeneracy
rather than introducing a new physical prediction.}
\label{fig:app_fixed_vs_free_chi2}
\end{figure*}

\begin{figure*}[H]
\centering
\includegraphics[width=0.82\textwidth]{figures/diag_fixed_vs_free_gdag.pdf}
\caption{Fixed-$M/L$ versus free-$M/L$ comparison of the fitted
effective critical scale $g^\dagger_{\rm eff}$.  The diagonal $y=x$
marks identical recovered values.  The moderate correlation indicates
that part of the scatter in $g^\dagger_{\rm eff}$ is robust under
changes in the disk mass-to-light treatment, while another part is
clearly entangled with the $M/L$ degeneracy.  This is why the fixed-$M/L$
mode is used in the main text as the cleaner test of the ECT galactic
closure, whereas the free-$M/L$ mode is interpreted as a robustness
check rather than as the primary physical result.}
\label{fig:app_fixed_vs_free_gdag}
\end{figure*}

The moderate fixed/free correlation (Pearson $r\approx0.54$ in the
present run) confirms that the effective critical scale is neither fully
determined by the stellar $M/L$ choice nor fully independent of it.

The full numerical pipeline produces several diagnostics that are useful
for interpreting the galactic fits but are too methodological to occupy
main-text space.  The figures collected here serve three distinct
purposes: they document the residual scatter of the fitted effective
critical scale, they show how that scatter changes between fixed-$M/L$
and free-$M/L$ fitting modes, and they quantify the extent to which the
apparent improvement of the free-$M/L$ mode should be attributed to
extra nuisance freedom rather than to a deeper physical closure.
Accordingly, these plots are retained in the appendix as robustness and
degeneracy diagnostics rather than as headline physical results.



% === Data and Code Availability and Acknowledgments ===

% ============================================================
% APPENDIX: Late-time phi-first cosmological background
% ============================================================
\section{Late-time $\phi$-first cosmological background apparatus}
\label{app:late_cosmo_background}

This appendix records the benchmark late-time specialisation of the
macroscopic background reduction derived in
Section~\ref{sec:background_reduction_macro}, with closure functions
understood in the sense of the ordered-branch EFT-to-macroscopic mapping
described in Section~\ref{sec:closure_function_mapping}.
It derives the minimal ECT background apparatus used for the
late-time cosmology calculations in Section~\ref{sec:hubble_jwst}.

\subsection{Derived condensate amplitude dynamics}
\label{sec:late_condensate_dynamics}

Before specialising to the benchmark late-time closure, it is useful
to record what the ordered-branch EFT already fixes at the structural
level.
The ordered-branch amplitude field $u(x)$ satisfies the Euclidean EFT
\begin{equation}
S_{\rm ord}^{(E)}[u,n]
=
\int d^4X\,
\left[
\frac{Z_u}{2}(\partial u)^2
+\frac{Z_n}{2}u^2(\partial n)^2
+W(u)
+\cdots
\right],
\label{eq:ordered_branch_eft_full}
\end{equation}
where $W(u)$ is the branch potential and $u_\infty$ is the late-time
screened amplitude satisfying $W_u(u_\infty)=0$.

The macroscopic amplitude variable is defined by the amplitude ratio:
\begin{equation}
\boxed{
\phi \equiv \frac{1}{\beta}\ln\!\frac{u}{u_\infty}}
\qquad\Longleftrightarrow\qquad
\boxed{u = u_\infty\, e^{\beta\phi}.}
\label{eq:phi_amplitude_relation}
\end{equation}
This gives the response factor its structural origin:
$F(\phi)=u/u_\infty=e^{\beta\phi}$.

\paragraph{Derived kinetic function.}
Substituting $u=u_\infty e^{\beta\phi}$ into the kinetic term:
\begin{equation}
\frac{Z_u}{2}(\partial u)^2
=
\frac{Z_u \beta^2 u_\infty^2}{2}\,e^{2\beta\phi}(\partial\phi)^2
\equiv
\frac{\omega_0}{2}\,e^{2\beta\phi}(\partial\phi)^2.
\label{eq:derived_kinetic}
\end{equation}
The derived kinetic closure is therefore
\begin{equation}
\boxed{\omega(\phi) = \omega_0\,e^{2\beta\phi}},
\label{eq:derived_omega}
\end{equation}
not a constant $K_0$.  The benchmark $K(\phi)=K_0$ arises only as
the leading local approximation of $\omega(\phi)$ around $\phi=0$.

\paragraph{Derived potential.}
The branch potential maps directly to the macroscopic potential via:
\begin{equation}
\boxed{U(\phi) = W(u_\infty\,e^{\beta\phi}).}
\label{eq:derived_potential}
\end{equation}
Near the screened branch $\phi=0$, expanding $W(u)$ around $u_\infty$:
\begin{equation}
U(\phi)
=
W_\infty
+\frac{m_u^2 u_\infty^2}{2}(e^{\beta\phi}-1)^2
+\frac{c_3 u_\infty^3}{3!}(e^{\beta\phi}-1)^3
+\frac{c_4 u_\infty^4}{4!}(e^{\beta\phi}-1)^4+\cdots,
\label{eq:derived_potential_expanded}
\end{equation}
which reduces to the benchmark quadratic $V_0+\frac12 m_\phi^2\phi^2$
at leading order in $\phi$, while encoding the full nonlinear amplitude
dependence at larger $|\phi|$.

\paragraph{Full derived condensate background system.}
The full macroscopic action with derived closure functions is:
\begin{equation}
S_{\rm full}
=
\int d^4x\,\sqrt{-g}\,
\Bigl[
\frac{\bar M_{\rm Pl}^2}{2}\,e^{\beta\phi}R
-\frac{\bar M_{\rm Pl}^2\,\omega_0}{2}\,e^{2\beta\phi}(\nabla\phi)^2
-U(\phi)
\Bigr]
+S_m+S_r.
\label{eq:full_condensate_action}
\end{equation}
For homogeneous FRW with $\phi=\phi(t)$ this gives the
\emph{derived condensate background system}:
\begin{align}
3e^{\beta\phi}H^2
&=
\frac{\rho_m+\rho_r+U(\phi)}{\bar M_{\rm Pl}^2}
+\frac12\omega(\phi)\dot\phi^2
-3H\,\beta e^{\beta\phi}\dot\phi,
\label{eq:condensate_friedmann1}
\\
-2e^{\beta\phi}\dot H
&=
\frac{\rho_m+\tfrac43\rho_r}{\bar M_{\rm Pl}^2}
+\omega(\phi)\dot\phi^2
+\beta^2 e^{\beta\phi}\dot\phi^2+\beta e^{\beta\phi}\ddot\phi
-H\,\beta e^{\beta\phi}\dot\phi,
\label{eq:condensate_friedmann2}
\\
\omega(\phi)\bigl(\ddot\phi+3H\dot\phi+\beta\dot\phi^2\bigr)
&+\frac{U_{,\phi}}{\bar M_{\rm Pl}^2}
-3\beta e^{\beta\phi}(2H^2+\dot H)=0.
\label{eq:condensate_scalar}
\end{align}

In the $N=\ln a$ form with $q\equiv d\phi/dN$, the Friedmann equation
becomes the algebraic relation
\begin{equation}
\boxed{
E^2(N)=
\frac{
\Omega_{m*}e^{-3N}
+\Omega_{r*}e^{-4N}
+\Omega_U(\phi)
}{
e^{\beta\phi}(1+\beta q)
-\tfrac{\omega_0}{6}e^{2\beta\phi}q^2
}},
\label{eq:condensate_E2_N}
\end{equation}
where $\Omega_U(\phi)\equiv U(\phi)/(3\bar M_{\rm Pl}^2 H_*^2)$.
The benchmark $K(\phi)=K_0$ denominator is the $\phi\to 0$ limit
of this expression.
The scalar equation in $N$-variables is
\begin{equation}
\omega_0 e^{2\beta\phi}
\Bigl[
\phi''+(3+E'/E)\phi'+\beta(\phi')^2
\Bigr]
+
\frac{3\,\Omega_{U,\phi}}{E^2}
-3\beta e^{\beta\phi}(2+E'/E)=0.
\label{eq:condensate_scalar_N}
\end{equation}

The benchmark late-time equations used in the current numerical section
are obtained by replacing the full derived functions
\[
\omega(\phi)=\omega_0 e^{2\beta\phi},
\qquad
U(\phi)=W(u_\infty e^{\beta\phi})
\]
by their local screened-branch truncations around $\phi=0$.

\paragraph{Status.}
Equations~\eqref{eq:condensate_E2_N}--\eqref{eq:condensate_scalar_N}
are structurally derived from the ordered-branch EFT (Level~A/bridge),
with $\omega_0$, $m_u$, and $c_3,c_4$ remaining Level~B coefficients.
The current benchmark closure corresponds to the $\phi\to 0$ local
truncation; the full derived system~\eqref{eq:condensate_E2_N} is
required for a self-consistent high-$z$ background branch
that does not rely on an interim matter+radiation tail.
The benchmark late-time closure introduced below should therefore be read
as the local screened-branch truncation of the derived system above,
rather than as an independently chosen scalar--tensor ans\"{a}tz.

\subsection{Effective action and FRW reduction}

We start from the effective scalar--tensor action
\begin{equation}
S_{\rm eff}
=
\int d^4x\,\sqrt{-g}\,
\left[
\frac12 f(\phi)R
-\frac12 K(\phi)(\partial\phi)^2
-V(\phi)
+\mathcal L_m
\right].
\label{eq:app_late_action}
\end{equation}
The effective gravitational stiffness and Newton coupling are
\begin{equation}
M_{\rm eff}^2(\phi)=f(\phi),
\qquad
G_{\rm eff}(\phi)=\frac{1}{8\pi f(\phi)}.
\label{eq:app_late_geff}
\end{equation}

For a spatially flat FRW metric $ds^2=-dt^2+a^2(t)d\mathbf{x}^2$ and a
homogeneous background $\phi=\phi(t)$, the effective equations are
\begin{equation}
3fH^2
=
\rho_m+\rho_r+\frac12 K\dot\phi^2+V-3H\dot f,
\label{eq:app_late_fried1}
\end{equation}
\begin{equation}
-2f\dot H
=
\rho_m+\tfrac43\rho_r+K\dot\phi^2+\ddot f-H\dot f,
\label{eq:app_late_fried2}
\end{equation}
\begin{equation}
K(\ddot\phi+3H\dot\phi)
+\tfrac12 K_{,\phi}\dot\phi^2
+V_{,\phi}
-\tfrac12 f_{,\phi}R
=0,
\quad
R=6(2H^2+\dot H).
\label{eq:app_late_phi_eq}
\end{equation}

\subsection{Convenient form in $N=\ln a$}

Setting $N\equiv \ln a$ and
\[
q(N)\equiv \frac{d\phi}{dN},
\qquad
\phi(N)=\frac{1}{\beta}\ln\!\frac{u(N)}{u_\infty},
\]
one has $\dot\phi=Hq$ and $\dot f=Hf_{,\phi}q$. Equation~\eqref{eq:app_late_fried1}
then gives the exact algebraic relation
\begin{equation}
H^2
=
\frac{\rho_m+\rho_r+V}
{3f-\frac12Kq^2+3f_{,\phi}q}.
\label{eq:app_late_H2_exact}
\end{equation}

\subsection{Benchmark late-time closure (Level~B)}

Guided by the ordered-branch EFT-to-macroscopic mapping discussed in
Section~\ref{sec:closure_function_mapping}, we specialise the late-time
closure to the following benchmark form.
In particular, the benchmark closure should be understood as a small-$\phi$
truncation of the derived condensate background dynamics recorded in
Section~\ref{sec:late_condensate_dynamics}.
\begin{equation}
f(\phi)=f_0 e^{\beta \phi},
\qquad
K(\phi)=K_0,
\qquad
V(\phi)=V_0+\tfrac12 m_\phi^2 \phi^2,
\label{eq:app_late_closure}
\end{equation}
Here the three choices do not have the same status.
The exponential response factor is structurally motivated by
$u/u_\infty=e^{\beta\phi}$.
By contrast, the constant kinetic coefficient $K(\phi)=K_0$ is the
simplest benchmark truncation of the amplitude-sector stiffness, and
the quadratic potential is the leading local expansion of the effective
branch potential around the screened late-time regime $\phi=0$.
Thus Eq.~\eqref{eq:app_late_closure} should be read as the simplest
benchmark realisation of the structurally anchored closure functions,
not as their unique final ECT form.

\paragraph{Status.}
The existence of a late-time macroscopic background closure is inherited
from the background reduction of the geometric branch.
The explicit benchmark choice~\eqref{eq:app_late_closure} is Level~B:
its exponential response factor is structurally motivated, while its
kinetic and potential terms are the simplest local benchmark
specialisations.

with dimensionless parameters
\begin{equation}
E(N)\equiv \frac{H(N)}{H_*},
\quad
\kappa\equiv \frac{K_0}{f_0},
\quad
\mu^2\equiv \frac{m_\phi^2}{H_*^2},
\quad
\Omega_{V*}\equiv \frac{V_0}{3f_0H_*^2}.
\label{eq:app_late_defs}
\end{equation}
The benchmark closure therefore combines one structurally motivated
ingredient and two simplest local benchmark choices:
\[
f(\phi)\sim e^{\beta\phi} \quad \text{(structural/bridge-level)},\qquad
K(\phi)=K_0,\quad
V(\phi)=V_0+\tfrac12 m_\phi^2\phi^2
\quad \text{(benchmark Level~B truncations)}.
\]
This distinction is essential when interpreting numerical outputs.

Here $H_*$ is an external calibration scale of the late-time closure.
It should not be read as a privileged $\Lambda$CDM-inferred value of
$H_0$, but simply as the normalization used to construct the
dimensionless background variables.

The macroscopic variable used in this late-time closure is
\[
\phi(N)=\frac{1}{\beta}\ln\!\frac{u(N)}{u_\infty},
\]
where $u(N)$ is the ordered-branch condensate amplitude introduced in
Section~\ref{sec:ordered_branch_eft}. In the late-time scalar--tensor
closure all background equations are written in terms of $\phi$, not
directly in terms of the dimensionful amplitude~$u$.

The dimensionless Friedmann equation becomes
\begin{equation}
E^2(N)
=
\frac{
\Omega_{m*}e^{-3N}
+\Omega_{r*}e^{-4N}
+\Omega_{V*}
+\frac{\mu^2}{6}\phi^2
}{
e^{\beta \phi}
-\frac{\kappa}{6}q^2
+\beta e^{\beta \phi}q
}.
\label{eq:app_late_E2}
\end{equation}

\subsection{Admissible late-time closure family beyond the benchmark}
\label{sec:late_closure_family}

The benchmark closure
\eqref{eq:app_late_closure}
is useful because it provides the simplest explicit late-time
specialisation of the ordered-branch macroscopic system.
However, it should not be mistaken for the unique admissible closure.
The ordered-branch EFT-to-macroscopic mapping of
Section~\ref{sec:closure_function_mapping}
already suggests a broader family of late-time closures.

The structural part is the response factor
\[
f(\phi)\propto e^{\beta\phi},
\]
since this is directly tied to the amplitude ratio
$u/u_\infty=e^{\beta\phi}$.
By contrast, the kinetic and potential sectors admit more general
late-time benchmark expansions:
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:app_late_general_K}
  K(\phi)=K_0\Bigl(1+k_1\phi+k_2\phi^2+\cdots\Bigr),
\end{equation}
\begin{equation}
  \label{eq:app_late_general_V}
  V(\phi)=V_0+\frac12 m_\phi^2\phi^2
  +\frac{\lambda_3}{3!}\phi^3
  +\frac{\lambda_4}{4!}\phi^4+\cdots.
\end{equation}

These extensions should be interpreted carefully.

\paragraph{(i) Why they are natural.}
From the ordered-branch EFT perspective, $K(\phi)$ is the macroscopic
image of amplitude-sector stiffness and $V(\phi)$ is the macroscopic
image of the branch potential $W(u_\infty e^{\beta\phi})$.
A constant $K$ and a purely quadratic $V$ are therefore not expected to
be exact in full generality; they are simply the leading local
benchmark truncation around the screened late-time branch.

\paragraph{(ii) Why the benchmark remains useful.}
The benchmark closure
\eqref{eq:app_late_closure}
is the first nontrivial member of the admissible family
\eqref{eq:app_late_general_K}--\eqref{eq:app_late_general_V}.
It isolates the minimal late-time effect of amplitude drift without
introducing a large parameter space too early.

\paragraph{(iii) What future code should generalise.}
Once the fixed-point solver is stabilised for the benchmark closure, the
next natural code-level upgrade is to replace the hard-coded
\[
K(\phi)=K_0,
\qquad
V(\phi)=V_0+\tfrac12 m_\phi^2\phi^2
\]
by parameterisable families such as
\eqref{eq:app_late_general_K}--\eqref{eq:app_late_general_V}.
This would allow the article and code to test whether the benchmark
truncation is quantitatively robust or merely the simplest illustrative
realisation.

\paragraph{Status.}
The existence of an admissible closure family is structural/bridge-level,
because it follows from the ordered-branch EFT origin of the closure
functions.
The explicit coefficients $k_i,\lambda_i$ are Level~B.

This immediately defines one of the central late-time open problems of
ECT: to determine which cosmological outputs are robust under
admissible deformations of the benchmark closure, and which are artefacts
of the simplest truncation.

\subsection{Numerical robustness programme for the admissible closure family}
\label{sec:late_closure_robustness}

The existence of the admissible closure family
\eqref{eq:app_late_general_K}--\eqref{eq:app_late_general_V}
immediately raises a numerical robustness question:
which late-time outputs are stable under admissible deformations of the
benchmark closure, and which are sensitive to the particular truncation
\eqref{eq:app_late_closure}?

At the present stage, the role of the benchmark closure is clear:
it is the first explicit late-time realisation of the ordered-branch
macroscopic system, and therefore the natural starting point for code
development.
But the next stage is equally clear:
the code must compare that benchmark against deformed closures of the
admissible family.

The most relevant observables for such robustness tests are:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item the direct and low-$z$ inferred Hubble shifts;
  \item the present cosmic age $t_0$;
  \item the epoch-dependent age $t_U(z)$;
  \item the galaxy-lifetime grids
  $t_{\rm gal}(z_{\rm obs};z_{\rm form})$;
  \item the distance shift $\Delta D_L/D_L$;
  \item the growth proxy $\mathcal{G}(z)$;
  \item the background critical scale
  $g^\dagger_{\rm bg}(z)$.
\end{enumerate}

A late-time output should therefore be regarded as
\emph{numerically robust} only if it persists, at least qualitatively
and preferably quantitatively, under admissible deformations of
$K(\phi)$ and $V(\phi)$.
Conversely, if a claimed effect disappears under mild admissible
deformation, it should be interpreted only as a property of the
benchmark truncation and not yet as a robust ECT feature.

As an initial numerical check, a mild deformed member of the admissible
family has been compared directly against the benchmark closure.
For example, taking
\[
k_1=0.2,\qquad \lambda_3=0.1,
\qquad k_2=\lambda_4=0,
\]
the code finds only a small change in the late-time Hubble shift:
from approximately $2.90\%$ in the benchmark member to approximately
$2.85\%$ in the deformed run.
The induced changes in $t_0$, $t_U(z)$, and
$t_{\rm gal}(z_{\rm obs};z_{\rm form})$ are correspondingly small at the
current benchmark level.
Thus the first benchmark conclusion appears numerically stable under at
least mild admissible deformations, although a broader scan is still
required before claiming full robustness.

The present derived-parent scan goes beyond the earlier benchmark-only
robustness check: it solves the full linear growth equation on the
derived-parent background and combines this with semi-analytic maturity
factors for collapse and BH-assisted development.
This is the first stage at which the ECT late-time programme can be
tested simultaneously against Hubble-shift, age-budget, and JWST
maturity requirements.

This robustness programme is now directly supported by the code-level
interface and artefact layers introduced in
Appendices~\ref{sec:late_cosmo_interface} and
\ref{sec:late_cosmo_artefacts}.

\subsection{Derived-parent working corridor and JWST object budgets}
\label{sec:late_jwst_object_budgets}

A first systematic scan of the derived-parent condensate background was
performed over
$\omega_0\in\{15,20,25,30,40,50\}$,
$\phi_0\in\{-0.15,-0.12,-0.10,-0.08,-0.05\}$,
with $\beta=0.8$, $\mu=1.5$, $A_2=\mu^2/(6\beta^2)$, $A_3=A_4=0$.
The scan identifies the Hubble-compatible corridor described in the
main text (Section~\ref{sec:derived_parent_jwst_main}) and selects
three representative working points.

For the four JWST anchor cases considered in the main text, the
derived-parent age of the Universe at observation is smaller than in
the reference background by an amount ranging from several tens of Myr
(highest-redshift cases) to a few hundred Myr (lower-redshift quiescent
case at $z=4.9$).
The minimum required maturity speed-up is therefore
\[
\mathcal R_{\rm req}(z_{\rm obs})
=
\frac{t_U^{\rm ref}(z_{\rm obs})}{t_U^{\rm ECT}(z_{\rm obs})}.
\]
In the preferred working points this factor is typically
$\mathcal R_{\rm req}\sim 1.19$--$1.24$.

The two semi-analytic maturity factors used are
\[
\mathcal R_{\rm gal}
=
\frac{D_{\rm ECT}}{D_{\rm ref}}
\frac{t_{\rm ff}^{\rm ref}}{t_{\rm ff}^{\rm ECT}},
\qquad
\mathcal R_{\rm BH}
=
\frac{D_{\rm ECT}}{D_{\rm ref}}
\frac{t_{\rm BH}^{\rm ref}}{t_{\rm BH}^{\rm ECT}},
\]
where free-fall time scales as
$t_{\rm ff}\propto (G_{\rm eff}\rho)^{-1/2}$
and BH growth time as
$t_{\rm BH}\propto G_{\rm eff}^{-1}$ (Salpeter-like estimate).
For the representative working points one finds
\[
\mathcal R_{\rm gal}\approx 1.21\text{--}1.23,
\qquad
\mathcal R_{\rm BH}\approx 1.48\text{--}1.54.
\]
This shows that the current derived-parent ECT corridor is close to
the required level for ordinary galaxy-assembly channels and clearly
above it for BH-assisted maturity channels.
This is a concrete partial result rather than a full resolution.
The detailed numerical logic used to obtain this result is summarised in
Appendix~\ref{sec:derived_parent_pipeline}.

\subsection{Current computational pipeline for the derived-parent JWST analysis}
\label{sec:derived_parent_pipeline}

The present numerical programme for the JWST problem proceeds in five
explicit stages.

\paragraph{(i) Background scan.}
A first scan is performed over the parameter rectangle
\[
\omega_0\in\{15,20,25,30,40,50\},
\qquad
\phi_0\in\{-0.15,-0.12,-0.10,-0.08,-0.05\},
\]
at fixed
\[
\beta=0.8,\qquad
\mu=1.5,\qquad
A_2=\frac{\mu^2}{6\beta^2},\qquad
A_3=A_4=0.
\]
For each point the derived-parent background is solved and the late
inferred Hubble shift, age quantities, and high-redshift background
outputs are recorded.

\paragraph{(ii) Selection of representative working points.}
From the Hubble-compatible corridor, three representative points are
selected:
a balanced point, a Hubble-priority point, and an age-priority point.
These serve as the working points for the next numerical layers.

\paragraph{(iii) Linear growth solution.}
For each representative point, the full linear growth equation
\[
D''+\left(2+\frac{H'}{H}\right)D'
-\frac{3}{2}\Omega_m(z)\frac{G_{\rm eff}(z)}{G_N}D=0
\]
is solved on the already-determined derived-parent background.
This replaces the earlier use of the proxy
$\mathcal G(z)=G_{\rm eff}(z)/H^2(z)$ as the sole indicator of growth.
Its derivation is recorded in Appendix~\ref{sec:ect_linear_growth_derivation}.

\paragraph{(iv) JWST object age budgets.}
The age of the Universe at the redshift of observation is then computed
for a set of representative JWST anchor cases, together with the
available galaxy lifetime between an assumed formation redshift and the
epoch of observation.
The minimum required maturity speed-up is
\[
\mathcal R_{\rm req}(z_{\rm obs})
=
\frac{t_U^{\rm ref}(z_{\rm obs})}{t_U^{\rm ECT}(z_{\rm obs})}.
\]

\paragraph{(v) Semi-analytic maturity accounting.}
Finally, the age-budget deficit is combined with the numerically solved
linear growth and with local acceleration factors.
In the current implementation this is done through
\[
\mathcal R_{\rm gal}
=
\frac{D_{\rm ECT}}{D_{\rm ref}}
\frac{t_{\rm ff}^{\rm ref}}{t_{\rm ff}^{\rm ECT}},
\qquad
\mathcal R_{\rm BH}
=
\frac{D_{\rm ECT}}{D_{\rm ref}}
\frac{t_{\rm BH}^{\rm ref}}{t_{\rm BH}^{\rm ECT}},
\]
where $t_{\rm ff}\propto (G_{\rm eff}\rho)^{-1/2}$ and
$t_{\rm BH}\propto G_{\rm eff}^{-1}$ are the present semi-analytic
collapse and BH-growth proxies.
Their status and derivation are recorded in
Appendix~\ref{sec:ect_maturity_factors}.
This pipeline does not yet constitute a full morphology-level treatment,
but it already provides a quantitatively structured test of whether one
and the same ECT parameter corridor can simultaneously address the
Hubble shift, the cosmic-age layer, and the observed maturity of JWST
systems.

\paragraph{Status.}
The present pipeline reaches the level of a solved background, solved
linear growth, and semi-analytic maturity accounting.
It does not yet include full morphology-level calculations, hydrodynamic
structure formation, or direct SMBH--host coevolution simulations.
In the present code implementation this pipeline is represented by
machine-readable outputs for the derived-parent scan, the selected
working points, the JWST object age budgets, the solved linear-growth
table, and the combined maturity budgets.

\subsection{Derivation of the linear growth equation on the ECT background}
\label{sec:ect_linear_growth_derivation}

The linear-growth layer used in the present JWST analysis is not
introduced independently of the background dynamics.
Rather, it is the first perturbative extension of the derived-parent ECT
background in the pressureless subhorizon regime.

\paragraph{Assumptions.}
We work in the matter-dominated perturbation sector, neglect pressure and
anisotropic stress, and restrict attention to subhorizon scales for
which the dominant effect of the modified background is captured by the
time-dependent effective gravitational coupling
\[
G_{\rm eff}(z)=G_N e^{-\beta\phi(z)}.
\]
At this level the ECT modification enters primarily through:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item the modified background expansion $H(z)$;
  \item the modified source strength $G_{\rm eff}(z)$.
\end{enumerate}

\paragraph{Perturbation equations.}
For the matter overdensity $\delta=\delta\rho_m/\rho_m$, the continuity
and Euler equations combine with the modified Poisson equation to give
\[
\ddot{\delta}+2H\dot{\delta}
=
4\pi G_{\rm eff}(z)\rho_m\,\delta.
\]
Using
\[
\rho_m(z)=3\bar M_{\rm Pl}^2 H^2(z)\,\Omega_m(z),
\]
this becomes
\[
\ddot{\delta}+2H\dot{\delta}
=
\frac{3}{2}H^2(z)\,\Omega_m(z)\frac{G_{\rm eff}(z)}{G_N}\,\delta.
\]

\paragraph{Conversion to $N=\ln a$.}
Writing
\[
D(N)\equiv \delta(N),
\qquad
{}' \equiv \frac{d}{dN},
\]
one has
\[
\dot{\delta}=H D',
\qquad
\ddot{\delta}=H^2 D''+\dot H\,D'.
\]
Dividing the perturbation equation by $H^2$ and using
$\dot H/H^2=H'/H$ gives
\begin{equation}
D''+\left(2+\frac{H'}{H}\right)D'
-\frac{3}{2}\Omega_m(z)\frac{G_{\rm eff}(z)}{G_N}D=0.
\label{eq:ect_linear_growth}
\end{equation}
For numerical work,
\[
\Omega_m(z)=\frac{\Omega_{m*}(1+z)^3}{E^2(z)},
\qquad
E(z)\equiv \frac{H(z)}{H_*}.
\]

\paragraph{Status.}
Equation~\eqref{eq:ect_linear_growth} is a derived perturbative layer on
top of the already solved ECT background.
Its validity is limited to the linear, pressureless, subhorizon regime.
It should therefore be interpreted as the first non-background test of
structure development in ECT, not as a full morphology-level treatment.

\subsection{Semi-analytic maturity factors for collapse and BH-assisted development}
\label{sec:ect_maturity_factors}

The present JWST analysis does not yet attempt a full morphology-level
simulation of galaxy or SMBH formation.
Instead, it supplements the solved background and solved linear growth
with two semi-analytic local development factors.

\paragraph{Free-fall / collapse factor.}
For a self-gravitating region of characteristic density $\rho$, the
free-fall timescale scales as
\[
t_{\rm ff}\sim (G_{\rm eff}\rho)^{-1/2}.
\]
Hence the relative collapse acceleration between the ECT background and
the reference background is estimated through
\[
\frac{t_{\rm ff}^{\rm ref}}{t_{\rm ff}^{\rm ECT}}
=
\sqrt{\frac{G_{\rm eff}^{\rm ECT}}{G_N}}
=
e^{-\beta\phi(z)/2}.
\]

\paragraph{BH-growth-like factor.}
For BH-assisted maturity channels, the present implementation uses a
Salpeter-like local estimate in which the characteristic growth time is
taken to scale as $t_{\rm BH}\propto G_{\rm eff}^{-1}$, giving
\[
\frac{t_{\rm BH}^{\rm ref}}{t_{\rm BH}^{\rm ECT}}
=
\frac{G_{\rm eff}^{\rm ECT}}{G_N}
=
e^{-\beta\phi(z)}.
\]

\paragraph{Combined maturity factors.}
The current article defines
\begin{equation}
\mathcal R_{\rm gal}
=
\frac{D_{\rm ECT}}{D_{\rm ref}}
\frac{t_{\rm ff}^{\rm ref}}{t_{\rm ff}^{\rm ECT}},
\qquad
\mathcal R_{\rm BH}
=
\frac{D_{\rm ECT}}{D_{\rm ref}}
\frac{t_{\rm BH}^{\rm ref}}{t_{\rm BH}^{\rm ECT}}.
\label{eq:ect_maturity_factors}
\end{equation}

\paragraph{Status.}
These factors are not a substitute for full structure-formation
simulations.
They are controlled first-order development proxies whose purpose is to
test whether the same ECT parameter corridor that improves the Hubble
shift can also plausibly compensate the tighter high-redshift age
budget.
These factors should be read as semi-analytic development budgets rather
than direct morphology-level predictions.
In particular, the distinction between ordinary galaxy-assembly channels
and BH-assisted maturity channels should be understood at the level of
the present semi-analytic accounting, not as a full morphological
classification of observed JWST systems.

\subsection{Slow-drift regime and present-epoch Hubble shift}

In the slow-drift branch ($|q|\ll 1$, $\kappa q^2\ll 1$,
$|\beta q|\ll 1$), eq.~\eqref{eq:app_late_E2} simplifies to
$H^2\approx (\rho+V)/(3f)$.
Within the benchmark closure \eqref{eq:app_late_closure}, this yields a
transparent analytic estimate for the present-epoch Hubble shift.
Comparing with the screened reference branch of the same closure,
defined by $\phi=0$ and therefore constant $f_0,V_0$:
\begin{equation}
\frac{\Delta H_0}{H_0}
\approx
\frac{\mu^2\phi_0^2}{12}
-\frac12\beta \phi_0.
\label{eq:app_late_deltaH}
\end{equation}
The two terms represent vacuum drift (positive) and effective
Planck-mass drift (sign determined by $\beta \phi_0$).

The slow-drift scalar balance gives
\begin{equation}
q
\approx
\frac{\beta e^{\beta \phi}}{\kappa}
\left(2+\frac{E'}{E}\right)
-
\frac{\mu^2}{3\kappa}\frac{\phi}{E^2},
\label{eq:app_late_q}
\end{equation}
showing that the late drift of $\phi$ is driven by the curvature
coupling $f_{,\phi}R$ balanced against the restoring force $V_{,\phi}$
and the kinetic stiffness $\kappa$.

\subsection{Distances, age, and JWST-related background effects}

Once $E(z)$ is solved, one obtains
\begin{align}
D_L(z)&=(1+z)c_*\int_0^z \frac{dz'}{H(z')},
\quad
D_A(z)=\frac{c_*}{1+z}\int_0^z \frac{dz'}{H(z')},
\label{eq:app_late_dist}\\
t_{\rm lookback}(z)&=\int_0^z \frac{dz'}{(1+z')H(z')},
\quad
t_0=\int_0^\infty \frac{dz}{(1+z)H(z)},
\label{eq:app_late_time}\\
t_U(z)&=\int_z^\infty \frac{dz'}{(1+z')H(z')},
\quad
t_{\rm gal}(z_{\rm obs};z_{\rm form})
=
t_U(z_{\rm obs})-t_U(z_{\rm form}).
\label{eq:app_late_agegal}
\end{align}
These background quantities control the JWST background layer.
The same condensate-amplitude drift that shifts the late inferred
$H_0$ also changes inferred luminosity distances, the age of the
Universe at the epoch of observation,
\[
t_U(z),
\]
and therefore the available formation time of an observed galaxy,
\[
t_{\rm gal}(z_{\rm obs};z_{\rm form}).
\]
This makes the Hubble shift, cosmic age, and JWST-related early-structure
inference parts of a single coupled background problem in the late-time
ECT closure.

% ============================================================
% APPENDIX: Numerical algorithm
% ============================================================
\section{Numerical algorithm for late-time ECT cosmology: Hubble tension, age, and JWST background}
\label{app:late_cosmo_algorithm}

\subsubsection*{Two algorithmic levels: parent system and benchmark solver}

The theoretical structure discussed above naturally defines two distinct
algorithmic levels.

\paragraph{(i) Parent derived system.}
The full condensate background dynamics is governed by
Eqs.~\eqref{eq:condensate_friedmann1}--\eqref{eq:condensate_scalar_N},
with
\[
\omega(\phi)=\omega_0 e^{2\beta\phi},
\qquad
U(\phi)=W(u_\infty e^{\beta\phi}).
\]
This is the system that should ultimately be solved for a fully
self-consistent ECT treatment of the high-redshift branch.

\paragraph{(ii) Current benchmark solver.}
The present code implementation instead solves the local late-time
screened-branch truncation
\eqref{eq:app_late_closure}.
This benchmark solver is therefore a valid numerical layer of the
current article, but it should not yet be confused with the final solver
for the full derived condensate dynamics.

\paragraph{Algorithm flow.}
At the algorithmic level, the logic is now:
\[
\text{derived condensate background system}
\;\longrightarrow\;
\text{benchmark truncation and derived-parent implementation}
\;\longrightarrow\;
\text{linear-growth and maturity-budget layers}.
\]
The benchmark truncation remains the control mode of the numerical
programme, but the present implementation also includes a derived-parent
background solver together with a solved linear-growth layer and the
semi-analytic maturity accounting used in the current JWST analysis.
The parent system itself is recorded in
Section~\ref{sec:late_condensate_dynamics}.

\subsubsection*{Algorithmic form of the derived condensate solver}
\label{sec:late_derived_solver_algorithm}

For the full derived condensate background system,
the natural numerical unknowns are
\[
E(N)=\frac{H(N)}{H_*},
\qquad
\phi(N),
\qquad
q(N)\equiv \frac{d\phi}{dN}.
\]
The parent fixed-point structure is then defined by the coupled pair:

\paragraph{(i) Algebraic background update.}
Given a current iterate $\phi^{(k)}(N)$ and $q^{(k)}(N)$,
compute
\[
E^{2\,(k)}(N)
=
\frac{
\Omega_{m*}e^{-3N}
+\Omega_{r*}e^{-4N}
+\Omega_U(\phi^{(k)})
}{
e^{\beta\phi^{(k)}}(1+\beta q^{(k)})
-\tfrac{\omega_0}{6}e^{2\beta\phi^{(k)}}(q^{(k)})^2
}.
\]

\paragraph{(ii) Differential scalar update.}
Using
\[
\left(\frac{E'}{E}\right)^{(k)}
=
\frac12\frac{d\ln(E^{2\,(k)})}{dN},
\]
update $\phi^{(k+1)}(N)$ from the scalar equation
\eqref{eq:condensate_scalar_N},
or equivalently update $q^{(k+1)}$ and then integrate
\[
\frac{d\phi^{(k+1)}}{dN}=q^{(k+1)}(N)
\]
with the chosen present-epoch boundary data $(\phi_0, q_0)$.

\paragraph{(iii) Iterate to convergence.}
Repeat steps (i) and (ii) until the background fields
$E(N)$, $\phi(N)$, and $q(N)$ converge under a chosen tolerance.

\paragraph{Benchmark relation.}
The current benchmark solver follows exactly the same fixed-point logic,
but with the local benchmark replacements
\[
\omega(\phi)\to K_0,
\qquad
U(\phi)\to V_0+\frac12 m_\phi^2\phi^2.
\]
Thus the benchmark code is not conceptually separate from the full
derived solver; it is its simplest local truncation.

\subsection{Self-consistent fixed-point solver design}

The numerical background solver architecture described in this subsection
originates from the benchmark late-time truncation of the ECT
macroscopic scalar--tensor background closure.
Its full parent algorithm is given immediately above.
The benchmark solver remains the control numerical layer of the article,
but the current code also implements a derived-parent background mode,
a solved linear-growth layer, and the semi-analytic maturity accounting
used for the JWST discussion.
It evolves the macroscopic amplitude variable $\phi(N)=\frac{1}{\beta}\ln(u/u_\infty)$
and computes the corresponding background expansion history $E(N)=H/H_*$.

Rather than importing a $\Lambda$CDM background as physical input, the
solver iterates the ECT system on itself:

\begin{enumerate}
  \item choose the present-epoch data $(\phi_0,q_0)$ and a calibration
  scale $H_*$;
  \item start from a zeroth approximation $q^{(0)}(N)$ (for example the
  slow-drift seed or $q^{(0)}=0$);
  \item compute $E^{(k)}(N)$ algebraically from
  Eq.~\eqref{eq:app_late_E2};
  \item obtain $(E'/E)^{(k)}$ from the current iterate
  \[
    \left(\frac{E'}{E}\right)^{(k)}
    = \frac{1}{2}\frac{d\ln[(E^{(k)})^2]}{dN};
  \]
  \item update $q^{(k+1)}(N)$ from the scalar slow-drift balance
  Eq.~\eqref{eq:app_late_q};
  \item integrate
  \[
    \frac{d\phi^{(k+1)}}{dN}=q^{(k+1)}(N)
  \]
  with the chosen present-epoch boundary condition;
  \item recompute $E^{(k+1)}(N)$ and iterate until convergence.
\end{enumerate}

In this way the ECT background is determined by the closure equations
themselves.
A comparison to $\Lambda$CDM may still be shown at the level of plots or
diagnostics, but not used as the defining physical background of the
solver.

The earlier $\Lambda$CDM-seeded quasi-static implementation should
therefore be regarded as an interim benchmark algorithm, not as the
intended final numerical realisation of the late-time ECT background.

Accordingly, future code revisions should be judged against the
structural roadmap above: improvement of the fixed-point background
solver, replacement of interim high-$z$ completion, and possible
implementation of admissible generalisations of the benchmark functions
$K(\phi)$ and $V(\phi)$ are the three main directions of progress.
A fourth direction is numerical robustness testing:
benchmark outputs should be compared systematically against admissible
closure deformations rather than reported only for the benchmark member.

\subsection{Implementation roadmap from benchmark closure to solver}
\label{sec:late_cosmo_roadmap}

For practical code development it is useful to separate clearly which
parts of the late-time cosmology apparatus are already fixed by the
present article and which parts remain provisional benchmark choices.

\paragraph{Layer 1: structural input already fixed by the theory.}
The following ingredients are already fixed at the structural or
bridge level:
\begin{itemize}
  \item the ordered-branch origin of the macroscopic amplitude variable
  $\phi=\frac{1}{\beta}\ln(u/u_\infty)$, i.e.\ $u/u_\infty=e^{\beta\phi}$;
  \item the amplitude-dominated macroscopic reduction;
  \item the existence of a coupled scalar--tensor background system for
  $(H,\phi)$ under isotropic FRW reduction;
  \item the standard FRW integral outputs
  $D_L(z)$, $t_{\rm lookback}(z)$, $t_U(z)$, and
  $t_{\rm gal}(z_{\rm obs};z_{\rm form})$ once the background is known.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Layer 2: benchmark late-time closure.}
The present code-facing closure
\[
f(\phi)=f_0 e^{\beta\phi},
\qquad
K(\phi)=K_0,
\qquad
V(\phi)=V_0+\tfrac12 m_\phi^2\phi^2
\]
should be understood as the first benchmark specialisation of the
structurally anchored closure functions.
Only the exponential response factor is directly tied to the amplitude
ratio $u/u_\infty=e^{\beta\phi}$.
The constant kinetic coefficient and quadratic potential remain the
simplest local Level~B benchmark truncations.
A broader admissible family of late-time closures is described in
Section~\ref{sec:late_closure_family}.

\paragraph{Layer 3: interim numerical completion.}
The present numerical age extension above $z_{\rm match}=10$ is still an
interim matter+radiation completion.
Therefore outputs that depend strongly on the high-redshift continuation
must be marked as interim benchmark outputs until the full
self-consistent ECT high-$z$ background branch is implemented.
Beyond the present benchmark implementation, the central next step is to
replace the benchmark truncation by a solver built directly on the
derived condensate background system
(Section~\ref{sec:late_condensate_dynamics}),
especially for the high-redshift branch relevant to cosmic-age and JWST applications.
The parent fixed-point structure of such a solver is already written
explicitly in Section~\ref{sec:late_derived_solver_algorithm}.

\paragraph{Layer 4: target solver outputs.}
A fully synchronised article--code pipeline should produce, at minimum:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item the converged background solution
  $H(z)$ and $\phi(z)$;
  \item the direct and low-$z$ inferred Hubble shifts;
  \item $D_L(z)$ and $t_{\rm lookback}(z)$ on the observational grid;
  \item the epoch-dependent cosmic age $t_U(z)$;
  \item galaxy-lifetime grids
  $t_{\rm gal}(z_{\rm obs};z_{\rm form})$
  for JWST-relevant $(z_{\rm obs},z_{\rm form})$ pairs;
  \item convergence diagnostics for the fixed-point iteration.
\end{enumerate}

These outputs should be emitted in standard tabular form so that the
article figures and benchmark tables can be regenerated directly from
solver products rather than from manually transferred values.

\paragraph{Purpose of this roadmap.}
This separation prevents the code from being interpreted as if every
implemented function were already an equally fundamental output of ECT.
It also makes clear which future code upgrades correspond to genuine
theoretical progress (improved closure functions and high-$z$ branch
completion) and which correspond only to numerical refinement.
The explicit article--solver contract is stated in
Section~\ref{sec:late_cosmo_interface}.

\subsection{Article--solver interface contract}
\label{sec:late_cosmo_interface}

To keep the late-time code and the theoretical text synchronised, it is
useful to state explicitly what the numerical solver is expected to take
as input, what it must return as output, and what constitutes a
successful implementation of the benchmark closure.

\paragraph{Required solver inputs.}
At minimum, the benchmark late-time solver should accept:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item the closure parameters
  \[
  \beta,\qquad K_0,\qquad m_\phi,\qquad V_0,
  \]
  or equivalently the dimensionless combinations used in
  Eq.~\eqref{eq:app_late_defs};
  \item the calibration scale $H_*$;
  \item the present-epoch background data
  \[
  \phi_0,\qquad q_0=\left.\frac{d\phi}{dN}\right|_{N=0};
  \]
  \item the matter and radiation background normalisations
  \[
  \Omega_{m*},\qquad \Omega_{r*},\qquad \Omega_{V*};
  \]
  \item the redshift grid on which the solution is to be reported;
  \item if used, the interim high-$z$ completion settings
  (such as $z_{\rm match}$), which must be recorded explicitly as
  numerical rather than structural inputs.
\end{enumerate}

\paragraph{Required solver outputs.}
A benchmark implementation consistent with the present article should
return, at minimum:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item the converged background fields
  \[
  H(z),\qquad \phi(z),\qquad G_{\rm eff}(z);
  \]
  \item the direct Hubble shift and low-$z$ inferred Hubble shift;
  \item the standard distance/time observables
  \[
  D_L(z),\qquad t_{\rm lookback}(z),\qquad t_U(z);
  \]
  \item JWST-oriented galaxy lifetime grids
  \[
  t_{\rm gal}(z_{\rm obs};z_{\rm form});
  \]
  \item the growth proxy
  \[
  \mathcal{G}(z)=G_{\rm eff}(z)/H^2(z);
  \]
  \item convergence diagnostics of the fixed-point iteration.
\end{enumerate}

\paragraph{Required validation checks.}
A solver run should not be considered article-consistent unless it also
reports:
\begin{enumerate}
  \item convergence of the fixed-point iteration from more than one
  initial seed;
  \item sensitivity of the solution to numerical resolution and redshift
  grid choice;
  \item explicit dependence of age-related quantities on the interim
  high-$z$ completion, if such a completion is still used;
  \item sensitivity of the benchmark outputs to admissible deformations
  of $K(\phi)$ and $V(\phi)$ once those are implemented;
  \item where implemented, direct comparison of benchmark and deformed
  admissible-closure runs, with explicit reporting of the induced shifts
  in $H(z)$, $\phi(z)$, $t_U(z)$, and the benchmark summary observables.
\end{enumerate}

\paragraph{Interpretive rule.}
This contract is meant to prevent confusion between three different
levels:
the structural background system of the theory,
the benchmark closure chosen for the present article,
and the current numerical implementation.
A code result should only be interpreted as an ECT prediction to the
extent that all three levels are kept conceptually distinct and the
required numerical artefacts are exported in reproducible form.

\subsection{Expected numerical artefacts for article synchronisation}
\label{sec:late_cosmo_artefacts}

To keep the late-time figures, tables, and numerical claims reproducible,
the solver should not only converge internally but also emit a standard
set of machine-readable artefacts.

\paragraph{Core background table.}
For each benchmark run, the code should produce a background table on a
common redshift grid containing at least
\[
z,\qquad H(z),\qquad E(z),\qquad \phi(z),\qquad G_{\rm eff}(z).
\]
This table is the primary source for all later derived observables.

\paragraph{Distance--time table.}
From the converged background, the code should produce a second table
containing
\[
z,\qquad D_L(z),\qquad t_{\rm lookback}(z),\qquad t_U(z).
\]
This table is the direct numerical support for the late-time cosmology
plots and age statements in the main text.

\paragraph{JWST age grid.}
For selected observation and formation redshifts, the code should output
a grid of galaxy-lifetime estimates
\[
(z_{\rm obs},\,z_{\rm form},\,t_{\rm gal}),
\]
with at least the benchmark combinations discussed in the paper.
This artefact is required if the JWST discussion is to remain directly
traceable to code outputs rather than to hand-entered numbers.

\paragraph{Benchmark summary table.}
Each benchmark run should emit a compact summary table containing, at
minimum,
\[
\Delta H_0/H_0,\quad
H_0^{\rm late},\quad
t_0,\quad
\Delta t_0/t_0,\quad
\Delta D_L/D_L,\quad
\mathcal{G}(z),\quad
g^\dagger_{\rm bg}(z)/g^\dagger_{\rm bg}(0),
\]
together with explicit metadata recording which closure functions and
which high-$z$ completion settings were used.

\paragraph{Benchmark--deformed comparison table.}
Whenever admissible closure deformations are enabled, the code should
also emit a comparison artefact showing the difference between the
deformed run and the benchmark member on the same redshift grid.
At minimum this artefact should report
\[
\Delta\phi(z),\qquad
\Delta E(z),\qquad
\Delta t_U(z),
\]
and the corresponding changes in the benchmark summary outputs.
In the current code implementation this role is played by a benchmark--
deformed comparison table exported on the common redshift grid.

\paragraph{Derived-parent scan and JWST maturity tables.}
For the derived-parent programme the code should also emit machine-readable
tables for the first $(\omega_0,\phi_0)$ scan, the selected working points,
the JWST object age budgets, and the combined maturity budgets
($\mathcal R_{\rm gal}$, $\mathcal R_{\rm BH}$, $\mathcal R_{\rm req}$).
These artefacts support direct comparison between the Hubble-compatible
parameter corridor and the astrophysical maturity requirements of the
observed high-redshift systems.
Together these artefacts define the machine-readable counterpart of the
pipeline described in Appendix~\ref{sec:derived_parent_pipeline}.

\paragraph{Derived-parent comparison and anchor-case figures.}
The current article also requires visual artefacts for:
(i)~benchmark-versus-derived-parent background comparison
(Fig.~\ref{fig:ect_derived_parent_comparison}),
(ii)~the derived-parent parameter scan with labelled working points
(Fig.~\ref{fig:ect_condensate_param_scan}),
(iii)~the JWST anchor-case budget comparison
(Fig.~\ref{fig:ect_jwst_anchor_budget}),
and (iv)~the schematic full evolution of the condensate sectors and
observable Universe
(Fig.~\ref{fig:ect_full_condensate_universe_evolution}).
These figures form the article-facing visual presentation layer of the
current numerical and conceptual results.
The full condensate-and-Universe evolution schematic is a conceptual
article-facing figure rather than a direct numerical solver output;
its purpose is to summarise the multi-sector ECT interpretation
framing the present late-time derived-parent calculations.

\paragraph{Convergence and sensitivity report.}
The solver should also emit a small diagnostic report containing:
\begin{itemize}
  \item iteration count and convergence tolerance;
  \item dependence on initial seed;
  \item dependence on redshift-grid resolution;
  \item dependence on interim $z_{\rm match}$ if high-$z$ completion is used.
\end{itemize}

\paragraph{Purpose.}
These artefacts define the practical synchronisation layer between the
article and the code repository.
Without them, even a numerically converged solver is not yet fully
article-reproducible.

\subsection{Benchmark parameter window}

Within the benchmark closure \eqref{eq:app_late_closure}, the natural
slow-drift parameter window that gives a few-percent Hubble shift is
\begin{equation}
\beta\sim 0.7\text{--}1.0,\quad
\phi_0\sim -(0.08\text{--}0.12),\quad
\mu\sim 1.0\text{--}1.5,\quad
\kappa\sim 10\text{--}20.
\label{eq:app_algo_window}
\end{equation}
The window above refers specifically to the benchmark truncation.
One of the next tasks is to determine how stable it remains under the
admissible closure deformations described in
Section~\ref{sec:late_closure_family}.

For Benchmark~B ($\beta=0.8$, $\mu=1.5$, $\kappa=15$, $\phi_0=-0.12$)
the analytic estimate~\eqref{eq:app_late_deltaH} gives
$\Delta H_0/H_0\approx 5.1\%$.
Interim numerical runs of the late-time benchmark closure typically give
a somewhat smaller shift, of order a few percent, once the $q$-dependent
corrections to $E^2$ are included.
A definitive numerical value should be quoted only from the converged
self-consistent fixed-point solver described above.
The difference between the analytic estimate and interim benchmark runs
reflects both the sensitivity of the Level~B closure to the detailed
background iteration procedure and the fact that the benchmark closure
itself is only the simplest late-time truncation of the ordered-branch
macroscopic system.

The analytic parameter-window scan (Fig.~\ref{fig:h0_scan_main} in the
main text) shows that the benchmark points lie inside a finite parameter
region producing a few-percent late-time Hubble shift, confirming that
the benchmark is representative rather than finely tuned.

What this benchmark window establishes is therefore modest but useful:
a few-percent late-time Hubble shift is readily obtainable within the
first amplitude-dominated closure without extreme tuning.
What it does not yet establish is uniqueness.
Different admissible Level~B benchmark choices for $K(\phi)$ and
$V(\phi)$ may lead to quantitatively different background histories even
while preserving the same structural ordered-branch logic.

\subsection{Derived outputs}

For age estimates, the present numerical implementation extends the
integration to sufficiently high redshift by attaching an interim
matter+radiation-dominated completion above a matching scale
$z_{\rm match}=10$.
This should be read purely as a temporary numerical completion of the
late-time closure, not as part of the defining ECT background dynamics.
A fully self-consistent ECT treatment of the high-$z$ branch remains to
be implemented in the next stage of the programme.

Accordingly, outputs that depend sensitively on the $z>z_{\rm match}$
extension---especially $t_0$, $t_U(z)$ for high redshift, and
$t_{\rm gal}(z_{\rm obs};z_{\rm form})$ with large $z_{\rm form}$---must
still be interpreted as interim Level~B numerical estimates rather than
as final ECT predictions.

From the solved macroscopic background branch the code computes:
\begin{enumerate}
\item the direct late-time Hubble shift $\Delta H_0/H_0 = E(0)-1$;
\item a ladder-style low-$z$ inferred $H_0$ from the $D_L$ slope;
\item the age correction $\Delta t_0/t_0$;
\item lookback-time and luminosity-distance shifts at $z=1,2,5,8,10,12$;
\item the epoch-dependent cosmic age $t_U(z)$ on the same redshift grid;
\item JWST-relevant galaxy-lifetime estimates
$t_{\rm gal}(z_{\rm obs};z_{\rm form})$ for selected
$(z_{\rm obs},z_{\rm form})$ pairs;
\item a simple growth proxy
$\mathcal{G}(z)\equiv G_{\rm eff}(z)/H^2(z)$
as a first indicator of early structure formation enhancement;
\item convergence diagnostics of the fixed-point background iteration.
\end{enumerate}
All outputs are collected in a summary CSV file and a four-panel figure.
The code is available at the repository listed in the Data and Code
Availability section.

In particular, the JWST discussion should no longer rely only on
present-age or lookback summaries.
The solver should explicitly expose the two distinct quantities
\[
t_U(z)
\quad\text{and}\quad
t_{\rm gal}(z_{\rm obs};z_{\rm form}),
\]
since they answer different physical questions: the age of the Universe
at the epoch of observation, and the available lifetime of a galaxy
between formation and observation.


\clearpage
\section*{Data and Code Availability}
\addcontentsline{toc}{part}{Data and Code Availability}

Galaxy rotation-curve data are drawn from the public SPARC database \cite{Lelli2016}
(\url{http://astroweb.cwru.edu/SPARC/}).
All numerical scripts used to produce the figures and calculations in this paper
are publicly available at:

\begin{center}
  \url{https://github.com/chufelo/ECT-preprint-code}
\end{center}

\noindent
For the late-time cosmology block, the currently released code should be
read as the benchmark implementation corresponding to the Level~B
closure and interim high-$z$ numerical completion described in
Appendix~\ref{app:late_cosmo_algorithm}.
The expected article-level numerical inputs, outputs, and validation
checks are summarised in
Appendix~\ref{sec:late_cosmo_interface}.
The expected machine-readable artefacts of such a benchmark
implementation are listed in
Appendix~\ref{sec:late_cosmo_artefacts}.
The same benchmark implementation is also intended to support the
robustness programme under admissible late-time closure deformations
described in Appendix~\ref{sec:late_closure_robustness}.
The current repository version already supports direct benchmark--deformed
comparisons within the admissible late-time closure family, so that the
first numerical robustness checks can be reproduced directly from code
outputs.

\noindent
The repository contains: the full-SPARC rotation-curve fitting pipeline
for the new ECT galactic closure (including fixed/free $M/L$ modes,
model-comparison diagnostics against MOND and $\Lambda$CDM/NFW, and
sample-level summary tables for all 165 galaxies),
cosmological calculations (Hubble tension, inflation $n_s$, JWST halo-abundance
enhancement), matching and consistency calculations for the gravitational and
coherent action scales (including the linearised $G_N$ matching logic collected
in Appendix~\ref{app:GN_derivation}), fifth-force bounds, baryogenesis estimates,
and the effective dimensionality calculation (Fig.~\ref{fig:dimensionality}).
Scripts are self-contained Python 3 files requiring only \texttt{numpy},
\texttt{matplotlib}, and \texttt{scipy}.
